issues.title: Core -01: name client-instance-jwt as a concrete actor_token_type in Delegation
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the instance/actor draft-family gap review. The core's Delegation section requires the delegate be identified by an actor_token (with its actor_token_type) or its own client authentication, with the AS asserting the actor itself, but leaves the token type abstract. The client-instance-assertion profile defines urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:client-instance-jwt as exactly this artifact (attested instance identity presented as actor_token at Token Exchange). Wire it as an informative example in the Delegation section. Deferred from working-tree edits because the core is published as -00 on the datatracker; target -01. Companion wiring landed in PR #108.

issues.title: Core -01: replace not-example.net with a reserved example domain
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From external review (idnits): the recipient_domain subset example uses not-example.net, which is not an RFC 2606 reserved name. Replace with example.net (reserved), which is equally not-within example.com for the subset illustration: 'mail.example.com is within example.com and example.net is not.' Core is published as -00 on the datatracker; apply in -01. Location: the Common Constraints recipient_domain subset-rule example (near line 1179 of draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission.md).

issues.title: Core -01: de-duplicate twice-stated normative rules
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the publication review's deferred items and the family critique. Three rules are stated normatively in two places within the core, and every edit risks divergence between the copies: (1) the authority_hash recomputation rule appears in the Resource Server Enforcement bullet and again in Security Considerations' Consent Binding, each with its own MUSTs; (2) the downgrade-by-omission rule appears in an rs-enforcement bullet and again in its own Security Considerations section including the invalid_token MUST; (3) the max_derivations cross-domain counting semantics appear in the controls member definition and again in Issuance Gating. Fix pattern: keep one authoritative home per rule (the section that owns the mechanism) and reduce the other occurrence to a pointer, preserving every normative clause once. Target -01.

issues.title: Core -01: add a JCS test vector exercising nested controls and delegation ordering
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the publication review's deferred items and the family critique. The core's two test vectors cover both typ values but neither exercises the JCS cases most likely to diverge across implementations: a Mission Intent whose controls object carries nested members (acr plus max_derivations), and an Authority Set entry carrying delegation.allowed_delegates, a nested array of matcher objects where the canonicalization rules' array-order preservation interacts with object-member sorting. Add one computable vector (canonical bytes plus digest, generated and verified like the existing pair) covering both shapes. Target -01.

issues.title: Core -01: promote the exclusivity (separation-of-duty) control once stable
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: The metering companion now defines the exclusive controls member: consented exclusivity groups over Authority Set selectors, PDP-latched first-exercise-wins, turning the quarantine pattern into enforceable consented structure (an Approver approves read-sensitive-store or communicate-externally, never both under one Mission). It is deliberately homed in the experimental companion while the semantics bake, per the maturity dependency rule. When stable, promote the member into the core's controls vocabulary (and consider a Common Constraints treatment for per-entry exclusivity references) so it rides the standard consent rendering rules rather than the companion seam. Target: post-experimentation, -01 or later.

issues.title: Core -01: RECOMMEND narrowing-mode derivation as the default
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the solution-architecture critique. Derivation from goal to Authority Set is the family's highest-leverage and least-checkable step: generative derivation is pure AS policy trust, while narrowing mode (proposed_authority + subset rule + deterministic reproducibility) makes every derivation independently verifiable and replayable by an auditor. The core currently presents the modes as peers. Make narrowing RECOMMENDED and generative derivation the documented exception (with its existing disclosure and policy_version accountability rules), in the Authorization Derivation and Mission Intent sections. Target -01.

issues.title: Core -01: class-differentiated token lifetime guidance
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the solution-architecture critique. The core recommends short-lived derived tokens unconditionally because token expiry is the revocation cutoff in an issuance-only deployment. The runtime profile's new Deployment Considerations section states the layer trade explicitly: where runtime enforcement covers the high-consequence classes with an active-freshness source, the PDP is the cutoff and lifetimes MAY extend without losing the kill switch. Mirror that trade in the core's Mission-Bound Access Tokens lifetime guidance (one sentence with a pointer to the runtime profile), so deployments scale issuance load deliberately instead of stretching lifetimes silently. Target -01.

issues.title: Core -01: state the unit of governance in Applicability (action, not content)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the solution-architecture critique. The Mission bounds where an agent may act (resources, actions), how much (constraints, metering), and never what content flows within an authorized action: an approved egress action carries a status update or an exfiltrated payload with equal authority, subject only to bound parameters. The family discloses this at the edges (no information-flow control, taint, volume metering) but the core's Applicability section should state the unit of governance plainly, with the constructive pointer that mediated execution places the natural insertion point for content-level controls (DLP, redaction) at the mediating PEP, now noted in the runtime profile's custody section. Target -01.

issues.title: Core -01: state the anchor's enforcement-vs-audit role and the intent/authority independence
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From the solution critique (finding 2). Two clarity gaps in how the core's headline 'integrity-bound' claim is read:

(a) intent_hash and authority_hash are independent commitments to independent objects; that the derived authority faithfully serves the approved intent is a derivation-policy SHOULD (auditable via policy_version), not a property either hash proves. The core should state that 'the approved task bounds the authority' is a governance assertion, not a cryptographic one.

(b) authority_hash commits the FULL consented Authority Set, but a per-RS token carries a narrowed subset and (per the recompute rule) cannot reconstruct the full set. So at a narrowed-token RS, authority_hash is an audit correlator, not an enforcement input, and the subset relationship it stands for is trust in the AS signature, not a per-token cryptographic subset proof. The core's Mission Claim and Resource Server Enforcement sections should say this plainly so the anchor is not over-read.

The security model now states both as named residuals (companion, immediate); this issue is for the core's own framing in -01. Neither is a mechanism change, only precision about what the anchors deliver.

issues.title: Core -01: state that the subset rule (narrowing guarantee) is type-specific
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the solution critique (finding 3). The subset rule is fully defined only over mission_resource_access and its Common Constraints. Authority in another authorization_details type, including opaque policy-language entries (e.g. Cedar), has no defined subset relation: it is carried as-approved and, per the family, is not narrowed, delegated, or cross-domain projected. The 'authority only narrows' guarantee therefore weakens exactly as a deployment moves expressiveness into policy-language entries. The core's Subset Rule / Other Authorization Details Types sections should name this as an explicit limit so the trade is made knowingly. The security model now states it as a named residual (companion, immediate); this issue is for the core statement in -01.

issues.title: Core -01: sharpen the derivation trust boundary (no portable Intent semantics)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From review feedback (item 8). The core states derivation is local policy and not a portable algorithm, which is correct, but the framing could be sharper so readers do not infer that a Mission Intent has portable semantics. In -01, state explicitly in the Authorization Derivation section: (a) interoperability begins at the committed result (the Authority Set and its anchors), not at the Intent; (b) a Mission Intent has no portable semantics, and audit establishes what was derived (against intent_hash and policy_version), never whether it was the correct reading of the task; (c) a deployment whose partners reason about its derivations SHOULD publish a derivation policy identifier and test fixtures pinning Intent-to-Authority-Set outcomes. The architecture now states this boundary informatively in a new Authority Derivation Boundary section; this issue is the core's own normative framing. Relates to #118 (narrowing-mode RECOMMENDED).

issues.title: Core -01: reserve 'Mission-bound token' for the gated class (token-class taxonomy)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the architecture review. Implementers may read 'token carries a mission claim' as 'Mission-bound authorization.' The conformance section already says a mission claim alone is insufficient. Make the taxonomy explicit in -01: a Mission-referenced token carries a Mission identifier only; a Mission-derived token carries authority derived from an active Mission; a Mission-bound token is Mission-derived AND active-state gated, subset-constrained, and refresh-gated. Reserve the term 'Mission-bound' for the third class throughout the core. The architecture now presents this taxonomy informatively (Token Classes section); this issue is the core's own terminology.

issues.title: Core -01: expand the Common Constraints registry for real agent deployments
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the architecture review. Unknown constraints fail closed (safe) but a small practical registry improves interop. The core defines max_amount, currency, recipient_domain, resource_issued_after/before; the metering companion adds max_calls, max_egress_volume, exclusive (covering max_operations / no_external_egress). Consider seeding, in -01, the remaining high-value constraints reviewers expect: time_window (bounded execution), data_classification (privacy/exfiltration control), allowed_tools (harness/tool restriction), and requires_action_approval as the Common-Constraint form of the runtime action-bound-approval trigger. Keep it minimal but enough to demonstrate cross-vendor interop.

issues.title: -01: retire 'security tiers' vocabulary in the Introduction
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: Lines ~226-234 describe 'the first of two deliberate security tiers... A deployment chooses its tier deliberately.' The family has since settled its claim vocabulary as the Mission Assurance Levels (levels, not tiers), and the same paragraph already says 'the runtime enforcement layer.' In -01, replace tiers/tier with chokepoints/layers and cite the architecture's Mission Assurance Levels informatively, so the core is not read as introducing a competing vocabulary.

issues.title: -01: stale future-work item: substrate-neutral generalization now exists
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: Line ~360 lists as 'Remaining future work, not yet specified... a substrate-neutral generalization of the Mission model across non-OAuth authorization substrates.' The Mission Substrate Requirements document and the AAuth binding now exist. Since the core stands alone, reword rather than cite: 'a substrate-neutral generalization... is out of scope for this document.'

issues.title: -01: max_derivations sizing guidance for steady-state cross-domain use
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: The cross-domain companion counts each cross-domain grant once against the Mission's derivation budget, and grants are capped at 300 seconds, so steady-state partner work consumes at least 12 derivations per hour per partner audience; the core's example max_derivations of 200 exhausts in under a day. Add guidance (no normative change): a deployment sizes max_derivations as a function of grant/lease cadence times audiences times Mission duration rather than a flat count.

issues.title: -01: forward pointer to the Deferred Approval companion
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: The core's lifecycle/out-of-scope text points forward to status, signals, and expansion but never to the deferred approval companion, whose premise is an explicit override of the core's approval-event/code-issuance sequencing. Add a one-line forward reference in the approval-event or out-of-scope section so a core-only reader knows the sequencing is overridable by a companion.

issues.title: -01: promote mission_id token-response parameter to RECOMMENDED
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: The mission_id token-response parameter (~line 1452) is OPTIONAL, but the client 'is not expected to parse the access token'; when the parameter is absent the only path to mission_id is reading the mission claim from the token, which fails for encrypted or opaque-to-client tokens. Promote OPTIONAL to RECOMMENDED in -01.

issues.title: -01 (joint with expansion): grant proof for refresh-token-less Missions
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: The core acknowledges Missions stoppable 'even when no refresh token is held,' while the expansion companion's only predecessor-grant proof is presentation of the refresh token. Either the core names an alternative grant-resolving presentation for refresh-token-less deployments, or expansion states explicitly that expansion eligibility requires a refresh-token-bearing grant. Expansion-side text alone cannot invent a grant artifact the core does not define.

issues.title: -01: note that companions extending the worked example must diverge anchors
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: The core's worked-example Authority Set is the family's canonical anchor (sha-256:l3KvZ4mP...). Companion examples that extend or vary the set while displaying the same anchor have drifted several times (mandate, child-delegation, attenuation; the cross-domain draft shows the correct 'anchors differ' note pattern). Add a short note at the worked example: a companion that extends this example MUST either reproduce the recorded set byte-exactly or state that its anchors differ.

issues.title: -01 design: OPTIONAL expires_at member in the mission claim
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Design question raised in review: should the mission claim carry the Mission's temporal facts?

**Recommendation: add OPTIONAL `expires_at` (RFC 3339, mirroring the record member, spelled identically per the record-fact naming rule); do not add `created_at`.**

Benefits of `expires_at` on tokens: (1) remaining-lifetime signal for downstream planning (token exp conveys nothing about the Mission horizon: a 5-minute token can serve a 3-month Mission); (2) a deterministic ceiling on offline and degraded validation paths, since expiry is an immutable committed fact and safe to cache in a credential, unlike state (relevant to attenuation edge validation and PEPs inside their staleness window); (3) misconfiguration detection: validators can check token exp <= mission.expires_at, which matters under the issuance-grant companion where a consuming AS performs the cap (catches misconfiguration, not compromise, since the token is AS-signed; a Mandate-holding verifier can go further because expiry is committed under intent_hash via intent.expires_at).

Required guardrail text with the member: it is a bounding and audit commitment carrying no liveness; expiry says nothing about revocation, and only `active` permits reliance. (The current design's forcing function, expiry only visible on surfaces that also deliver state, is already unbundled by the Mandate.)

Against `created_at` on tokens: no enforcement use, and it is an approval-instant correlation fingerprint on every credential.

Coupling: if -01 adds this member, the issuance-grant companion's mission object (carried verbatim onto issued tokens) includes it, and that profile's top-level mission_expiry can deprecate into the claim in a later revision.

comments.author: mcguinness
comments.body: Implemented ahead of -01 in the companions (PR #157-adjacent, merged): the issuance-grant profile now defines the member ({#expires-at-member}), carries it inside the grant's mission object (top-level mission_expiry removed before publication), and issued tokens carry it verbatim; the AAuth PS SHOULD mint it; runtime and attenuation define credential-side ceiling consumption with the no-liveness guardrail. Remaining -01 work: canonicalize the member into the core's mission claim definition and re-home the defining text from the issuance-grant profile.

issues.title: Core: give mission.expires_at a home in the core's mission claim
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From the family-wide Chief Architect review.

The `expires_at` member of the `mission` claim is currently defined by the issuance-grant profile "ahead of the issuance profile's revision" (draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-issuance-grant, the `expires_at` member section), and the Mission Mandate makes it REQUIRED while citing issuance-grant only informatively. The AAuth binding also requires it. Three companions now depend on a member whose definitional home does not exist yet.

**Proposed resolution (core revision):** define `expires_at` as an OPTIONAL `mission` claim member in the core's mission-claim section; issuance-grant, mandate, and aauth then cite the core and drop the ahead-of-revision framing.

Because the core is published, this is filed as an issue for the next revision rather than edited directly.

issues.title: Core: Cross-Domain conformance capability rests on an informative reference
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From the family-wide Chief Architect review.

The core's Conformance section defines the OPTIONAL Cross-Domain capability as: "an implementation claiming this capability conforms to that document", where "that document" (draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-cross-domain) sits in the core's informative references. The core also leans on cross-domain semantics in ~20-25 places in normative context (principal-model invariants, `max_derivations` accounting, delegation-matcher portability, `exp` transitivity), so the capability's conformance bar is defined by a document the reference classification says is optional reading.

**Options for the next revision:**
1. Reclassify the cross-domain reference as normative, accepting that it is conditional on the OPTIONAL capability (the same pattern as the actor-profile reference confined to the Delegation capability).
2. Keep it informative and restate the capability so the conformance requirement is self-contained in the core, with the companion cited for the mechanism.

Option 1 matches how the Delegation capability already handles its Internet-Draft dependency and is the smaller edit.

Because the core is published, this is filed as an issue for the next revision rather than edited directly.

issues.title: Core: decide the IANA registry strategy for the extensible namespaces before WG adoption
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From the family-wide Chief Architect review.

The core deliberately creates no registry for five extensible namespaces: Common Constraint names, integrity-anchor `typ` values, `mission` claim members, lifecycle states, and `controls` members. Extension rests on collision-resistant naming plus author coordination (e.g. the `mission-` prefixed `typ` values are "author-coordinated"). Companions have since grown parallel unregistered namespaces (denial-reason enums in expansion/child-delegation/authzen, cascade modes, evidence types), and typed-JWT media-type registration is inconsistent across the family (authzen registers three; audit defines three and defers; child-delegation uses one "by local agreement"; `mission-join+jwt` and the Mandate SD-JWT form are unregistered).

This is survivable for an individual-draft family but "author-coordinated namespace" is not an IETF change-control story, and reviewers will raise it at adoption.

**Ask:** one deliberate family-wide decision, anchored in the core's IANA section, on:
1. which namespaces get IANA registries at WG adoption (lifecycle states and Common Constraints are the strongest candidates: both are load-bearing for fail-closed behavior),
2. which stay specification-defined with collision-resistant naming, and
3. a single rule for registering typed-JWT media types.

Because the core is published, this is filed as an issue for the next revision rather than edited directly.

issues.title: Partial derivation has no wire signal
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From a harness-developer review of the family.

Mission Authority says the AS SHOULD signal that derivation was partial, but defines no wire format for the signal. A client can only detect partial derivation by diffing the token-response authorization_details echo against its proposed_authority, and every client will diff differently (array order, defaults, value-space normalization). The harness needs a deterministic signal to decide between proceeding, narrowing its plan, or prompting expansion.

Suggestion: a token-response member (for example derivation: full | partial, or an omitted-entries indicator) so the shortfall is machine-readable without echo diffing.

issues.title: RS denial does not distinguish expansion-eligible from step-up from fail-closed
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From a harness-developer review of the family.

An RS 403 is byte-identical across three cases with different correct client behavior: out-of-authority (expansion-eligible per the expansion companion), step-up required, and unrecognized-constraint fail-closed (Resource Server Enforcement). The expansion companion calls the expansion-versus-step-up distinction security-relevant, yet it is undetectable from the error, so every harness routes 403s heuristically, and misrouting turns a step-up into an authority-widening ceremony.

Suggestion: WWW-Authenticate error detail (or an error extension) distinguishing insufficient_authority, step_up_required, and constraint_unrecognized, so the denying component states which path it denies into.

issues.title: invalid_grant is overloaded and the derivation budget is unobservable
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From a harness-developer review of the family.

Revoked, expired, superseded, and controls.max_derivations exhaustion all surface at the token endpoint as invalid_grant. Disambiguation requires optional introspection, and there is no way to query remaining derivation budget at all, so a harness budgeting refreshes across an hours-long Mission (each refresh counts against the cap, including committed-but-undelivered issuances) is planning against an unobservable counter.

Suggestion: a disambiguating error detail on the token response for the non-active cases, and a budget surface (for example remaining derivations on the Status response or introspection projection).

issues.title: Constraint-key capability negotiation is unspecified
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From a harness-developer review of the family.

Mission Authority says the AS SHOULD emit only constraint keys the RS is known (by registration or deployment policy) to understand, but no mechanism exists for that knowledge. A mismatch fail-closes tool calls at the RS (correctly), yet nothing lets the AS learn, or the client predict, which keys a given RS supports.

Suggestion: an RS metadata member (RFC 9728 protected-resource metadata extension) enumerating supported constraint keys, which also gives the domain-authorized-issuer check a companion surface to verify against.

issues.title: Core: Token Exchange delegation surface — three hardening gaps
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Found during an OAuth-expert review of the full Mission-Bound Authorization family. These three findings all concern the OPTIONAL Token Exchange delegation surface in the published core; they do not affect the mandatory issuance chain. Filed as an issue rather than a PR because the core is published.

## 1. Bare possession of the agent's token functions as delegation consent (Major)

Section: *Delegation Within a Mission*.

Token Exchange is delegate-initiated ("The AS MUST authenticate the delegate at the Token Exchange"), and nothing requires the AS to verify the subject token's sender-constraint or otherwise confirm the delegating party authorized the exchange. RFC 8693 provides no proof-of-possession check on `subject_token`, and the primary token is only `SHOULD` be sender-constrained. Any party that legitimately receives the agent's access token (most obviously an audience Resource Server) can replay it into Token Exchange and mint itself a delegated token bound to *its own* key, provided it matches `allowed_delegates` — and when `allowed_delegates` is absent, "any actor is permitted (subject to `max_depth`)." This audience-replay-into-exchange threat (a standard RFC 8693 Security Considerations concern) is not named in *Delegation and Chain Compromise*.

Suggested resolution: require the AS to apply explicit delegation-authorization policy at exchange; make `allowed_delegates` RECOMMENDED, or make its absence mean "no delegates permitted" rather than "any actor"; and name the threat in Security Considerations.

## 2. Self-exchange down-scoping has no requester-identity check (Major)

Section: *Self-Exchange Down-Scoping*.

The section allows an agent to present its own Mission-bound access token as `subject_token` with no actor, but contains no normative requirement that the AS verify the requesting client *is* the Mission's `client_id` (or holds the presented token's `cnf` key). A thief or downstream holder of the agent's (SHOULD-only sender-constrained) token can exchange it with no `actor_token`, obtaining a fresh token with `client_id` = the approved agent, `cnf` = the *presenter's* key, and no `act` chain — laundering possession into an unattributable, key-bound credential indistinguishable from the agent's own. The delegation path at least records the delegate in `act`; this path records nothing.

Suggested resolution (one sentence): "The AS MUST verify that the client authenticated at a no-actor exchange is the Mission's `client_id`."

## 3. The `client_id` redefinition breaks attribution for generic RFC 9068 consumers (Major)

Sections: *Delegation*, *Resource Server Enforcement*.

Keeping `client_id` equal to the approved agent on delegated tokens deliberately overrides RFC 8693 Section 4.3 (incorporated by RFC 9068 Section 2.2) while still stamping the token `at+jwt`. Every generic RFC 9068 consumer that authorizes, logs, or rate-limits on `client_id` will attribute a delegate's actions to the approved agent, and the only guard is a `SHOULD NOT` route delegated tokens to such an RS. The *Design Alternative* section argues the case, but redefining a registered claim's semantics inside an unchanged token type is the cross-consumer confusion RFC 8725 warns against, and it will draw WG pushback.

Suggested resolution: consider keeping `client_id` per RFC 8693 and carrying the approved agent as a `mission` claim member (e.g., `mission.agent`), which the claim's open-object design already supports; or, if the current design is kept, strengthen the guardrail beyond `SHOULD NOT`.

issues.title: Core: extensibility seam for Deferred Approval + stale forward-references
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Found during an OAuth-expert review of the full family. Two places where the published core needs a normative seam or an updated forward-reference so companions compose cleanly. Filed as an issue because the core is published.

## 1. Deferred Approval needs a sequencing extension seam (Major)

`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-approval` (Deferred Approval) "explicitly overrides" the core requirement that the Mission record be created *atomically with issuance of the authorization code*. But the core's *Extensibility* section enumerates four extension seams (authority types, integrity anchors, the `mission` claim, lifecycle state) and none covers approval **sequencing**, and the core never references the profile even informatively. As written, a core-conformant auditor or verifier would judge a deferred deployment non-conformant against the core.

Suggested resolution: add an extension seam permitting a companion to relocate or defer the approval event — with the Mission record created atomically with the asynchronous decision instead of with code issuance — or add an informative pointer from the core to the Deferred Approval profile. (An `Updates:` relationship is the heavier alternative.)

## 2. "Not yet specified" substrate forward-reference is stale (Minor)

Section: *Scope and Future Work*. The core lists, as future work "not yet specified," "a substrate-neutral generalization of the Mission model across non-OAuth authorization substrates." That generalization now ships as the Standards-Track `draft-mcguinness-mission-substrate`, together with a shipped non-OAuth binding (`draft-mcguinness-mission-aauth`). The sentence should be updated to point at the substrate draft, or removed.

## 3. Optional: define the token-class vocabulary the family uses (Minor)

The Architecture document refers to a Mission-referenced / Mission-derived / Mission-bound token taxonomy and attributes it to "the core's conformance rule." The core states the *substance* (a `mission` claim alone "is not, by itself, Mission-bound authorization") but does not define those class names. The Architecture attribution is being corrected on the companion side; if the family wants the vocabulary to be normative and citable, the core is its natural home. Optional.

issues.title: Core: minor interoperability, IANA, and editorial fixes
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Found during an OAuth-expert review of the full family. Minor interoperability, IANA, and editorial fixes in the published core, collected in one issue. None blocks the mandatory issuance chain; each is a one-to-two-sentence clarification. Filed as an issue because the core is published.

- **PAR + JAR carriage** (*Submission via PAR*). "a `mission_intent` carried inside a signed request object is equally rejected; only PAR submission is accepted" is ambiguous for a JAR request object *pushed through* PAR (RFC 9126 Section 3 permits `request` in the PAR body), where the stated front-channel rationale does not apply. FAPI-profile deployments mandate signed request objects and will hit this. State explicitly whether PAR+JAR carriage is forbidden or permitted, with the real rationale.

- **Client-set `expires_at`** (*Mission Intent* / *Mission Record*). The Mission lifetime is fixed unilaterally by the client, and because the record mirrors the Intent and `intent_hash` commits the approved Intent, the AS has no defined way to record a policy-narrowed expiry; nor is it stated whether the approved Intent may ever differ from the submitted Intent. Add one sentence (e.g., "the approved Intent is byte-identical to the submitted Intent; an AS that cannot accept it refuses with `invalid_authorization_details`").

- **RS multi-entry combination rule** (*Resource Server Enforcement*). The combination rule is unstated when multiple `authorization_details` entries cover the same resource and action with different `constraints`. RFC 9396 does not settle this, so implementations will diverge. State the union rule: "a request is permitted if at least one carried entry permits it."

- **`resource_match: "prefix"` edges** (*Subset Rule*). "extends its path at a path-segment boundary" is undefined for URIs carrying a query or fragment, and empty-path vs root-path (`https://a.example` vs `https://a.example/`) compare unequal under the stated RFC 3986 syntax-based normalization. Prohibit query/fragment in `resource` values and define the empty-path case.

- **AS mix-up on the consent-bearing redirect leg** (*Mission Approval*). The approval event newly makes the redirect leg consent-critical; add a `SHOULD` for the RFC 9207 `iss` authorization-response parameter, per RFC 9700 Section 4.4. RFC 9700 is currently cited only generically.

- **`approval_event_id` semantics** (*Mission Record*). "used as the approval idempotency key" is a REQUIRED member whose idempotency semantics are never defined (which operation, who retries, behavior on duplicate). Specify the dedup behavior (e.g., a retried approval callback MUST NOT create a second Mission) or demote it to an opaque audit identifier.

- **Deployment-level MUSTs without a publication surface** (*Mission Approval*, *Revocation*). "A deployment MUST declare a minimum approval-authentication strength" and "MUST NOT couple routine token revocation to Mission revocation unless it documents that behavior" have no defined publication artifact in this document, so they are unverifiable. Point at a concrete surface (the Deployment Profile) or soften to `SHOULD`.

- **IANA change controller** (*IANA Considerations*). All six registrations list "Change Controller: IETF"; the JWT Claims, OAuth Parameters, Token Introspection Response, AS Metadata, and Protected Resource Metadata registries use IESG as change controller for IETF-stream documents.

- **Refresh example malformed** (*Mission-Bound Access Tokens*). The `DPoP` header field is shown after the blank line terminating the header section, i.e., inside the message body. Move it above the blank line so implementers do not copy a malformed HTTP message.

- **`proposed_authority` containment** (*Mission Authority*). Clarify that each proposed entry's `resource` must lie within the Intent's `resources` array; the derivation rules already make an entry outside it underivable, so a client benefits from being told before a guaranteed refusal.

issues.title: Core: anchor verification-key retention to the audit horizon
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From an enterprise deployability review (IAM-operator seat), key-custody lens.

**Finding.** Signing and Key Rotation says the AS "SHOULD retain the verification key for each key identifier it has signed under long enough to verify tokens issued before a rotation". "Long enough" is unquantified, while the same document defines an audit horizon of at least the Mission's lifetime plus a declared post-expiry period, and the worked examples run Missions for months. Runtime evidence and Mission Receipts reference tokens whose signatures an auditor may want to re-verify inside that horizon.

The companions already fixed this for their own artifacts, each differently: the status companion requires per-`kid` retention for the Mission record retention period, and the AuthZEN binding requires retired evidence keys to remain resolvable for the evidence retention window. The core's token keys are the only ones left on "long enough".

**Recommendation.** Anchor the retention SHOULD to the audit horizon ("at least as long as the audit horizon of any Mission whose tokens were signed under that key"), and cite the family-wide retired-key rule the companions are converging on, so one rule is stated once and cited everywhere.

issues.title: Core: issuer signing-key custody guidance (compromise voids the model)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From an enterprise deployability review (IAM-operator seat), key-custody lens.

**Finding.** The security model states that Mission Issuer key compromise "voids the model" and names it the strongest trust assumption in the suite, and the adversary model excludes key forgery by assumption. Yet no document in the suite says anything about how that key is generated, held, or controlled: no custody-grade language (even SHOULD), no non-exportability, no dual control, no key hierarchy. "HSM" appears exactly once in the whole suite, in a parenthetical about PEP replicas in the runtime profile. For a family whose value proposition is compromise resistance, the custody of the one component whose compromise voids the model is out of scope without being declared out of scope.

**Recommendation.** Add SHOULD-level issuer signing-key custody to Security Considerations: non-exportable keystore or HSM/KMS-grade custody, dual-controlled generation. One sentence stating that key-compromise recovery is deployment-owned (and belongs in the deployment's documented procedures) would close the loop without growing the core.

issues.title: Core: recommend per-artifact-class signing keys (distinct kids)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From an enterprise deployability review (IAM-operator seat), key-custody lens.

**Finding.** One `jwks_uri` key set signs both high-volume, short-cadence artifacts (nonce-bound Mission Status responses, which cannot be cached across consumers, plus decision-adjacent artifacts) and low-volume, years-lived, estate-wide artifacts (Mandates, Issuance Grants, Consent Evidence). The family's `typ` discipline prevents cross-artifact confusion under a shared key, but it does nothing for blast-radius separation, which is the operator's actual concern: a deployment cannot put the Mandate and grant keys under HSM dual control while running status and evidence signing as replicated online KMS keys if nothing recommends separating them.

**Recommendation.** Recommend (SHOULD or deployment guidance) distinct `kid`s per artifact class within the one `jwks_uri`, so high-value low-volume signing can sit under stricter custody than high-QPS online signing. No wire change; verification is already `kid`-indexed.

issues.title: Core: state the opaque-access-token position explicitly
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From an enterprise deployability review (IAM-operator seat), migration lens.

**Finding.** The core mandates RFC 9068 JWT access tokens carrying `authorization_details` for Mission-bound issuance. Estates whose Resource Servers validate opaque/reference tokens via introspection, a large fraction of installed enterprise deployments, are thereby foreclosed silently: the JWT requirement is stated, but the consequence for opaque-token estates is never acknowledged, and the introspection surface is defined as an add-on for JWT tokens rather than as an opaque-token binding. The first question a brownfield architect asks ("can my reference-token estate adopt this?") has no answer in the document.

**Recommendation.** State the position explicitly, even if the answer is "out of scope: Mission-bound access tokens are JWTs; introspection provides Mission state to Resource Servers; opaque-token estates enter through the standalone MAS binding at the runtime layer". One paragraph in Applicability or the token section removes the silent foreclosure.

issues.title: Core: Mission Client fallback guidance when mission_bound_authorization_supported is absent
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From an enterprise deployability review (IAM-operator seat), migration lens.

**Finding.** The metadata rule says a client "MUST NOT infer that the AS supports this specification" when the flag is absent, and the core deliberately defines no capability negotiation. What the Mission Client should then do is unspecified anywhere in the suite: fall back to a plain scope request (silently converting governed work into ungoverned work), refuse, or route to a MAS. In a mixed estate (one agent talking to one Mission-aware AS and three plain ones) this is the client's most common decision, and the silent-widening path is the dangerous default.

**Recommendation.** One paragraph of guidance: a client holding a Mission Intent MUST NOT silently downgrade governed work to an ungoverned authorization request against a non-advertising AS; the standalone MAS binding is the governed alternative for estates whose AS cannot change. This composes with the existing downgrade-by-omission treatment, which covers the issuance-side and RS-side halves but not the client's.

issues.title: Core: introspection freshness/caching semantics for core-only deployments
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From an enterprise deployability review (IAM-operator seat), PDP/runtime-cost lens.

**Finding.** The core's introspection projection reports `mission.state` with no freshness metadata. `fresh_until` and `mission_max_stale_seconds` exist only in the status companion, which also adds the rule that a consumer MUST NOT cache reported state across requests when `fresh_until` is absent. An RS deploying core-only introspection therefore has no conformant caching rule at all, which in practice means per-request introspection, exactly the pattern the core elsewhere tries to avoid ("Derived tokens SHOULD be short-lived so that a transition ... takes effect promptly without per-request revocation checks").

**Recommendation.** Either note normatively that caching semantics for the introspection projection require the status companion (and that per-use introspection is the only conformant pattern without it), or define minimal freshness semantics in the core's own introspection section. Related but distinct from the token-lifetime guidance ask in #119.

issues.title: Define the portable Mission Receipt schema
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Roadmap prioritization from the handbook alignment review.

**Current state.** The runtime profile (Section 11.3) names the Mission Receipt, the portable, tamper-evident projection of a runtime enforcement evidence record, and gives a SHOULD-bind list (mission id and issuer, decision, policy versions, executor and act chain, custody boundary, target, outcome, timestamps, previous-receipt digest), but the portable schema and canonical byte representation are explicitly deferred (its Section 16).

**Why promote it.** The handbook's validation chapter argues the evidence family answers the AI governance frameworks' record-keeping demands with receipts, not logs. That argument leans on receipt portability, and the deferred schema is the gap between the promise and the wire.

**Proposed home.** Define the schema and canonical byte representation in Mission Audit Transparency, since receipt chaining and tamper-evidence are transparency concerns and the family already does canonical bytes there (JCS, domain-separated envelopes), with the AuthZEN profile referencing it rather than defining a second shape.

issues.title: Core definition says "human-approved"; the Approver is a role, not a species
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Filed per the published-core convention (see #183-#188).

**Finding.** The core's own definition compresses the approval event into a species requirement, twice: the introduction ("This document defines a Mission: a structured, human-approved, integrity-bound authorization artifact for OAuth 2.0") and the terminology entry ("a structured, human-approved, integrity-bound OAuth authorization artifact"). Everything else in the family is species-neutral: the Approver on the record is an `{iss, sub}` principal, the Architecture's litmus and the handbook read "a human (or authorized policy) approved," Progressive Authorization adjudicates in-ceiling expansions by policy, and policy-approved child creation exists in Child Delegation. The definitional sentences are the strongest remaining statement that approval means a person.

**Proposed change.** In both places, "human-approved" becomes "explicitly approved," and the terminology section gains one sentence on the Approver: an accountable `{iss, sub}` principal, a human directly or an authorized policy tracing to a human-consented ceiling or human-approved policy, with `policy_version` keeping that chain re-checkable. The Architecture's Approve and Record verb now carries the full composition statement (spectrum of approving authority, signals as decision inputs never authorities, generated judgment never the sole authority, the class guard floor); the core needs only the definitional fix and the one-sentence trace rule.

**Why it matters.** The approval event is the architecture's foundation, and reviewers read the definition first. "Human-approved" invites the consent-screen critique (a manager approving forty missions a day is the same rubber stamp one level up) against a design that already answers it: what the event requires is accountability and commitment against committed inputs, not a species, and the human enters deliberately through the policy-consent chain and the class guard.

issues.title: Core: give the approval-strength declaration a home and recast the correlation-property duty
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
From the burden-reduction review (companion to the Tier 3 PR), two core duties per the published-core convention:

1. **The minimum approval-authentication strength declaration** is a MUST-declare with no named home artifact. Proposed: it becomes one field of the deployment's published statement (the Enforcement Scope Statement / Mission Deployment Profile), so the essential fact stops floating.
2. **The Mission-ID correlation property SHOULD-document** (also mirrored in the AAuth binding) has no named consumer. Proposed: recast as Privacy Considerations guidance rather than a publication duty.

issues.title: Roadmap: the Mission Model split (plan of record and triggers)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
The end-state for the substrate inversion finding: promote the substrate contract into a binding-neutral **Mission Model** (Standards Track) that normatively owns the Mission record and identifiers, principals, Mission Intent, the abstract Authority Set with subset semantics, the approval-event duties, integrity anchors and canonicalization, the lifecycle state machine, the abstract Mission-bound credential contract, key material, audit horizon, and evidence references, with OAuth, MAS, and AAuth re-scoped as bindings. Deferred in favor of the substrate-as-contract + move-on-touch policy (see the substrate's Status section).

**Rules when executed:** relocation only (every normative Model sentence traceable to core or substrate text); the core loses text only by replacement with a normative reference; zero new core MUSTs; discovery and metadata sections byte-identical; the Model defines no wire surface (no endpoints, parameters, claims, or media types); verification by MUST-inventory (1:1 accounting) plus byte-diff of the rendered discovery sections.

**Staging:** Stage 1: write the Model by relocation (core + substrate), rev the core section-by-section to pointers plus OAuth deltas, retire the substrate into the Model, crosswalk. Stage 2: repoint the ~27 companions' Mission Substrate sections (natural moment to fold in the three parked document merges: approval-revision into approval, completion into status, issuance-grant into authority-server). Stage 3: strip the core's remaining restated text to pure binding deltas.

**Relocation map (Stage 1):** Model owns record/identifier/issuer (core Mission Record + Identifier Format), principals incl. Single Accountable Approver, Mission Intent structure (minus PAR), abstract Authority Set + subset + Common Constraints, approval-event duties, anchors + canonicalization, lifecycle semantics + only-active + revocation semantics, abstract credential contract, retired-key rule, audit horizon, anchors-commit-not-prove and authority-hash-not-identifier. Core keeps: PAR intake, RAR serialization + types + tools modeling, grant binding, mission claim + RS enforcement, issuance-gating mechanics, introspection, delegation mechanics, extensibility's RAR points, AS/Protected Resource Metadata byte-identical, OAuth-shaped security/privacy items.

**Triggers for execution (any one):** an OAuth WG adoption conversation begins (ask the WG's document-architecture preference first); a second non-OAuth binding gains real implementer commitment (AAuth graduating from experimental composition); or MAS control-plane evolution starts requiring core revs at a felt cadence. Until then, move-on-touch migrates ownership incrementally and this issue is the plan of record.

issues.title: Roadmap: a reference enforcement adapter (ERP or messaging)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From the enterprise review's finding 3. The Resource Server runtime profile is now named and versioned as the family's enforcement adapter contract; the missing proof is a worked reference adapter for one real resource class (an ERP ledger or a messaging system): its published adapter contract (operation identifiers and classes, operation profiles with normalization, permit windows and replay controls, policy composition, evidence fields, versioned), plus the matching AuthZEN decision-request exhibits end to end. This validates cross-vendor interoperability of enforcement semantics the way the wire appendix validates the issuance flow, and belongs beside the Mission Receipt schema (#194) on the standardization list.

issues.title: Roadmap: the make-it-testable trio (Deployment Manifest schema, Mission Event Envelope)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From P1 findings 8 and 12, designed together with #194 and #210 or they drift apart. (1) A versioned, machine-readable Mission Deployment Manifest: one serialization (owned by the runtime profile, field semantics owned by their profiles, the architecture's illustrative JSON as seed) identifying binding, adoption stage, assurance claims, issuers, state sources and staleness, PEP coverage, custody, adapter-contract versions, mapping versions, evidence posture, and residual risks, for automated discovery, procurement comparison, and conformance testing. (2) The Mission Event Envelope: one signed envelope (event id, type, mission, issuer, actor, timestamp, sequence, payload type and digest) unifying the twelve evidence types' transport, signing, ordering, and registration, evolving the AuthZEN evidence_envelope convention; typed payloads retained; full individual evidence for high-consequence actions, batching or aggregation allowed for low-risk decisions so audit volume stays operationally viable.

issues.title: Revision-based Mission evolution: the alternative and the evidence that would justify it
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body: From P1 finding 10. The proposal: immutable approved revisions beneath a stable Mission identity (per-revision intent, Authority Set, hashes, approval; tokens name their revision; activation makes older revisions non-deriving with a bounded transition window; successors reserved for material identity change). Rejected for now on principle: revision activation is the successor model with different bookkeeping, except it reintroduces mutable authority under a stable identifier and the transition window weakens Termination; immutable anchors per approved object are load-bearing for evidence. The operational cost claim is real, though, and this is the admission-grain wager: the evidence that would justify revisiting is deployment data showing chain lengths and churn (token migration, cascade invalidation, CAS conflicts at redemption) materially burdening real estates. A cheap ergonomic to consider independently: a stable chain identifier for correlation across successors.

issues.title: Future option: a RAR-free first-tier conformance mode (scope projection)
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Parked by decision: too early to cut RAR from the adoption path; this is the plan of record if feedback demands it.

**The finding (adoption review):** the core's authority model is RFC 9396 end to end, and no major IdP (Okta, Entra, Keycloak) ships RAR three years after publication; the documented fallbacks (MAS-first, issuance grants) still bottom out in RAR-shaped machinery. The reviewers rated a RAR-free tier the single highest-leverage adoption change.

**The option, if triggered:** a normative first-tier conformance mode in the core: `mission` claim + state-gated issuance and refresh + revocation by `mission_id` + scope projection, with per-entry constraints served by reference (introspection / Mission Status) rather than carried in `authorization_details`. Full RAR remains the flagship second tier; the subset rule and anchors are unchanged (the Authority Set still exists at the issuer; only its token carriage relaxes).

**Triggers:** an IdP-vendor or deployer explicitly declining the family over the RAR prerequisite; WG feedback naming RAR as the adoption blocker; or an implementation attempt stalling on RAR support. Until then the entry-ramp table's MAS-first path is the documented answer for RAR-less estates.

issues.title: Core: derivation becomes mechanical (narrowing or template mode); retire the reproducibility and inspectability apparatus
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
Proposed core revision from the full-family review (finding S3, the pragmatism wave's top item), filed per the published-core convention. The problem: the Mission Authority section's mandatory story is a purpose-to-permission compiler with reproducible builds (deterministic narrowing-mode derivation, SHOULD-inspectable policy, re-derivation on policy change), a product category no AS roadmap contains; the text itself concedes generative derivation cannot meet the determinism bar.

**Proposed change 1: derivation is mechanical, in two modes, at the approval event.** Replace the Mission Authority MUST list's derivation story with: derivation is mechanical, in whichever of two modes applies, and happens at the approval event over the policy then in force. **Narrowing mode** (Intent carries `proposed_authority`): the Authority Set is the proposal narrowed to policy, each derived entry a subset of some proposal entry, with entries, actions, and constraint values policy forbids removed or tightened. **Template mode** (no proposal): the Authority Set comes from a deployment-configured mapping keyed on the Intent's `purpose` or `resources`, then narrowed to policy: a lookup, never synthesis. `proposed_authority` stays OPTIONAL (making it required would trade the AS-side burden for a client-side one and kill the cheapest integration path). In either mode the existing Intent bounds hold unchanged (resource membership, `controls` ceilings).

**Proposed change 2: the record is the accountable artifact.** Delete the two accountability rules (deterministic reproducibility at a given AS; SHOULD-inspectable policy) and the where-interop-matters paragraph they support: both modes are an intersection or a lookup, so what was derived is fully stated by the committed Authority Set and `authority_hash`, and a consumer enforces the committed result rather than re-running the derivation. `policy_version` stays on the record as an opaque audit correlator with no reproducibility semantics (align the Compromised or Over-Broad Derivation wording, which currently says "audited and reproduced"). Generative derivation (free text, model-assisted) is demoted in place: permitted as a local-policy extension whose output is consented, anchored, and enforced like any other Authority Set, named as the least portable option and not the interoperable path.

**Ripples once this lands (companion PRs, held until then):** the deferred-approval profile's pending-staleness rule collapses to "derivation happens at the approval event over current policy, so a reviewer never decides over a set derived under superseded policy" (its re-derive/re-render machinery deletes); the derivation locality paragraph stands unchanged; the runtime's materialized-view compiler is unaffected (it compiles the recorded set, not the Intent).

**Why it is safe:** litmus property 2 is carried by the subset relationship, not the compiler; both anchors, the consent path, and the derivation-boundary doctrine (anchors commit, they do not prove semantics) are untouched; and the shape is OAuth's own request/grant asymmetry made exact (RAR clients request authorization_details, the AS grants a subset). The AS-side implementation drops from "build a policy compiler" to "intersect two arrays or look up a template."

issues.title: Handbook alignment, round two: semantic narrowing, Mission-record privacy, evaluation-context binding
issues.author: mcguinness
issues.body:
# Mission-Bound Authorization: Draft Family Alignment, Round Two

**For review.** This note replaces the first alignment review, whose
nine proposals have been executed in the
[mission-bound-authorization](https://github.com/mcguinness/mission-bound-authorization)
draft family (ledger below). Three new proposals follow, all raised by
an external book-level review of the handbook on 2026-07-11 and now
carried on the handbook side, so the two works stay one work.

## Round-one ledger

| Proposal | Status |
| --- | --- |
| 1. Laws mapped to the Mission Invariants (Architecture) | Merged, PR #195 |
| 2. Six-property litmus converged to the handbook slotting | Merged, PR #195 |
| 3. Assurance Levels as grantable action classes | Merged, PR #195 |
| 4. Current practice named in the motivation | Merged, PR #195 |
| 5. OWASP agentic threat mapping (Security Model, Appendix A) | Merged, PR #195 |
| 6. Mission Receipt portable schema | Open, tracked as issue #194 |
| 7. Rung-1 rendering identification (template, version, locale) | Merged (Consent Evidence now requires `template_id`, `template_version`, `template_hash`, `locale`) |
| 8. Revocation latency as a priced dial | Merged, PR #200 (lifetime-bounded reliance, runtime as overlay) |
| 9. Approval authority as a role, not a species | Merged, PR #197 |

## Context, refreshed

The handbook has restructured since round one. It is now the cover as
the five-minute introduction, five chapters (the corporate-card
intuition, Designing, Building, Proving, and Weighing Mission-Bound
Authorization as the conclusion), two companions, and four appendices
ending in the citable Field Reference. Three positioning moves matter
to the family:

- **The control plane for delegated authority** is the operational
  reading of the layer (already reflected in the Architecture via PR
  #218): the Mission is desired state, tokens, PEPs, and PDPs are the
  data plane, and the noun is scoped, never an agent control plane.
- **The conclusion carries five falsifiable wagers**: admission grain,
  issuer home, the price of Termination, the necessity of the object
  itself (composition might suffice), and the portability of its
  authority across domains. The last two are new and connect directly
  to proposals 2 and 3 below.
- **The bounded claim is stated up front**: Mission compliance is
  evaluated against the approved representation of purpose, not
  against an independent oracle of human intent. The validation
  chapter now also holds the model against the OAuth community's own
  gap catalog (draft-chen-oauth-agent-authz-use-cases), five framings
  in all.

## Proposal 1: Representational versus semantic narrowing (core, subset rule; or Security Model)

The subset rule is stated as testable containment: exact-or-prefix
resources, subset actions, tighter constraints, capped expiry, no
broader delegation policy. That is representational narrowing, and it
is the right normative floor. What the documents do not yet say is
that semantic narrowing, the property that a child cannot produce
effects outside the parent's approved boundary, is strictly stronger
and not always provable from representation alone: contextual,
quantitative, or cross-vocabulary constraints can compare as narrower
while permitting purpose-inconsistent effects. One or two sentences in
the subset rule's security considerations (or the Security Model)
naming the distinction, with conservative refusal as the required
posture where the comparison relation cannot decide, would close an
overclaim a careful reviewer will otherwise find. The handbook now
carries the distinction in the Field Reference, and the cross-domain
treatment names it as one face of the portability wager.

## Proposal 2: Privacy considerations for the Mission record (core and Mandate)

The Mission record is a record of business intent: purpose, targets,
constraints, approver identities. The core's design already keeps the
token-side claim a reference (`id`, `issuer`, `authority_hash`), which
is the right instinct, but no document states the privacy properties:
the `mission.id` join that enables audit reconstruction is equally a
correlation surface, cross-domain projection hands that correlation
handle to another domain's logs, and evidence retention outlives the
task by design. A short Privacy Considerations section in the core
(reference-not-content on tokens, evidence-store access control,
retention as deployment policy) and a sentence in the Mandate pointing
at selective disclosure as the minimization tool would cover it. It is
an RFC requirement on the IETF track anyway, and cheaper to write now.
The handbook carries the deployment-facing version in the Field
Reference's adversary model.

## Proposal 3: Evaluation-context binding for high-consequence permits (runtime profile, extension or considerations)

`parameter_digest` binds the permit to the request's own fields and
closes request-drift TOCTOU. It cannot freeze the target: the resource
can change meaning between decision and execution (a revised document,
a reclassified record, a query that returns a different set). Worth
either a security-considerations paragraph naming the residual with
the current mitigations (tight permit lifetime, re-evaluate on retry,
bind resource versions as parameters where the resource exposes them),
or a small optional extension letting a high-assurance permit commit
resource version, policy-view version, and decision time. The
considerations paragraph is the minimal move and reuses existing
fields, and the extension can wait for implementation demand. The
lifecycle state version that PR #217 made first-class is the adjacent
mechanism on the record side; this proposal is its permit-side
counterpart. The handbook names the residual in the runtime part.

## Explicitly not proposed

- No regulatory crosswalk inside any draft. Regulation citations age
  badly in Internet-Drafts. The handbook carries that mapping.
- No changes to the README.
- Cross-references from the drafts to the handbook wait until the
  handbook publishes.

pulls.title: Add Mission-Bound Runtime Enforcement companion draft
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Brings the runtime companion (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime`) into this repo alongside the issuance MVP and wires it into the build and README.

## Changes

- **`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime.md`** (new) — copied from the `mission-bound-authorization` suite, with `venue.github`/`venue.latest` repointed to `mcguinness/draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`. Its only cross-reference is to the MVP draft already in this repo (`I-D.draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`).
- **`.gitignore`** — ignore the generated `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime.xml` artifact.
- **`README.md`** — converted to the standard i-d-template multi-draft layout, adding the runtime draft's Editor's Copy / Datatracker / Individual Draft / diff links. `regenerate: on` preserved.

The build auto-discovers `draft-*.md`, so no Makefile change was needed.

## Verification

Built both drafts to text via the i-d-template toolchain — `kramdown-rfc` and `xml2rfc-txt` both pass `OK`. Remaining warnings are benign (RFC2119/8174 inline + YAML header; explicit reference block for the not-yet-published MVP draft).

pulls.title: feat: Non-goals section + standardized consumption-bound vocabulary
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Two changes, both pre-empting recurring agent-authorization critiques without altering the core normative model.

**1. Non-goals subsection (legibility, no normative change).** Names the deliberate deferrals in implementers' own vocabulary, and routes each to where it belongs:
- Semantic / intent verification -> the runtime layer (`{{runtime-boundary}}`); reasoning-declaration attestation is outside both layers.
- Just-in-time Mission expansion -> future (widening needs a successor Mission).
- Lifecycle event distribution -> a Shared Signals (RFC 8935) / CAEP profile, future.
- Human-in-the-loop suspension -> a `suspended` state + holding-token resume, future lifecycle work.
- Multi-hop cross-domain provenance -> future (single hop only here).
- Decentralized agent identity -> out of scope (klrc / WIMSE); this profile governs the approved-task artifact, not the identity.

**2. Standardized consumption-bound vocabulary.** Define `max_budget { amount, currency }`, `max_calls [ { scope, count } ]`, and `max_duration` (RFC 3339 duration) as recognized Mission-level `context` members, so issuance and the runtime layer share one key vocabulary. They are carried and committed by `proposal_hash`, but — unlike `max_derivations` (AS-enforced) — they are **consumption bounds enforced at the point of use by the runtime layer** (`{{runtime-boundary}}`); absent that layer they are not enforced. Added RFC 8935 informative reference. The runtime draft's metering section is updated separately to name these keys.

Build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0 / dup-ref 0.

pulls.title: feat(runtime): name standardized consumption-bound keys in metering
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Companion to the issuance-draft consumption-vocabulary change (PR #2). The runtime metering section now names the three Mission-level consumption bounds the issuance profile defines, with per-key enforcement semantics:

- `max_budget` — atomic reserve-or-charge against remaining balance; refuse when insufficient.
- `max_calls` — atomic per-`scope` counter; refuse past `count`.
- `max_duration` — elapsed wall-clock since Mission activation; refuse past the bound.

Per-entry `constraints` expressing a consumption bound are metered the same way. Issuance and runtime now share one key vocabulary. Build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0.

pulls.title: Align TOC with OAuth RFC naming and conventions
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Structural review of the draft's table of contents, aligning section names and ordering with OAuth/IETF RFC conventions. **Heading text and structure only — no normative content changes.** All cross-reference anchors are preserved, so no `{{...}}` links break.

## Tier 1 — naming alignment

- **Title Case** all section/subsection titles per OAuth/IETF house style (RFC 6749, 9396, 9068, 9700); identifiers stay lowercase in backticks (e.g. `The mission Claim`, `mission_id Format`), mirroring RFC 9396's own `Request Parameter "authorization_details"`.
- **IANA subsections** renamed to the exact registry titles used by RFC 9396 §14:
  - `OAuth parameter` → **OAuth Parameters Registration**
  - `JWT claim` → **JSON Web Token Claims Registration**
  - `OAuth token introspection response member` → **OAuth Token Introspection Response Registration**
  - `OAuth Authorization Server metadata` → **OAuth Authorization Server Metadata Registration**
- `Discovery` → **Authorization Server Metadata** (RFC 8414 / RFC 9396 §10).
- `Modeling tools` → **Modeling Tools and Function Calls** (the old name read as software modeling).

## Tier 2 — structure / ordering

- Merge the BCP-14 requirements language with the term definitions into **§1.7 Conventions and Terminology**, kept ahead of §1.8 Conformance (so the keywords are defined before Conformance uses them).
- Move **Principal Model** up to **Overview §2.1**, before any section relies on the `(iss, sub)` model — and no longer adjacent to Terminology, removing role-definition overlap.
- Fold **Subset Rule** under **Authorization Derivation** as a subsection, where narrowing is first defined.
- Move **Authorization Server Metadata** to **after the protocol mechanics, before Security Considerations**, matching RFC 9396 ordering.

## Verification

- Builds clean: `kramdown-rfc` + `xml2rfc-txt` both `OK`.
- Zero broken cross-references in the rendered output.
- Directional prose ("above/below") audited near every moved section — none went stale; one ("the rules above … subset …") is now *corrected* by the Subset Rule move.
- Confirmed in rendered text that §1.7 (requirements language) precedes §1.8 Conformance.

pulls.title: Refine section names to match blog and draft vocabulary
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Heading-text refinements for a consistent **Mission Intent → Mission Authority → Mission Approval → Mission Record** progression (proposal → derived authority → approval → resulting record). Anchors and cross-references unchanged; build clean, no broken refs.

- **Authorization Derivation → Mission Authority** — the section's output is the **Authority Set** committed as `authority_hash`; "authority" is the draft's distinctive noun (blog: "derive the candidate authority"). Anchor `{#authorization-derivation}` preserved.
- **The Approval Event → Mission Approval** — matches the draft's own "the Mission is the approval" and the blog's "approved mission". The "approval event" concept term stays in the body. Anchor `{#approval-event}` preserved.
- **The Mission → Mission Record** — matches the section's own opening ("A Mission is the durable record created at the approval event") and the existing `{#mission-record}` anchor. Anchor preserved.

pulls.title: Resolve review feedback: duration citation, purpose semantics, derivation invariant
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Addresses the clear-cut items from review feedback. The remaining normative forks (sender-constraint level, conformance profile split, consent_rendering_hash) need an explicit decision and will land separately.

## Fixes

- **`max_duration` citation (issuance + runtime drafts).** It was described as an "RFC 3339 duration," but RFC 3339's normative body is date-time only; the duration grammar (`PT8H`) is ISO 8601, captured non-normatively in RFC 3339 Appendix A. Now: "an ISO 8601 duration (e.g. `PT8H`), matching the `duration` rule in Appendix A of [RFC3339]." Fixed in both drafts for consistency.
- **`purpose` policy/audit contradiction.** It said "for policy and audit" yet "MUST NOT be used to derive, widen, or gate authority." Now recorded for disclosure and audit, opaque to derivation; a deployment MAY consult it for *out-of-band* policy that does not affect derived authority.
- **Derivation invariant restated in Mission Authority.** Added the invariant where implementers need it: the derived **Authority Set** (not the Intent text) is what the Approver consents to and what carries authority; Intent members constrain what the AS MAY derive, never widen it.
- **`mission_resource_access.resource` reframed (item 7), not renamed.** The wire field stays `resource`. The confusing part was its *negative* definition ("not the RFC 8707 param, not `locations`, MUST NOT treat as either"). It is now defined positively: it is a protected-resource identifier of the same kind as the RFC 8707 `resource` value (RFC 9728), from which the AS derives the token `aud`, carried per `authorization_details` entry (which RFC 9396 permits) rather than as the request parameter. The RFC 9396 orthogonality and the distinction from `locations` are kept as clarifications. The value's alignment with RFC 8707 is intentional and now stated, not disclaimed.

## Also verified (no change needed)

- **Section-reference drift:** not reproducible. The source has 0 unresolved refs; all `Section N` literals are correct external-RFC citations; `{{runtime-boundary}}` renders as §14.3 consistently, and the published editor's copy is current. The reviewer reviewed a stale snapshot (Security at §16).

Both drafts build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), no broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Resolve review items 5 and 8 (compatibility-aware)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the remaining review forks in their compatibility-aware form (option 2). Item 6 (sender-constraint) is intentionally unchanged.

## Item 8 — Conformance: named optional capabilities

Keeps the two mandatory roles (Mission Issuer, Mission-Aware RS) but turns the loosely-described optional features into three named, independently claimable capabilities — **Delegation**, **Introspection**, **Cross-Domain** — so an implementation can state exactly what it supports (e.g. "Mission Issuer with Delegation and Cross-Domain"). No separate profile sections; each capability's own section still holds its detailed requirements.

## Item 5 — Consent rendering: out-of-band SHOULD for high-risk

Adds a **SHOULD** that deployments whose Missions carry high-risk authority record presentation-level audit evidence **out of band** (e.g. a hash over the exact rendered consent disclosure), and names the threat the reviewer raised: a buggy/malicious rendering layer can mislead the Approver with no committed trace. The on-the-wire `consent_rendering_hash` and its structured disclosure object remain **deferred to future work** — a normative wire SHOULD isn't possible without defining that object, which would expand MVP scope.

## Item 6 — Sender-constraint: unchanged

Primary tokens stay **SHOULD** sender-constrained; refresh, delegated, and cross-domain tokens remain **MUST**. (Compatibility-aware choice; the security-optimal alternative would make all Mission-bound tokens MUST.)

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), no broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Add non-normative freshness-posture guidance table (runtime)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds an illustrative table to the runtime draft's *Mission state and freshness* section, mapping each action class to a suggested freshness posture.

## What

| Class | Suggested freshness posture |
|---|---|
| Consequential read | Token lifetime or a short state lease; tighter for privacy-sensitive/cross-tenant/bulk |
| Consequential write | A short state lease, typically minutes |
| Irreversible action | Immediate check or single-use permit |
| External commitment | Immediate check or single-use permit |
| Privileged administration | Immediate check, suitable for composition with local step-up |

## Why it's safe

- **Non-normative.** It illustrates the existing normative requirement (each deployment MUST publish its maximum staleness bound per action class) without imposing universal values.
- **Consistent taxonomy.** The five action classes match the *Action classification* table verbatim — no drift.
- **Established format.** Same kramdown GFM table syntax already used by the classification table.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: feat: OAuth Extensions + AuthZEN Profile (recast from the suite, building on the MVPs)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds two thin profiles that build on the two MVPs, recast from the `mission-bound-authorization` suite essays so the OAuth/runtime mechanics live in exactly one place (the MVPs).

**`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-extensions` — "Mission-Bound Authorization for OAuth 2.0: Extensions"**
Recast of the suite "Mission-Bound OAuth Profile". Keeps only the OPTIONAL OAuth-substrate capabilities the issuance MVP defers: a canonical by-`mission_id` Mission Status operation, a lifecycle management endpoint (`suspend`/`resume`/`complete`), graduated revocation-enforcement classes, RFC 9701 signed status evidence, and a Common Constraints registry. Normative dependency on `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`; **re-specifies none** of its mechanics (intent submission, derivation, the `mission` claim, code redemption, token exchange, ID-JAG, lifecycle gating, sender-constraint are all referenced, not duplicated).

**`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-authzen` — "Mission-Bound Runtime Enforcement: AuthZEN Profile"**
Recast of the suite "Mission-Bound Runtime Enforcement Profile". The runtime MVP says AuthZEN is recommended but is *not* an AuthZEN profile; this **is** that profile: Mission-to-policy materialization, the concrete AuthZEN PDP-request envelope (`context.mission`/`actor`/`parameters`/`audience`/`freshness`), and the Decision/Execution Evidence Object schemas. Normative dependency on `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime` and AUTHZEN; defers action classification, PEP placement, parameter binding, metering, and enforcement scope to the MVP.

Both build clean (errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0). The README draft list is auto-regenerated by the i-d-template CI. Suite-side cleanup (removing the originals + repointing references) follows separately in the suite repo.

pulls.title: refactor: focus the extensions draft to Mission Status/Lifecycle; Common Constraints to core
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Acts on the review note: each extension needs a clear scope and specific name (not a generic "extensions" grab-bag), and Common Constraints belongs in core.

**Renamed + refocused:** `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-extensions` -> **`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-status`** ("Mission Status and Lifecycle for OAuth 2.0"). Scope is now exactly the Mission state-management surfaces the issuance MVP defers: the by-`mission_id` Mission Status operation, the lifecycle management endpoint (`revoke`/`suspend`/`resume`/`complete`), graduated revocation-enforcement classes, and RFC 9701 signed status. Removed Common Constraints from it (section, the `mission_common_constraints_supported` metadata member, and its IANA registry).

**Common Constraints -> core** (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`): a new `Common Constraints` section defines registered-vs-deployment-defined keys with value/subset/intersection semantics and seeds `max_amount_usd`/`issued_after`/`issued_before`; the constraints member definition and the Subset Rule now reference it; and a new IANA "Mission Common Constraints" registry (Specification Required) is created in core.

Both drafts build clean (errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0). Note: this touches the issuance MVP core, which has active WIP on the `full-spec-review-consistency` branch — a rebase there may need to reconcile the new Common Constraints section.

pulls.title: docs(oauth-mission): consistency — runtime-enforcement-evidence term + scope reflow
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Two clean editorial/consistency edits to the issuance draft (the uncommitted WIP from the full-spec consistency pass):

- `per-action decision evidence (a policy-decision record per call)` -> `runtime enforcement evidence for each consequential action`, in the "Issuance scope, not runtime enforcement" list — aligns the term with the runtime draft (`decision evidence` -> `runtime enforcement evidence`). No semantic change; the bullet still states issuance does not produce it.
- Reflow of the "Scope and future work" paragraph into two paragraphs.

No normative change (no RFC 2119 keywords touched). Build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0.

pulls.title: style: parameter/member definitions as OAuth-RFC definition lists
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
