pulls.title: Add Mission Deferred Approval companion (profiles gerber Deferred Token Response)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
New OPTIONAL companion: **Mission Deferred Approval**, the approval-time interaction protocol the issuance profile left unspecified. Built per your direction: a **single companion**, on **draft-gerber-oauth-deferred-token-response**, now (ahead of consolidation).

## What it adds
The core records an approval event but treats it as immediate. Real agent review is asynchronous and reviewers commonly approve a **narrowed subset**. This companion profiles gerber's Deferred Token Response (DTR) to supply both:

- **Deferred approval** — the approval MAY be deferred (`completion_mode=deferred`), polled with the DTR deferred grant type. Timing only; the committed authority and anchors are unchanged.
- **Revisable approval** — a `revisable` completion-mode value (registered in DTR's Completion Mode Values registry). When the AS can grant only a narrowed Mission, it extends DTR's `authorization_pending` response (using DTR's §5.4 add-response-parameters hook, so non-revisable clients keep polling normally) with `revision_required` + `clarification_handle` + `rejected_scope`/`rejected_authorization_details`. The client pushes a **narrowing** revision via PAR, bound by the single-use, sender-constrained handle, and keeps polling the same `deferral_code`. Narrowing enforced by the core subset rule (RFC 9396 inclusion for `authorization_details`).

## Design choices (per the decision note, included in the PR)
- **Gerber-native**: a completion-mode value + response parameters on the pending response, not a new error code or a separate grant_mode parameter — matches DTR's actual extension model.
- **Single companion** (not abstract+binding split), core untouched, OPTIONAL.
- **Scoped against the four existing narrow/widen concepts**: shaping (narrow before), derivation/subset (narrow at derivation), expansion (widen after), progressive authorization (drawdown). This is the fifth, distinct one: negotiate-the-approval, narrowing, during review.
- **Integrates** with consent-evidence (fresh `consent_rendering_hash` on re-review), shaping (the `rejected_*` signals feed re-shaping), and the integrity anchors (final narrowed `authority_hash`).

## Notes
- Normative deps on the core and gerber DTR (both individual drafts), so it cannot advance ahead of them — same posture as the suite's other normative deps.
- Wired into README (new section under a renamed "Approval time" group + an approval-time bundle note) and .gitignore.
- This is additive scope; the **status+signals / runtime+authzen consolidation** remains the separate larger pass.

All 12 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Make consent-vs-enforcement honesty explicit at the approval point
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the review's findings 3, 4, 5 and the two smaller items as core honest-framing fixes; #6 gets a framing clarification with the category decision left to you.

- **#3 (the family's biggest footgun)** — at the approval/rendering step, the core now states plainly that *rendering a bound is not enforcing it*: per-entry `constraints` bind only at an RS that enforces the key, the consumption bounds (`max_budget`/`max_calls`/`max_duration`) only under the runtime layer, and absent those they are **advisory** (rendered, consented, committed, enforced nowhere). The AS SHOULD tell the Approver which rendered bounds its deployment actually enforces. This puts the README's "audit, not action-time defense" honesty where the Approver's consent is actually formed.
- **#4** — same place: `max_derivations` is an origin-AS cap that does **not** bound a Resource AS's local-token minting in another domain, so "max 200 derivations" is not read as an end-to-end cap.
- **#5** — the flat subset rule's limitation (no resource containment / action families; byte-exact `resource`, flat `actions`) is now named as **known near-term work in the core**, not only in runtime's out-of-scope list, since it is the subset rule's limitation.
- **smaller (authority_hash)** — the abstract now leads with "audit anchor, verifiable by full-set holders"; downstream/cross-domain parties hold only subsets and can't recompute it.
- **smaller (aud vs resource)** — reconciled the "aud derived from resource" line with the coarser-`aud` rule, so an implementer doesn't read `aud == resource`.

**#6 (runtime Standards Track)** — I made the *framing* fix only: runtime now states it is the architecture/invariant layer and the interoperable wire surface is the AuthZEN binding (two invariant-conforming deployments don't thereby interoperate at the PEP-PDP boundary). I did **not** change its category. The real options are: (a) keep Standards Track and defend the invariants-spec framing; (b) move the normative wire bits into the AuthZEN draft and make runtime Informational/BCP. That's your call — happy to do (b) as a focused change if you want it.

Core + runtime build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Strengthen the load-bearing properties (review findings 1, 2, 3, 5)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves findings 1, 2, 3, and 5 as scoped fixes. **#4 (offline attenuation)** is held as a separate companion effort to green-light next, per the analysis.

### #1 — Mandatory freshness for the classes that justify it (runtime)
The kill switch was soft (latency bounded by token lifetime). Now: for the irreversible / external-commitment / privileged-administration classes, the Mission state source **MUST** be an active freshness mechanism (introspection, Status, or Signals); **token-lifetime expiry alone is not acceptable** for these classes, since it only ages out a revoked Mission instead of stopping it. Closes the "marketing says instant, mechanics say eventual" gap where it matters.

### #2 — Named agent-compromise-resistant enforcement tier (runtime)
Turns the self-asserted claim into a verifiable one. A deployment claims **agent-compromise-resistant enforcement** only when, for the high-consequence classes, custody (cnf key in the PEP), no-unmediated-egress, action-bound approval, and active freshness are **all MUST** (each is SHOULD in the base profile). Leave any as SHOULD → must not make the claim. "Protects against agent compromise" now has teeth.

### #3 — Consent binds the derived authority, not the goal (core + consent-evidence)
The deepest consent-integrity gap, closed at the principle level: the object the Approver consents to is the **derived Authority Set** rendered in human terms; `goal`/`success_criteria`/`purpose` are context; an approval surface — or a consent disclosure (`authority_summary`) — that renders only the goal and not the derived authority **does not conform**. Shuts the goal-only product default.

### #5 — Scope the derivation-reproducibility MUST (core)
Corrects a MUST I introduced that the permitted free-text path violated. Reproducibility is **MUST** for narrow-from-proposed-`authorization_details` (deterministic); generative/free-text derivation is **SHOULD/best-effort with disclosure** plus `policy_version` for after-the-fact review.

README bundle rows reflect #1/#2 non-normatively (teeth live in runtime). Core + runtime + consent-evidence build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Add Mission Offline Attenuation companion (profiles Attenuating Agent Tokens)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Finding #4, green-lit: removes the Authorization Server from the sub-agent fan-out hot path. New OPTIONAL companion profiling Attenuating Agent Tokens (draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens) — a Mission-bound token holder mints a narrower child **offline**, no issuer round-trip, carrying the same `mission` claim.

I fetched the AAT substrate to profile it faithfully; the key fact shaping the design is that **AAT defines no revocation of its own**, which is exactly why the runtime-state recheck has to be mandatory here.

### Built with the three preconditions from the analysis
- **Verifiable narrowing**: the child carries the parent token chain, so a consumer checks the subset (AAT's capability monotonicity) from the tokens it holds — solving "the consumer only holds the child."
- **Kill switch via runtime**: AAT can't recall an offline child, so a Mission-bound attenuation chain **MUST** be consumed under the runtime profile, which re-checks Mission state at use; a revoked Mission stops the whole chain regardless of children's `exp`. A deployment **MUST NOT** accept these tokens on a path that doesn't enforce Mission state — so it's an **enforced-tier** capability.
- **`authority_hash` as lineage**: it commits the root Mission's Authority Set and rides the chain as an audit anchor, not the narrower child's own commitment.

### Boundaries
Offered **alongside** AS-mediated delegation (online, issuer-observed), not instead of it. Distinct from **Child Mission Delegation** (which creates a separate child Mission with its own lifecycle; this creates no new Mission — every child rides the same Mission and dies with it).

Additive companion (consistent with the no-consolidation decision); core untouched; wired into README (Sub-agents group) + .gitignore. All 13 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

Note: adds a normative dependency on draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens (an individual draft), so it can't advance ahead of it — same posture as the gerber DTR dependency in Mission Deferred Approval.

pulls.title: Name the prompt-injection threat model; add a harness session-taint rule
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Acts on the lethal-trifecta review: states the posture the design already takes implicitly, and adds the session-level control the per-action layer structurally can't express. Did both of the reviewer's offers, since they're complementary.

## Framing (honest, each draft about its own layer)

- **core security considerations** names the threat (an injected agent combining private-data access, untrusted-content exposure, and external communication). The core constrains the data-access leg (a Mission narrows authority to the task; per-task Missions shrink blast radius), contributes the inert `goal`/`purpose`/`success_criteria` so injected text can't talk a Mission into widening itself, and states plainly it provides no information-flow control: within-scope laundering is out of reach, and exfiltration is the runtime layer's job.
- **runtime security considerations** state that runtime gates the exfiltration leg (external communication is consequential; with mediated execution the agent doesn't even hold the egress credential), but conditional on complete PEP placement (the Achilles' heel: every channel, DNS, logs, error strings, shared stores, must be mediated, and it can't prove a deployment enumerated them all), and with no information-flow control (a sequence of authorized steps can compose into an exfiltration no single check catches; `max_calls`/`max_budget` bound volume, not flow).

## Mechanism (the design opportunity)

- **harness** gets a new "Untrusted Content and Egress" section. A governed session is tainted once attacker-influenceable content enters it (a fetched doc, a tool result, an inbound message). Once tainted, before a consequential external-communication or external-commitment action, the harness SHOULD require a fresh action-bound approval or downgrade that authority, the plan-then-execute pattern: untrusted content may inform planning but must not, alone, drive egress. Framed honestly as a coarse session-level control, not information-flow control (it doesn't track which datum reached which action; it raises the bar; it is recorded in Harness Evidence). This converts the trifecta defense from "hoped deployments preserve it" into something the harness enforces, and gives the harness draft a sharper purpose.

This is the honest framing a Cedar/AuthZEN reviewer will test, stated before they do. Core untouched in substance (security-considerations text only). All 13 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Add Mission Audit Transparency companion (profiles SCITT)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
New OPTIONAL companion: **Mission Audit Transparency**, profiling the SCITT architecture so the suite's audit trail becomes tamper-evident, append-only, and independently verifiable. I fetched the SCITT architecture to profile it faithfully.

## The gap
The suite produces many evidence records (approval event, lifecycle transitions, runtime decision/execution, consent, harness, orchestration, child). Each is signed, but *signed is not transparent*: a key holder can backdate, drop, or reorder records, and a cross-domain auditor must trust the issuer's own logs.

## The profile (defines no new evidence; registers the existing records)
- **Registers** Mission evidence as SCITT **Signed Statements**.
- **Mission as subject** (`sub`), derived deterministically from `mission` `origin`+`id`, so all of a Mission's evidence is one **append-only feed** an auditor replays as a single coherent narrative (the "one mission, one story" property).
- **Detached payload / commit-by-hash**: statements commit to the evidence by its existing integrity anchor, not the evidence itself, so **sensitive task data stays out of the log**; the evidence is retrieved separately under access control and checked against the commitment.
- **Receipt** retained as a **Transparent Statement**, so any party in any domain verifies inclusion **offline** — which is also a partial answer to the cross-domain provenance gap a bare narrowed-token signature can't close.

## Honest limits (stated in the draft)
Accountability, not prevention: a dishonest producer can register a false record; transparency makes it permanent, attributable, and visible — SCITT's own model. It proves a record was *registered*, not that the action occurred or was authorized. Completeness is only visible against an expected cadence. Privacy: append-only log → commit-by-hash only, and `sub` derived so it doesn't expose the Subject/task.

## Notes
Additive companion (consistent with the no-consolidation decision); core untouched; new "Audit" group in the README. Normative dependency on `draft-ietf-scitt-architecture` (a WG draft), so it can't advance ahead of it — same posture as the gerber-DTR and AAT companions. All 14 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Add worked non-normative examples across the family
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Reviewed all 14 drafts for worked non-normative examples. Seven had none or only an object-dump; each now has a labeled **Worked Example**, all in the suite's running scenario (agent `s6BhdRkqt3` for `alice`, Mission `msn_8RfX2Lqv9TqMv4z7sA2bN1k0YpEdHc9-`, reconcile Q3 invoices, canonical ids and 43-char anchors), so the examples read as one coherent story across the family.

| Draft | Worked example added |
|---|---|
| **attenuation** | Mission-bound AAT root, an offline-minted read-only child (iss=jwk-thumbprint, `par_hash`, narrowed tools), gateway chain + PoP + Mission-state checks |
| **audit** | SCITT Signed Statement (COSE EDN) committing a consent-evidence hash, Mission-as-`sub` feed listing, cross-domain auditor verifying the Receipt offline |
| **approval** | full deferred → `authorization_pending` → `revision_required` (drop write) → PAR narrow → poll → token flow |
| **signals** | revoke transition as a SET pushed to a partner consumer that stops within seconds; `version` defeats a stale `active` |
| **shaping** | free-text goal → constraint-first, per-task candidate Mission Intent, with the AS (not the shaper) deriving the Authority Set |
| **harness** | overnight resume that finds the Mission revoked and suppresses, plus the session-taint egress gate after an untrusted email enters the session |
| **expansion** | mid-task $1,200 adjustment → expansion request → fresh consent → superseded predecessor with a `predecessor` member |

The other seven drafts already carry at least one strong worked example and were left as-is: **core / authzen / status** are example-rich; **runtime** has the AuthZEN binding flow; **orchestration** has the Worked Unwind Plan; **child-delegation** has the parent/child token; **consent-evidence** has the full Consent Evidence object.

All 14 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Raise rigor of the three profile drafts (audit, attenuation, approval)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The three newest drafts (`audit`, `attenuation`, `approval`) are profiles of external substrates (SCITT, Attenuating Agent Tokens, gerber Deferred Token Response) and are intentionally thin. But measured against the issuance profile and the fuller companions they carried genuine rigor gaps. This raises them to the suite bar **without** restating the substrates or padding for length.

## audit (the priority)
- **Correctness fix:** the `sub` derivation was given as a "for example" construction (`origin` URL with `mission_id` as a path/fragment) while also being required to be *identical across independent producers* so they share one feed. Each producer could be individually deterministic yet pick a different construction, fragmenting the feed and breaking the "one coherent feed an auditor retrieves" value. Now pins a single canonical construction: `<origin>/missions/<id>`.
- **Verifier algorithm + failure taxonomy:** the relying-party verification prose is now numbered steps (1–5), plus a `Verification Failures` section distinguishing **integrity failure** (transparency claim is false → reject) from **audit failure** (unretrievable evidence is *not* proof of tampering), mirroring the consent-evidence distinction.
- Negative case added to the worked example; conformance is now a producer/relying-party checklist.

## attenuation
- Adds explicit consumer fail-safe rules for the **Mission binding** (which this profile owns and AAT does not define): refuse a chain unless every link carries the same `mission` claim (`id`/`origin`/`authority_hash`) as the root, and do not treat a root without a `mission` claim as Mission-bound. Chain mechanics (`par_hash`, capability monotonicity, depth, PoP) remain the substrate's.

## approval (already rigorous — minimal)
- One owned edge case: a Mission Issuer that receives `revisable` without `deferred` MUST ignore it.

All three build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: runtime: relate the enforcement layer to inspection-based controls
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Agentic-AI runtime defenses increasingly pair an **authority layer** with an **inspection layer** (deterministic message-flow checks + semantic intent checks). This positions the Mission runtime profile as the authority half and says plainly what it covers and what it doesn't — folding the useful concepts in as prose, without citing external research (not common in OAuth/IETF specs).

**New security subsection — "Relationship to inspection-based controls":**
- Maps two existing mechanisms to deterministic integrity checks: **parameter binding** (arguments can't be altered after the decision) and **capability-source binding** in the AuthZEN binding (swapped/poisoned tool definitions fail the decision).
- Names two adjacent checks as **out of scope**: the integrity of a tool **result relayed back to the model** (an app can still falsify what the model sees), and binding an **executed action to the model's own decision** (mediated execution bounds it to the Authority Set but doesn't bind it to the model's choice; a deployment that can establish that correspondence SHOULD).
- States the **semantic intent-alignment** stance: such a signal MAY be **advisory** decision input that can only contribute to a *denial*, and MUST NOT widen, grant, or refresh authority — consistent with the inert treatment of `goal`/`purpose` and the issuance profile's semantic-verification non-goal.

**Out-of-scope register** updated to record both gaps and the advisory-input stance.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references. +49/−2, runtime draft only.

pulls.title: consent-evidence: add constraint provenance to the disclosure
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Records **which authority imposed each Authority Set bound**, so the Approver and a later auditor see not just a bound but *whose rule* it is — a delegator ceiling and a court order need different remediation when one fails at the point of use.

**Modeled in the consent layer, not the core.** Provenance is inert disclosure/audit material, so it belongs with the disclosure that is rendered and committed by `consent_rendering_hash`, not in the enforced Authority Set on the token. This:
- keeps the issuance profile and its subset rule **untouched**,
- adds **no wire weight** to tokens (the runtime never reads provenance), and
- puts authorship where auditors already look (audit reconstruction goes through the Mission record / consent evidence; the runtime separately records *which* bound failed, and the join to *whose rule* happens there).

**Shape.** An OPTIONAL `constraint_provenance` member on the Consent Disclosure object: an annotation array of `{ applies_to, source, source_uri }`.
- `applies_to` identifies the bound by the entry's `resource` and the `constraint` key or `action` — so it attributes **actions and bounds alike** (a court order can bar an action, not just cap a number), and avoids a name-keyed parallel map that would drift against `constraints`.
- `source` is a descriptive string (recommended: `subject`, `delegator`, `platform`, `regulatory`, `judicial`).
- `source_uri` follows the OAuth `*_uri` convention and is URN-friendly for non-dereferenceable instruments (`urn:court:order:2026-55`).

Inert by construction: grants no authority, never carried on a token, excluded from enforcement; a consumer that predates it ignores it safely.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references. Consent-evidence draft only, +41 lines.

pulls.title: Add Mission Completion companion (terminal_when / entry discharge)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Gives the inert `success_criteria` an **enforceable, monotonic counterpart** — in a new OPTIONAL companion rather than the core, so the issuance profile stays focused and **completely untouched**. (`terminal_when` was the scope-creep risk in the earlier core-edit attempt; this resolves it as a self-contained draft, the same way every other additive capability layers on.)

## What it adds
`terminal_when`, an OPTIONAL **entry-level** completion condition on a `mission_resource_access` entry. When a condition fires, the entry is **discharged**: the AS stops deriving tokens carrying it.

- **Monotonic** — discharge only *removes* an entry's authority, never widens it, so it's injection-safe by construction (the worst an injected agent can do is retire its own authority sooner).
- **Partial completion** — gates at the entry, so a multi-resource Mission completes one entry at a time while the Mission stays `active`.
- **Subset rule** — a derived entry carries parent conditions byte-equal and MAY add more (the only checkable rule for opaque events); never drops.
- **Fail closed twice** — a consumer that doesn't understand `terminal_when` refuses the entry (mandatory-to-understand, mirroring the core's unknown-type rule), and the AS refuses to derive when it can't determine status.
- **Anchor hygiene** — the descriptor is committed by `authority_hash`; *fired status* is evaluated state, kept out of the anchor.

Sits beside **Expansion** as its mirror image: expansion draws *up* to a pre-consented ceiling; completion retires authority *down* as tasks finish. Composes with the Status `completed` state and with runtime point-of-use denial.

## Also
- The action/constraint/`terminal_when` **derivation cleavage rule** as non-normative guidance — kept in the companion, not the core.
- Worked example (Q3 close discharges the write entry; read continues), conformance, security & privacy considerations.
- Wired into README (Lifecycle group) and `.gitignore`. **Core unchanged.**

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references. New draft, +400 lines.

pulls.title: signals: minimize the lifecycle-change reason instead of broadcasting it
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The reason for a Mission transition (why it was revoked/suspended/completed) can be more sensitive than the *fact* of the transition, and a consumer enforces on the fact alone. This addresses that **by minimization, keeping the useful `reason` field intact** — not by removing it.

The `mission.lifecycle-change` SET is already audience-scoped to a single authorized consumer (`set-protection`), so `reason` is disclosed only to a party already authorized for the Mission, never broadcast. Building on that, a new Privacy Considerations note says a Mission Issuer:
- SHOULD include in `reason` only what that consumer needs;
- SHOULD keep sensitive specifics in its own audit log rather than the event; and
- MAY omit `reason` entirely (it is OPTIONAL) when even a minimal reason would disclose more than the consumer requires.

The `reason` claim, its definition, and the examples are unchanged. +10 lines, signals draft only. Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: runtime: simplify AuthZEN binding, high-consequence classes, RS conformance
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Three function-preserving simplifications from the suite-wide review (stack-rank #1, #2, #5).

**#1 — Decision API binding (~55 lines removed, strengthens substrate-independence).** The runtime inlined a full AuthZEN request/response example and `context.mission`/`context.actor` members, duplicating the normative authzen companion. Replaced with a short pointer: the abstract contract maps onto a binding; the normative AuthZEN binding is the companion; other APIs may be bound elsewhere. Keeps the `{#authzen}` anchor so all cross-refs resolve; fixes the intro, `{{decision}}`, and IANA sentences that claimed the binding/examples were "defined here."

**#2 — High-consequence classes (dedup + a correctness fix).** Named the triad (irreversible / external-commitment / privileged-administration) once as the **high-consequence classes** at the classification floor, and referenced the term at the repeated full-triple sites. Also fixed a genuine inconsistency: the classification table listed execution evidence for only *two* of the three, while execution-outcome evidence MUST applies to all three — the Irreversible row now carries "and evidence." (The custody nuance is preserved: base custody SHOULD still covers two classes; full custody-for-three remains the compromise-resistant tier.)

**#5 — Resource Server runtime profile (dedup).** It re-enumerated the enforcement-scope items (resources, classes, paths, PEP/PDP, types, state source/staleness, evidence, metering bound). Now it records those by reference at operation granularity and lists only the RS-specific additions (per-operation identifiers + min class, operation-profile internals, permit validity window + replay controls, Resource-policy composition, metering topology, evidence fields/privacy).

No normative behavior change beyond the table-evidence correction. Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: consent-evidence: define the material-notice class list once
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Stack-rank #3. The material-notice class set was stated **three times** — the `material_notices` member, the `risk_summary` member, and the Material Notice Requirements section (the authoritative, fullest list of 10 conditions) — with overlapping-but-different subsets that had to be kept in sync.

Both members now reference the canonical list (`{{material-notices}}`) instead of re-enumerating it, while keeping their distinct roles:
- `material_notices` — per-authority notices, one per condition present.
- `risk_summary` — the same conditions identified as risk dimensions.

No conformance change. Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: authzen: v1 correctness fixes (parameter binding, denial reasons, subject, capability drift, pre-PDP evidence)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
First batch of the v1-readiness roadmap: the security **blocker** and the **runtime↔authzen contradictions**. All fixes are authzen-side — the runtime contract already accommodates partial refusal records ("available fields") and lists the conditions, so no runtime edit was needed.

- **B1 (blocker):** PDP consistency check #5 only verified the digest "when `context.parameters` is present," so a parameter-bound (high-consequence) action supplying *neither* parameters nor digest passed and could be permitted — defeating the TOCTOU/parameter binding. Now: `parameter_digest` MUST be present for parameter-bound classes; absent or unverifiable → deny; **a parameter-bound action MUST NOT be permitted without a verified digest.**
- **B2:** added denial reasons `unsupported_authorization_type` and `constraint_unsupported`, mapping the two runtime "Refuse" conditions (unsupported `authorization_details` type; unknown/unmetered constraint) that had no AuthZEN reason. Sharpened `parameter_violation` (evaluated-and-violated, or missing/mismatched digest) so it stays distinct from `constraint_unsupported` (cannot evaluate).
- **B4:** pinned the subject binding — AuthZEN `subject` is the token's authenticated `sub` (the Subject), unchanged under delegation; the agent `client_id` and `act` chain ride `context.actor`.
- **B5:** aligned capability-drift (check #6) with runtime's *conditional* posture — applies only where a source digest was recorded at derivation; source category comes from the materialized policy view, not the PEP request.
- **B3:** added a "Pre-decision refusal records" subsection reconciling the contradiction (runtime requires evidence for pre-PDP refusals; the Decision Evidence Object's PDP-derived members are REQUIRED). Pre-PDP refusals are a refusal record with available fields + a disjoint reason set.

Also added the missing `{#parameter-digest}` anchor that check #5 references. Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references. +61/−9, authzen only.

pulls.title: attenuation: make the kill-switch fail-closed case explicit
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Batch 2 of the v1 roadmap (fail-safe on unreachable state) — which turned out to be **mostly already covered**, so this is a single small tightening rather than a sweep.

**What was already specified (left as-is):**
- child-delegation §Child Mission State: "Consumers that cannot obtain parent state MUST obey the cascade mode: for `status_required`, they MUST refuse..." and suspended-parent → non-active.
- harness §Cached Credentials: "MUST NOT treat cached access as evidence that a Mission remains active" + must verify Mission active and runtime enforcement before using a cached item.

**The one genuine gap (fixed here):** attenuation's kill switch required establishing the Mission `active` within the staleness bound but only *implied* the failure case via the runtime reference. Now explicit: if the consumer cannot establish the Mission as active within the bound — **including when the state source is unreachable** — it MUST refuse; and a chain held in a harness cache is re-checked against current Mission state before each consequential action.

Adds the harness draft as an informative reference. Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. +20/−2, attenuation only.

pulls.title: v1 structural readiness: authzen Conformance, attenuation Privacy, AUTHZEN date
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Batch 3 of the v1 roadmap — the structural IETF-readiness gaps (S3–S5).

- **S3 — authzen Conformance section.** It was the only std-track companion without one, yet it defines its own MUSTs (envelope binding, `context.audience` matching, `parameter_digest`, PDP-side consistency checks, denial classification) and two evidence objects. The new section names the **PEP** and **PDP** obligations separately and scopes conformance to the documented runtime enforcement scope.
- **S4 — attenuation Privacy Considerations section.** It was the only std-track draft missing one. Covers the `mission`-claim correlation surface across the chain, the deferred cross-audience unlinkability (consistent with the issuance profile), and that offline derivations are visible only at the consuming enforcement points.
- **S5 — AUTHZEN reference date.** Aligned runtime (2025) to authzen (2026); both normatively cite the same spec.

All three build clean, 0 broken cross-references. +54/−1 across runtime, attenuation, authzen.

pulls.title: Add Mission Security Model companion (consolidated threat model / trusted base)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Batch 4 of the v1 roadmap — the suite-level **threat model / trusted-base statement** the review found missing (the top structural gap; also independently flagged in the Delegation comparison).

**Placed in a new Informational companion**, not the core: the consolidated view must name the companions, and the core stands alone. A README section was the lighter alternative but is non-normative and never appears in a published RFC, so it wouldn't close the gap for a reviewer.

**What it provides:**
- The **trusted base** in one place — Authorization Server, PEP, PDP, Mission state source, harness, consent rendering layer, Access Request Service, Transparency Service, event source — and for each: *what it must achieve · what it assumes of the others · how its compromise degrades the guarantees*, pointing to each profile's normative Security Considerations rather than restating them.
- The **untrusted-agent** framing and the two structural choices that bound it (authority fixed at approval; credential not held by the agent).
- **Cross-cutting assumptions** (sender-constraint, fail-closed-on-uncertainty, inert intent).
- **What the model does and does not guarantee** (intact trusted base → bounded/attributable misuse; compromised component → degraded per the table; semantic verification a non-goal; substrate threat models inherited).
- A **"document your trusted base"** expectation that builds on runtime's enforcement-scope documentation.

Defines no new mechanism, claim, or registry. Wired into README (new "Security model" group) and `.gitignore`. **Core unchanged.** Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. New draft, ~330 lines.

pulls.title: core: pin Common Constraint comparison to value space
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Batch 5, core area. The registered Common Constraints gave prose subset/intersection rules but never said whether comparison is **lexical or value-space**, so two deployments could disagree on equality — `500` vs `500.0`, or the same instant written with different RFC 3339 timezone offsets / trailing subsecond zeros — making the subset rule non-reproducible across vendors (D1).

Now pinned: `max_amount_usd` compared as a decimal number; `issued_after`/`issued_before` compared as the instants they denote after normalization to UTC; and a registered Common Constraint MUST define its subset and intersection in value-space terms.

**Scope note:** this is the *one* genuine core item from the review's batch-5 list. The other three it flagged turned out to be already specified in the core, so I left them rather than add redundancy:
- audience-scoped projection → `#audience-scope` ("includes only the Authority Set entries whose `resource` that Resource AS is authoritative for… Entries for other Resource ASes MUST NOT be disclosed");
- introspection `mission.state` visibility → `#caller-authorization-and-minimization` (state is Mission data, "MUST NOT reveal Mission detail to an unauthorized introspection caller");
- cross-domain sender-constraint → the cross-domain grant already "MUST be sender-constrained" (`#cross-domain-grant`).

Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. +10 lines, core only.

pulls.title: harness + audit: cross-companion coordination clarifications (v1 lifecycle/sub-agents)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Batch 5, lifecycle/sub-agents area — verified-then-fixed. Two of the five reviewer items were non-issues and left as-is:
- **D5** — cascade_mode is a per-child-**Mission** lifecycle dependency (the child depends on the parent Mission), not a per-entry property; no ambiguity (the reviewer conflated it with per-entry delegation policy).
- **D7** — a stale unwind plan can't exceed current authority: orchestration already says "a compensation action that is consequential MUST itself pass Mission … authorization."

Three genuine clarifications:
- **D6 (harness):** where the orchestration profile is also deployed, the harness MUST NOT resume a governed item the orchestrator has cancelled or is holding for review, even when the Mission is `active` — an unwind decision is not a Mission-state change, so the resume check wouldn't otherwise catch it.
- **D8 (audit):** a Child Mission writes its own feed; its `cascaded` transition is in that feed, and its lineage (`parent` member) is how an auditor reaches the parent feed to see the cascade trigger.
- **D9 (audit):** across trust domains the `sub` is unchanged, so producers share one feed only on a shared Transparency Service; otherwise each holds a partial feed with per-service non-equivocation, and an auditor reconciles across services (or producers register with one shared service).

Adds child-delegation as an informative reference to audit. Both build clean, 0 broken cross-references. +36 lines across harness and audit.

pulls.title: approval + consent-evidence: v1 approval-time clarifications (D10, D11)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Batch 5, approval-time area — these findings were more substantive than the lifecycle ones.

**approval (D10):**
- The revision MUST narrow **every** dimension named in `rejected_scope` / `rejected_authorization_details` (was the ambiguous "at least the refused dimensions").
- The revised Authority Set is derived under the **same `policy_version`** that governed the proposed Mission, so the subset comparison is reproducible by an auditor; if policy changed, the Issuer re-derives the baseline or refuses.
- The revision-cycle bound is now **MUST** (was SHOULD): a Mission Issuer MUST bound revision cycles and resolve to `access_denied` at the bound, so a client can't drive an unbounded reshape-and-retry loop. Security-considerations bullet aligned.

**consent-evidence (D11):**
- Pin what **"faithful"** means for `authority_summary` *without* imposing a layout: it MUST render every `mission_resource_access` entry (`resource` + `actions`) and every constraint, delegation right, and consumption bound — so a verifier can check the rendered set against the committed Authority Set.
- The **expansion delta disclosure** already enumerates what it must distinguish (retained/added/removed/narrowed/expiry/delegation/predecessor), which is the same specification level and consistent with the draft's deliberate non-schematizing, so it was left as-is.

Both build clean, 0 broken cross-references. +27/−9 across approval and consent-evidence.

pulls.title: v1 minors sweep: Intent size bound, harness independent-Mission, audit sub privacy
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The v1 minor-findings sweep. As predicted, most shrank on contact — already covered, or artifacts of the reviewers' excerpts. **Three were genuine:**

- **core:** an AS **MUST** bound Mission Intent total size and array lengths (was SHOULD) — an oversized untrusted Intent shouldn't be able to exhaust the AS at rendering/derivation/hashing. Limits stay deployment-defined (no magic numbers).
- **harness:** define **"independent active Mission"** when a harness leaves a sub-agent running after a parent stop — it's one whose authority does not derive from the stopped Mission (not a Child Mission of it or its ancestors); a cascaded child MUST NOT be classified as independent. Closes a real security-boundary ambiguity.
- **audit:** correct a **misleading privacy note** — it told deployments to "derive `sub` so it does not expose the Subject," but the `sub` construction is fixed and doesn't contain the Subject; the real concern is the durable identifier/existence/cadence being visible in the log.

**Left as-is (verified already-covered or non-issues):** token-TTL ceiling (SHOULD is right), mid-approval Intent re-validation, delegation-depth wording (not actually truncated), `authority_hash` consumer behavior (already audit-only/non-gating), attenuation verification ordering (implied by "in addition to the substrate's chain verification"), `fanout_exceeded` granularity (avoid code proliferation), rendering-assurance rung evidence (each rung already names its evidence member), shaping Informational keywords, orchestration evidence retrieval (deployment concern).

Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. +20 lines across core, harness, audit.

pulls.title: security-model: add the adversary-model coverage table
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds the adversary-centric complement to the trusted-base (component) view: a single table mapping each **adversary move → mechanism that addresses it → residual it leaves**. The residual column is the honest part — what a deploying party still owns.

Covers: compromised/injected agent beyond task, prompt injection trying to widen authority, stolen token, confused-deputy replay, TOCTOU, ambient standing authority, the kill switch, over-broad/mis-rendered consent, the exfiltration leg (lethal trifecta), offline sub-agent fan-out, audit tampering, agent result-fabrication (explicit non-goal), and a compromised trusted-base component.

Also:
- states the **adversary's assumed capabilities** up front (controls the agent, reaches its inputs, captures tokens; does *not* break crypto, forge a component key, or compromise the trusted base);
- calls out the three headline **residuals** on their own — comprehension, single-service equivocation, offline breadth — since those are the limits most often overstated away elsewhere;
- cross-referenced from the guarantees section so the prose and the table don't duplicate.

This is the "honest threat model + what it does/does not stop" piece. Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. security-model draft only.

pulls.title: core: add integrity-anchor test vectors (S2)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The v1 interop fixture the review found missing (S2) — a non-normative appendix with **reproducible** test vectors for `intent_hash` / `authority_hash`.

For a sample Mission Intent and Authority Set it shows: the `value` object, the **exact JCS canonical bytes** of the `{typ,iss,value}` envelope (single line, no whitespace — shown wrapped for layout, with instructions to reconstruct by removing the layout breaks), and the resulting `sha-256:` anchor.

The values are **real**, computed per the integrity-anchor + canonicalization rules (not fabricated — a wrong digest would be worse than none), so an implementation can verify its JCS and base64url byte-for-byte. The vectors also concretely demonstrate the two canonicalization rules that bite: JCS member-name sorting (`iss`<`typ`<`value`; `actions`<`constraints`<`resource`<`type`) and array-order preservation.

```
intent_hash    = sha-256:P38IRTmTaUESJ5RpCw1WXmIqfsQmYek7zxiQWERcq-E
authority_hash = sha-256:-rBZZJ8tVIyGoR1tBg6BO6QG0kimVvef8vjigpoVuPw
```

Placed before Acknowledgments (numbered appendix must precede the unnumbered section). Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. Core only, non-normative appendix.

Scope note: this covers the core's anchor computation — the highest-value, most-cited fixture. The AuthZEN decision/response already has worked examples in that draft; a broader cross-bundle fixture set can follow if wanted.

pulls.title: core: make cross-audience unlinkability an explicit, rationalized v1 non-goal
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Per the decision to **accept the privacy posture for v1** (option b). The cross-audience correlation was already documented, but as "deferred to future work" — which reads as *unfinished* rather than *deliberate*. This records it as a design choice with its rationale, which is the honesty that earns trust:

- **New Non-Goals entry — "Cross-audience unlinkability":** a single canonical `mission_id` (and the shared `authority_hash`) deliberately lets any party correlate a Mission across audiences and resources, *because* a stable, correlatable anchor is what lets a Resource Server, a cross-domain Resource AS, and an auditor bind credentials and evidence to one approved Mission — a core goal of the suite. Pairwise/unlinkable presentation works against that anchor, so it's future work, not a v1 property.
- **Strengthened** the "Mission Identifier Correlation" Privacy Considerations subsection with the same rationale and an explicit anchor, cross-linked to the non-goal.

No mechanism change — this makes the deliberate scope decision explicit so the limit is honest rather than ambiguous. Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. Core only, +23/−8.

pulls.title: editorial: remove parameter/code names from section titles
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
First pass of the suite-wide structural/editorial review: **section titles no longer embed code spans or bare parameter names**, so the rendered tables of contents read as prose (OAuth RFC convention). The parameter a section defines is named in the body, not the heading.

19 headings across 7 drafts renamed. **All explicit `{#anchor}` ids are preserved** (no cross-reference changes), and none of the renamed headings were referenced by their auto-generated id, so the renames are safe. Examples:

- authzen: `context.mission`/`actor`/`credential`/`audience`+`freshness`/`capability_source`/`parameters` → "Mission Decision Context", "Actor Decision Context", …; `policy_view_id` → "Policy View Identifier"; media-type subsections → "Decision/Execution Evidence Media Type".
- core: "`mission_id` Format" → "Mission Identifier Format"; "The `mission` Claim" → "The Mission Claim"; "Composite `active`" → "Composite Active State"; the `mission_resource_access` registration → "The Mission Resource Access Authorization Details Type".
- completion/consent-evidence/child-delegation/expansion/signals: `terminal_when`/`consent_rendering_hash`/`parent`/`predecessor`/`superseded`/`mission.lifecycle-change` headings → prose names.

All 7 drafts build clean, 0 broken cross-references.

This is **phase 1** of the editorial pass. Next: readability of dense normative requirements (sub-bullets / paragraph breaks) and section-name/flow conventions, which I'll do per-cluster so each stays reviewable.

pulls.title: Address the two actionable items from the implementer/eng/security review
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The two small, clearly-worth-it actions from the three-lens (implementer / head-of-eng / security) review. Both are clarifications/examples — no mechanism change.

**1. README — agent-compromise-resistance framing (security review's overclaim finding).** The Governed bundle row read as "adopt this → you're protected." Added a note that agent-compromise resistance is the runtime profile's *named claim*, gated on all four MUSTs (mediated custody, no unmediated path, action-bound approval, active-freshness state source), and that mediated custody **relocates** the high-consequence sender-constraint key into the mediating PEP rather than removing it — so the PEP becomes a critical trusted component whose compromise is out of scope. A deployment missing any condition must not represent itself as agent-compromise resistant.

**2. runtime — Parameter Digest Worked Example (implementer's buildability finding).** The digest *construction* was already pinned (`sha-256:` + base64url of the JCS of the normalized parameter object); what was missing was a **reproducible worked value**. New non-normative appendix gives a `payments.send` operation profile, the normalized parameter object, the exact JCS canonical bytes, and the computed `parameter_digest` (`sha-256:e7GXd0GWwOK2…`), so two implementations verify byte-for-byte — parallel to the core's integrity-anchor test vectors.

Real values, verified by reconstruction. Runtime builds clean, 0 broken cross-references. +59 lines across README and runtime.

The review's larger themes (substrate maturity, full-flow test vectors, the deployment-dependence of the guarantees) are genuine future work, not v1 fixes.

pulls.title: authzen: fix AuthZen -> AuthZEN capitalization in the COAZ reference title
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The MCP-profile (COAZ) reference title used lowercase "AuthZen"; the suite and the OIDF working group brand it "AuthZEN" everywhere else. One-character title alignment for consistency, surfaced by the citation fact-check. Builds clean. authzen draft only.

pulls.title: Resolve review findings: child subset rule, event-driven cache bound, status aud example
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Three findings from external review, all verified and fixed.

**High — child-delegation weakened the core subset rule.** §Subset Evaluation re-described it as "resource equal to or **narrower than**" and "action **deployment-defined as narrower**," contradicting the core's deliberately *flat* rule (resource equality + action array membership; hierarchy deferred). Realigned to the flat rule verbatim and added that resource containment / action-family narrowing are available **only** through the hierarchical-subset extension the issuance profile defers — a Mission Issuer MUST NOT treat a child resource as a subset merely because it's "narrower" under an undefined hierarchy.

**Medium — authzen `event_driven` cache read as unbounded** ("valid until an event invalidates it"). Now bounded: it remains within the staleness bound or lease as for `cached`; a missed/delayed event does not extend validity; on bound elapse the PEP MUST refresh. Aligns with runtime's freshness-bound and Signals' missed-events-not-fail-open rule.

**Low — status revoke-lifecycle example** (no audience supplied → `aud` = authenticated requester) set `aud` to the ERP URL while `sub` was the requesting client. Set `aud` to the requester (matching `sub`). *Verified the other status example is correct and unchanged* — its request supplies `audience=erp.example.com`, so its `aud` is right.

All three build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc; my env uses the `BUNDLE_PATH` override, so I wasn't blocked by the Bundler-version issue), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Label experimental profiles and add a maturity front-door (A1, B6, B4)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Honest expectation-setting about which profiles are ready to build on now. No mechanism change.

- **A1 — experimental banners on the substrate-dependent profiles:** `attenuation` (Attenuating Agent Tokens), `audit` (SCITT), `approval` (OAuth Deferred Token Response) each note they depend normatively on an **unratified** Internet-Draft that may change, and name the stable path to prefer (AS-mediated delegation; signed evidence without a Transparency Service; synchronous approval).
- **B6 — experimental banners on the speculative profiles:** `orchestration` and `completion` are marked experimental and not part of the recommended v1 bundles — newer, less-exercised models. Labeling, not deletion.
- **B4 — README maturity front-door:** a three-tier "what to build on now" guide — **Stable** (core, runtime, authzen, status, signals) · **Stable but situational** (expansion, child-delegation, consent-evidence, harness, shaping) · **Experimental** (the five above) — with the key takeaway that the Enforced bundle is built entirely from stable drafts.

All five drafts build clean, 0 broken cross-references. README + 5 drafts, +57 lines.

Next: **B5** (tighten `shaping`) as a separate PR.

pulls.title: shaping: consolidate the three trust-boundary subsections (B5)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Tightens the longest Informational draft (Mission Intent Shaping) by consolidating its three trust-boundary subsections, which stated one boundary from three angles.

## Change

The shaper-role section had three subsections, each with its own heading and framing intro, that all said "the shaper proposes, it does not issue":

- The Shaper Does Not Issue Authority (`proposes-only`)
- Untrusted Output Principle (`untrusted-output`)
- The Shaper Never Crosses the Trust Boundary (`never-crosses`)

These are merged into one subsection, **The Shaper Proposes; It Does Not Issue**, with three bullets (does not act as a credential issuer / does not mimic Mission Issuer output / does not cross the trust boundary).

## Content preservation

Purely structural. Every normative clause is preserved:
- the untrusted-input `MUST` and the `SHOULD NOT` against structuring output to imply trust
- the credential-issuer `NOT RECOMMENDED` and client-internal integrity `MAY` / `MUST NOT`-rely
- the Issuer-output `MUST NOT` list and the Issuer `MUST ignore`
- the no-boundary-crossing rules

The auditor-deception threat note and the explicit trust-boundary definition are kept, and **Deployment Roles** is unchanged. Net -20 words (duplicated section framing only); no normative change.

Build is clean, 0 broken xrefs.

pulls.title: README: reconcile Governed bundle with the orchestration maturity label
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves two review findings on the README maturity/bundle guidance introduced with the experimental labeling.

## Medium: bundle guidance contradicted the orchestration maturity label

The "Governed agent (recommended for AI agents)" bundle listed `orchestration` as a core member, but:
- the Maturity section marks `orchestration` **Experimental** and says experimental profiles "are additive and can wait", and
- the orchestration draft's own banner says it is "not part of the recommended v1 deployment bundles."

**Fix:** demote `orchestration` from the Governed bundle's core drafts to an explicit experimental add-on for safe unwinding, parallel to how `child-delegation` and `expansion` are already presented ("Add ... for ..."). The Governed bundle now contains only stable and situational drafts, so it agrees with the maturity closer and with the draft's banner. Safe unwinding is now clearly an experimental add-on, not core v1 guidance.

## Low: `authzen` "Stable" bucket could be over-read

The Stable label is right for the mandatory AuthZEN decision binding, but AuthZEN also names informative ARAP/COAZ integrations and optional requestable-denial / MCP-composition text. Added a short caveat so the stable bucket isn't read as blessing those optional compositions.

No normative draft content changed; README only. (README is not built by xml2rfc, so no render step.)

pulls.title: Resolve family-wide expert review findings across all drafts and README

pulls.title: Rename substrate-neutral profiles: drop oauth from seven draft names
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The Mission model's upper layers are not OAuth-specific. This encodes the boundary in the draft names before first submission pins them: profiles extending the Authorization Server's own surfaces keep "oauth"; profiles specifying components outside the AS drop it.

Renamed: runtime, authzen, harness, orchestration, shaping, audit, security-model (now `draft-mcguinness-mission-*`). Kept: core, status, signals, expansion, completion, approval, consent-evidence, child-delegation, attenuation.

Each renamed document gains a **Mission Substrate** section enumerating the primitives it consumes and naming the issuance profile as this version's normative OAuth binding; another mission-based protocol (e.g., aauth) supplying the same primitives can host it unchanged.

Family-wide sweep of cross-references, titles, abbrevs, .gitignore, and README (which now states the naming principle). All 16 documents build clean through xml2rfc under the new names with zero broken cross-references.

Note: the published editor's-copy URLs for the seven renamed drafts change on merge.

pulls.title: Add Mission Authority Server and Mission Mandate; position deployment modes
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Opens the model beyond AS-integrated OAuth deployments, following the substrate boundary the family now encodes.

**Mission Authority Server** (`draft-mcguinness-mission-authority-server`): the AS-optional binding. Implements the Mission Issuer role (intent submission, natively asynchronous approval, record, lifecycle, state, well-known discovery) without being an OAuth AS and without deriving tokens; enforcement joins ordinary OAuth tokens to Missions at the PDP under six normative join rules. Limitations stated plainly (no Mission-bound credentials, no issuance gating, runtime enforcement is a MUST); the upgrade path is the issuance profile. Security model gains the matching trusted-base entry.

**Mission Mandate** (`draft-mcguinness-mission-mandate`): a signed, portable, independently verifiable statement of a Mission's committed facts. Evidence, never a credential; `state_at_issuance` is history, not currency. Optional selective disclosure via SD-JWT (RFC 9901), plain JWS mandatory to implement. Use cases: cross-domain verification, vertical mandate derivation, audit registration.

**README**: two-deployment-mode positioning (OAuth binding flagship, MAS on-ramp, Mandate portability), a Standalone governance (AS-optional) bundle row, document entries, Experimental maturity labels.

All 18 documents build clean through xml2rfc with zero broken cross-references.

pulls.title: README: retitle and restructure around the model, its layers, and read paths
pulls.author: mcguinness

Retitles the front door to "Mission-Bound Authorization" (the suite is named for the model; OAuth 2.0 is the flagship binding) and restructures reader-first: problem, the Mission with both bindings, a verb-spine architecture diagram, a new "How to read this suite" section with six persona read paths, deployment bundles, maturity, and a document catalog regrouped to match the architecture layers (model and bindings, approval time, lifecycle, runtime enforcement, agent runtime, sub-agents, proof and portability, security model).

pulls.title: Add An Architecture for Mission-Bound Authorization
pulls.author: mcguinness

The model spans nineteen documents and two bindings; this Informational document is its single citable structural view. Contents: the Mission and its integrity anchors; roles and components with the two Mission Issuer bindings side by side; the Mission Substrate consolidated as six primitives with a binding checklist for new substrates; the verb-spine layers; deployment patterns; requirements R1-R17; the full document map.

pulls.title: Resolve round-2 review: cross-domain split, two-binding seams, examples
pulls.author: mcguinness

Second full-family review resolution (~15 major, ~50 minor findings).

**Structural**: Cross-Domain Missions extracted from the core into `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-cross-domain` (the core's only remaining in-progress normative dependency is the OAuth Actor Profile, confined to OPTIONAL Delegation); the two-binding composition is now engineered, not asserted (runtime's Mission binding establishment step with the MAS join as its profiled external mode, mission-claim refusal carve-outs, binding-dependent substrate sections); authzen denial-reason extensibility; audit evidence-type extension rule with a Mandate row; MAS requires the full lifecycle operation set, adds `mission_auth_methods_supported` (IANA token-endpoint auth values + `dpop_bound_token`), and states plainly that standalone mode has no delegation/widening surface yet.

pulls.title: Simplify the v1 surface: extract three experimental companions, tier the rest
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Applies the complexity-budget review's park-don't-delete plan. The stable v1 conformance surface shrinks by ~200 normative tokens, five state machines, and four commitment types; every deferred mechanism is preserved as a complete experimental companion.

**Extracted** (one-directional references; IANA actions travel with the mechanism): `oauth-mission-approval-revision` (the revisable mode; the approval profile is now deferred-only with deny-and-resubmit as the stable path), `oauth-mission-progressive` (ceiling/drawdown/`ceiling_hash`/`out_of_ceiling`; under expansion alone every widening is human-approved), `mission-metering` (the three consumption bounds as `context` extensions plus all metering/settlement machinery, with a new consent-integrity rule: a bound is never rendered as enforced unless metered; the core keeps `max_derivations`).

**In place**: child delegation adopts `immediate` cascade (exotic modes to an experimental appendix); consent-evidence Rungs 2-4 to an experimental appendix; harness/orchestration schemas scoped as evidence vocabulary; `evidence_envelope` named the suite's one signing convention; the Common Constraints registry request becomes specification-defined with a future-registry note; attenuation and signals carry honest experimental framing.

**Not done**: no document merges; the introspection projection is retained.

All 23 documents build clean with zero broken cross-references, and no stable draft normatively references an experimental companion.

pulls.title: Add Mission-Bound Authorization for AAuth: the third Mission Issuer binding
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Binds the Mission model to the AAuth protocol (draft-hardt-oauth-aauth-protocol), the first non-OAuth substrate. AAuth natively carries a mission reference on every signed request but leaves the mission's structure implementation-defined, its lifecycle at two wire states with revocation only sketched, and governance evaluation unspecified; this binding supplies those pieces from the Mission model.

The AAuth Person Server is the Mission Issuer: the mission blob carries the Mission record and family anchors under AAuth's `s256` commitment (anchors independently reproducible per the core's canonicalization); propose/clarify/approve is the natively asynchronous approval event; the family lifecycle rides AAuth's `active`/`terminated` states with revocation and expiry made normative. Because the Person Server issues or gates every auth token, **issuance gating holds**, and the auth token is a Mission-bound credential under per-request HTTP Message Signature coverage, so runtime enforcement composes credential-carried.

README and architecture updated to the three-binding reality. All 24 documents build clean with zero broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Hardening wave: design-defect fixes, hierarchy, substrate spec, management
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the architectural review's top four tiers across sixteen drafts, with two new documents (the family is now 26).

**Design defects**: per-capability `source_digest` (benign catalog changes no longer invalidate approved capabilities); the `proposed_authority` Intent member (closes the narrowing-mode derivation contradiction); source-classed, provenance-triggered taint; one digest-encoding convention.

**Hierarchy and constraints**: `resource_match` prefix containment + dot-segment action families (anchors stay byte-exact); `max_amount_usd` retyped to string-decimal family-wide with every computed vector regenerated and cross-verified; `tenant` and `recipient_domain` constraints.

**New documents**: `mission-substrate` (the binding checklist's normative home for future bindings only; nothing existing depends on it) and `oauth-mission-management` (purpose-audited enumeration, dry-run-first bulk lifecycle with per-Mission outcome manifests, incident patterns).

**Interop hardening**: `action_class`/`class_source` evidence members with testable class predicates and the `mission_action_class_floors` resource metadata; the `Mission-Decision` propagation header; consent risk-tiering and layered rendering; the MAS Mission Join Assertion; the audit derivation record; RS logging; a minimum-interoperable-deployment note.

All 26 documents build clean with zero broken cross-references. Note for local builds: past 24 drafts, the i-d-template's `-latest` sed rewriting exceeds BSD sed's buffer; use GNU sed locally (CI unaffected).

pulls.title: Adoption compression: minimal implementation, adoption ladder, tier honesty
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Applies external review feedback aimed at adoption surface, not mechanism. No normative mechanism changes; both core test vectors unchanged.

**README**: leads with "start with the Architecture document and the OAuth core"; a one-screen minimal-implementation section plus "what the core alone does not protect"; maturity split into an adoption ladder and a dependency-stability axis; a crisp standardization ask (adopt the model and the OAuth issuance profile as the substrate; companions proceed as companion drafts); catalog reordered (core first and alone; alternate bindings after the implementation minimum).

**MAS framing**: a peer binding, not a staging area — the governance-decoupled-from-issuance rationale stated in the README, bundle table, and the MAS document itself (deliberate-choice MAY), with the OAuth binding remaining the family flagship.

**Core**: two-tier security statement in the introduction; a Design Alternative subsection on client_id rebinding; the Intent-closure security rationale made first-class; the Authority Set disambiguation sentence; a consolidated privacy opening.

Declined with recorded rationale: the Authority Set rename (vector-breaking) and the README title change.

All 26 documents build clean with zero broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Add editor's-copy targets to every family reference, family-wide
pulls.author: mcguinness

Every family reference stanza in all 26 documents now carries a `target:` URL pointing at the published editor's copy, so cross-document references resolve for any reader while the companions are not yet on the datatracker. 202 targets added. The OAuth Actor Profile reference (a separate datatracker draft) deliberately carries no target and continues to resolve through bibxml.

pulls.title: Compose with client instance identity across the family
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Integrates the client-instance-assertion profile and its AI-agent profile as identity substrate the family composes with. All references informative; the family does not depend on instance identity.

One normative fix standing on its own: `sub_profile` matching is list-aware (a matcher is satisfied when its value is among the actor's space-separated values), in the core's `allowed_delegates` and child-delegation's `allowed_child_actors`.

MAS: the join SHOULD bind (subject, client, **instance**) where instance identity is deployed, restoring per-instance granularity behind shared gateway `client_id`s; the Join Assertion names the instance and its instance-specific `cnf`; the shared-`client_id` residual now names instance assertions as the standard fix.

Composition notes across core delegation, child-delegation, runtime custody, authzen attribution, harness stop-propagation evidence, the security model (attester outside the trusted base: compromise forges who executed, never what was approved), and the architecture.

All 26 documents build clean with zero broken cross-references; core test vectors unchanged.

pulls.title: MAS-native expansion and child creation
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Closes the standalone binding's delegation hole: Mission Expansion and Child Mission creation ride the MAS's own submission surface. Carriage and binding only — every mechanism stays owned by its profile, with the tier rule holding (the experimental MAS normatively cites the stable profiles, never the reverse).

**Carriage**: `predecessor` (expansion) or `parent`+`child_actor` (child creation) as submission members, stripped before Intent validation so the closed-Intent rule is preserved; anti-oracle `not_found` vs section-scoped `conflict` error mapping; codes ride `mission_expansion_status`/`mission_denial_reason` as their profiles define.

**Binding**: authenticated client identity in place of token possession, with the delta stated plainly (proves the same registered client, not a held grant); Subject equality required for expansion; instance-grade binding and the Join Assertion as the strengtheners; residuals and mitigations in Security Considerations.

Approval of a successor or child is the MAS's native asynchronous approval event. Conformance gains the OPTIONAL capability; Limitations carries the binding caveat; the README standalone row updated.

All 26 documents build clean with zero broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Final naming pass before publication: controls, resource_issued, max_amount
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Pre-publication naming review of the core with family hindsight; three renames applied family-wide while renaming is still free.

- Mission Intent `context` → `controls` (generic name; collided with the AuthZEN evaluation `context` inside the same family; Intent-member sense only, every survivor justified)
- Common Constraints `issued_after`/`issued_before` → `resource_issued_after`/`resource_issued_before` ("issued" reads as token issuance in an OAuth document; rationale recorded in the definitions)
- `max_amount_usd` → currency-generic `max_amount` `{amount, currency}` mirroring `max_budget`'s shape (equal-currency subset/intersection, no conversion)

Full vector cascade recomputed with validated pipelines and re-derived from displayed inputs: the core authority_hash vector, `consent_rendering_hash`, approval-revision's echoed anchors, the mandate segments, the audit derivation digests, and the aauth blob's `authority_hash`/`s256`. The runtime/authzen digest vectors are untouched (their `amount_usd` values are deployment-defined action parameters, a different namespace, verified by what each digest covers).

All 26 documents build clean with zero broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Drop the mission prefix inside Mission-scoped objects
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Members of Mission-scoped objects no longer repeat the `mission` prefix; prefixed names belong to surfaces that reference a Mission from outside.

- Intent and record `mission_expiry` -> `expires_at` (pairs with `created_at`)
- record `mission_id` -> `id` (matches the `mission` claim)
- record `mission_intent` -> `intent` (symmetry with `authority_set`/`authority_hash`)

Kept prefixed, each bridging to the record's `expires_at` where it mirrors it: the `mission_intent` PAR parameter and AAuth proposal member, `mission_id` response/API/evidence keys, the Mandate's `mission_expiry` claim beside `mandate_exp`, and status/signals/management response members (the status response carries its own `expires_at` freshness member in the same object).

Recomputed vectors, validated against the published anchors before editing and re-derived from the files after: core `intent_hash`, `consent_rendering_hash`, AAuth `intent_hash` and `s256`. Both `authority_hash` values, Mandate segments, and audit/runtime/AuthZEN digests are unchanged. All 26 documents build clean with zero broken references.

pulls.title: Split report freshness from Mission expiry: fresh_until, summary id/expires_at
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Follow-up to #92. The status profile's `mission` object overloaded `expires_at` as report-freshness metadata while the record member of the same name now means Mission expiry. Renames the freshness bound to `fresh_until` and carries the Mission's expiry as `expires_at`, mirroring the record. Also renames Management summary members to record vocabulary (`id`, `expires_at`) and sweeps concept-level `mission_id` prose to "Mission Identifier". All seven drafts build clean with no broken references; no hash vectors affected.

pulls.title: Update links for repo rename to mission-bound-authorization
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The GitHub repo is renamed from draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission to mission-bound-authorization (old URLs redirect). Updates every draft's venue block and pinned editor's-copy reference targets, plus the README and LICENSE links. Draft document names are unchanged.

pulls.title: Rename origin to issuer across the suite
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Renames the Mission record and `mission` claim member `origin` to `issuer` and carries the same spelling on every surface (tokens, status, introspection, Mandate, SETs, evidence, MAS, AAuth). The core's naming note now states the convention: compact JWT names (`iss`, `exp`, `iat`) describe a signed artifact's envelope or identify a party in an {iss, sub} object; spelled-out names (`issuer`, `expires_at`, `created_at`) describe the Mission. The claim's `issuer` definition records the provenance rationale: a credential's `iss` names its minter, `mission.issuer` names the Mission's issuer, and the two deliberately differ cross-domain. Prose vocabulary follows (issuer AS, cross-issuer enumeration, {issuer}/missions/{id}). Recomputed vectors: the audit transition and derivation digests (JCS reorders issuer before mission_id) and the AAuth blob s256; old values reproduced first to validate the pipeline. All 26 drafts rebuilt with zero broken references.

pulls.title: Core -00 review feedback: widening, status hook, conformance floor
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Three additions from pre-publication review. (1) The subset rule now states the widening prohibition normatively in the body rather than only in Non-Goals: authority MUST NOT widen after approval on any dimension; broader authority requires a fresh approval (new Mission or expansion successor). (2) The revocation section states that token validity and Mission validity are distinct, with a SHOULD-level hook to introspection or the Status companion for consumers that rely on Mission state beyond token lifetime. (3) Conformance gains the anti-decorative-label floor: carrying a mission claim without the gates (anchored approval, subset rule, state-gated derivation) conforms to no role and MUST NOT be described as implementing this document. Items already covered normatively (durable-object framing, state-gated issuance, Authority Set, actor binding, derivation rules) are unchanged; authority_version was rejected as incompatible with record immutability.

pulls.title: Core publication-readiness review: normative, accuracy, and editorial fixes
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the multi-lens pre-publication review. Normative: the approval-rendering rule no longer mandates distinguishing AS-added broadening the subset rule forbids; the front-channel mission_intent reject rule is scoped so a stray parameter on a request_uri redemption is ignored, not rejected; the prompt-injection analysis no longer claims goal is inert (it shapes pre-approval derivation; purpose/success_criteria remain inert); the forward-compatibility rule no longer names the base mission claim as a state surface; an empty delegation narrowing now refuses with invalid_target (RFC 8693 2.2.2) instead of invalid_grant. Citation accuracy: the delegated-token client_id choice is stated as an explicit override of RFC 8693 4.3 / RFC 9068 2.2; RFC 7662's inactive-token rule is acknowledged as a SHOULD NOT deviation (three spots); the refresh-token MUST is described as strengthening RFC 9700 2.2.2; the RFC 9396 resource-parameter statement is scoped to the authorization request; ISO 4217 and MCP references added and cited. Mechanics: enforcement table and canonical-bytes blocks re-wrapped so no rendered line exceeds 72 chars (canonical bytes re-verified byte-identical to the printed hashes); explicit anchors for Applicability/Non-Goals; IANA field names aligned to Specification Document(s); metadata integrity note citing RFC 8414/9728; body pointer to the end-to-end appendix; DPoP proof added to the refresh-narrowing example. Plus ~25 editorial precision fixes (Approver vs principal, RFC 2119 keyword misuses, four extension points, garden-path rewrites).

pulls.title: Drop seriesinfo from suite-internal references
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Removes the 203 seriesinfo/Internet-Draft blocks from the hand-authored companion references across all 26 drafts, so rendered citations carry only the live github.io editor's-copy targets instead of datatracker -00 URLs that would 404 until the whole suite is submitted. External pins (draft-hardt-oauth-aauth-protocol-08, ISO 4217) are untouched. Pure deletion (406 lines); all 26 drafts rebuild with zero broken references.

pulls.title: Add workgroup header to OAuth drafts
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Adds workgroup: Web Authorization Protocol to the front matter of the 13 draft-mcguinness-oauth-* documents, so their first-page banner names the intended venue instead of the Network Working Group default. The substrate-neutral mission-* drafts are unchanged. Cosmetic; no effect on submission or adoption status.

pulls.title: Least exposure: Mission bounds inputs to reasoning, not only outputs
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Adds the input-side containment principle from review. Runtime gains a Least Exposure subsection after Credential Custody: a Mission-aware runtime SHOULD minimize exposure of prompts, retrieved documents, memory, tool catalogs, schemas, credentials, and downstream responses to what the active Mission needs, and where mediated custody is the deployment's declared control for an action class, credential material and the sender-constraint key MUST NOT be exposed to the agent. The subsection is explicit that this is not information-flow control and never replaces per-action authorization. The harness's Mission Mediation section gains the input-side dual (scope tool catalog, schemas, memory, retrieved content, and cached connections to the active Mission, tied to the existing taint policy and cache keys). Runtime's prompt-injection security considerations note the input-side complement without changing the stated limits, and the security model's lethal-trifecta row records the new mitigation. All three drafts rebuild with zero broken references.

pulls.title: Define Mission Receipt alongside Mission and Mandate
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: From review: the family's artifact triad is now formally named. The runtime profile, which owns per-action evidence, gains a Mission Receipt subsection: the portable, tamper-evident projection of a runtime enforcement evidence record (plus execution-outcome record for high-consequence actions). A Mission Receipt MUST identify the Mission (mission.id and mission.issuer, or a verifiable projection such as the cross-domain grant's mission claim) and SHOULD bind the policy decision, executor, custody boundary, downstream target, outcome, timestamps, and, where receipt chaining substitutes for a transparency feed, the predecessor digest. The portable schema stays deferred; the term and minimum binding are fixed now, mirroring the status-hook pattern. The audit profile disambiguates the SCITT Receipt (proof of inclusion) from the Mission Receipt and positions receipt chaining as the log-less alternative to the feed. The Mandate profile states the triad (Mission: governed task object; Mandate: portable evidence about the Mission; Mission Receipt: portable evidence about an action under it), and the architecture's Prove cluster lists all three. Name qualified as Mission Receipt to avoid colliding with SCITT's Receipt term. All four drafts rebuild with zero broken references.
