pulls.title: Toolchain integrity MVP: executor identity in the capability source binding
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: MVP of the toolchain-integrity review feedback. The AuthZEN capability source binding gains an OPTIONAL executor member: the executing component that serves the capability at request time (an MCP server instance), asserted by the authenticating PEP, recorded in Decision Evidence, and explicitly a request-time fact outside the derived authority. A new rule permits Resource policy to refuse a catalog-sourced action whose source_uri or executor is outside the deployment's trusted set, classified as resource_policy rather than capability_drift. The runtime's Authority bullet acknowledges executor identity as an input the binding MAY carry. Declined by design: a parallel toolchain object (tool_id/schema_hash/registry/egress facts already have stronger, hash-committed homes with MUST-refuse semantics), tool_version and tool_publisher (subsumed by source_digest and source_uri), and model_id/model_version (unverifiable without attestation, which the deferred list already names).

pulls.title: Attribution alignment: actor chain, on-behalf-of stance, receipt policy state
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Three small alignment additions from reviewing the family against external agentic-identity design principles. Architecture gains The Actor Chain: a single citable mapping of the roles one material action splits across (principal, approver, intent generator, authorizer, approved agent, executing delegate, credential holder, capability executor, downstream identity) to their family constructs and homes. The Security Model states the on-behalf-of stance explicitly: the Mission carries task pursuit, tokens present delegated authority without identity collapse (stable sub and client_id, act names who executed), personal sanction rides only on approval events, and the subset rule prevents authority laundering. The runtime's Mission Receipt SHOULD list now includes the policy state the decision was made under (PDP policy-view version and the Mission's policy_version), which the underlying evidence records already carry. No new mechanism; all three make existing coverage legible. All drafts rebuild with zero broken references; no new over-length lines.

pulls.title: Clear idnits line-length debt in companion drafts
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Brings every companion draft to the core's standard: zero rendered lines over 72 characters across all 26 documents. Architecture's Document Map table becomes grouped definition lists with every cell preserved verbatim (one redundant self-citation dropped). Status's state-machine table drops the unwrappable in-cell companion citations, which the prose below the table already carries. Canonical-bytes blocks in consent-evidence and orchestration re-wrapped at 66 columns with byte identity asserted and both hashes re-verified (consent_rendering_hash, unwind_plan_hash). JSON hash members wrapped onto continuation lines; audit's cbor-diag values and feed subject wrapped; signals' event-type URI shortened from .../secevent/mission/lifecycle-change to .../mission/lifecycle-change (an author-controlled identifier, changed in all seven occurrences including the namespace note); attenuation's jwk-thumbprint iss wrapped with an explicit display note; expansion's form-encoded elision tightened; runtime's parameter_digest vector split across lines. All 26 drafts rebuild with zero broken references.

pulls.title: Strengthen lethal-trifecta coverage: five coordinated additions
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Turns the family's scattered trifecta defenses into a coherent, claimable, enforceable posture. (A) The runtime gains a trifecta containment claim mirroring agent-compromise-resistant enforcement: least exposure applied, the harness taint rule enforced as MUST, and the external-communication classes fully mediated, published with the enforcement-scope statement and explicitly containment-not-immunity. (B) The AuthZEN profile gains an OPTIONAL taint context (tainted, granularity, source_class) so the harness's untrusted-content determination reaches the PDP: when present and true on an egress-class action, the PDP MUST deny or require action-bound approval, and records the taint context in Decision Evidence; the harness notes the routing option. (C) The harness scope statement MUST enumerate secondary egress channel classes (DNS, logs and error output, shared stores) per mediated class as mediated, excluded, or out of claim. (D) Metering gains max_egress_volume (bytes and/or messages over egress-class actions), pricing the within-scope-laundering residual with atomic PDP enforcement. (E) The architecture's deployment patterns gain the quarantine pattern: split trifecta legs across Missions so no single Mission holds untrusted input and an egress path at once. Security model updated (trifecta row and guarantees). Cross-references added between harness and AuthZEN. All six drafts rebuild clean: zero broken references, zero long lines.

pulls.title: Security model: availability axis completed, role-scoped trust anchors
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Two cross-cutting assumptions generalized from rules the suite already applies locally. The fail-closed bullet becomes the full availability axis: authority-relevant facts that cannot be established fail closed, while unavailable inert evidence (consent-evidence retrieval, the transparency feed, a Mandate) is recorded and never by itself grounds for refusal, with tampered inert evidence remaining an integrity failure owned by its defining profile. This closes a literal misreading under which evidence unreachability could become a denial lever, the exact failure mode our external review caught elsewhere. A new role-scoped trust anchors bullet generalizes what audit (authoritative producer per evidence type), the Mandate (issuer trust by local policy, never from a signed artifact naming an issuer), and cross-domain (never inferred from a self-named mission.issuer) each state locally. Mandate reference added. Builds clean.

pulls.title: Wire attested-instance, issuer-trust, and actor-provenance profiles into the companions
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Informative wiring of six sibling datatracker drafts into the gaps they address, core untouched (issue filed for its -01 item). Runtime: the deferred actor-provenance/attestation item now points at actor-proofs, actor-receipts, and ai-agent-instance as the specifying profiles whose results this profile consumes as token-derived facts; the Actor decision input notes that attested agent_instance_id/agent_model claims are attester-backed facts Resource policy MAY evaluate, the verifiable form of the model-provenance ask previously declined as self-asserted. AuthZEN: the executor member SHOULD carry the attested instance identifier where the executing component authenticates under client-instance-assertion or ai-agent-instance. Cross-domain: the identity-assertion trust framework and domain-authorized-issuer are named as concrete mechanisms for the issuer policy the trust-boundary section requires, and issuer-signed hop receipts (actor-receipts) as independently verifiable upstream provenance across the re-mint. Security model: the role-scoped trust anchors bullet cites the same two as publication/evaluation mechanisms. All references are informative bare bibxml entries; four drafts rebuild clean.

pulls.title: Resolve five review findings: status aud binding, completion ref, metering surfaces, build note
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: (1) Build: not a source defect; the template's draft-name sed expression exceeds BSD sed's per-expression buffer at this draft count. CI (GNU sed) is unaffected; README's Command Line Usage now documents the gnu-sed requirement for macOS. (2) Status: the dedicated Status operation now carries the Lifecycle endpoint's rule normatively: when the request omits audience, the AS MUST set aud to the authenticated requester's identifier, and state-only responses cache on (mission_id, requester identifier). (3) Completion: the Mission Status reference moves to normative, since the profile imposes MUST behavior on the Status operation and introspection projection when deployed. (4) Metering: abstract, IANA note, and README now say four bounds including max_egress_volume. (5) Metering gains its Mission Substrate section (identifier/issuer key the counters, Authority Set representation supplies call_class, anchor envelope commits the bounds, enforcement composes through the runtime binding), and the substrate requirements table gains the matching Consumption Metering row with a composition note. All four drafts rebuild clean.

pulls.title: Review round 2: mission_origin straggler, taint fence, anchor, build guard
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Second-round external review fixes. Harness: the Mission Binding member mission_origin, a compound-name straggler the issuer rename's patterns missed, becomes issuer in the definition, the queue-item example, and the evidence prose, aligning the flat binding key with the audit profile's flat-surface convention; no mission_origin remains anywhere in the suite. AuthZEN: the taint context example is now a valid JSON document (the bare object, introduced as the value of context.taint), matching the capability-source example style. Runtime: the Security Considerations heading gets an explicit anchor for the existing {{security-considerations}} reference. Makefile: a tracked GNU-sed guard turns the cryptic BSD-sed 'unterminated substitute pattern' failure into an actionable error with the brew remedy and a SKIP_SED_CHECK escape hatch. Core untouched per -00 policy: not-example.net filed as issue #110; the transaction-tokens -08 finding was a stale local bibxml cache (the reference is an unpinned bibxml entry), refreshed locally, resolved to -09 in the rendered text with no source change. Findings 3-6 of this round were already resolved in PR #109 (stale checkout). All rebuilt clean.

pulls.title: Full-family consistency and accuracy pass
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the four-lens family review (issuance-side, runtime-side, bindings/meta, external-spec) plus deterministic sweeps: 56 findings across 25 files. Normative: AuthZEN's Decision and Execution Evidence objects gain the members their consumers require (authorizing entry/digest, taint, PDP-populated policy_view_id) and a coordinated-companion extension seam replacing the absolute closure that made metering's measured_duration and taint recording unsatisfiable; the runtime defines the external-communication predicate the taint rule, trifecta containment, and metering already used; child-delegation stops forbidding core-defined subset forms; status gains the suspended-to-cascaded transition, renames its form parameter to mission_id per the core's convention, generalizes the signing-alg metadata member, and restructures its RFC 9701 example around token_introspection; shaping is aligned with the core's proposed_authority; the security model's assumptions count, goal-inertness split, compromise-resistance strengths, and adversary-table prose are corrected. Examples: the canonical Mission's anchors are no longer contradicted (fresh identifiers in consent-evidence/approval/revision), expansion's two successors are one, the child's hash and identifier are distinct and semantics-free, cross-domain's extended e2e Mission carries its own anchors end to end, journal-entries.write is irreversible_action everywhere (unwind_plan_hash regenerated and verified), evidence sequences are decoupled, and all companion epochs shifted 49 days so they match their RFC 3339 displays. External-spec: the Mandate's worked JWS re-encoded (the payload still carried pre-rename origin inside base64url; round-trip verified), SSF delivery discovery corrected to Transmitter Configuration Metadata, ID-JAG bearer nature and the refresh-token subject deviation owned explicitly, payload-preimage-content-type named fully, RFC 9701/RFC 8610 citations corrected. Meta: architecture checklist gains approval-fidelity, progressive is cited as itself, substrate routing and section claims match reality, MAS examples carry required response members, and the six self-described experimental drafts (signals, approval-revision, progressive, metering, orchestration, attenuation) now declare category exp. All 26 drafts rebuild clean: zero broken references, zero long lines; all computed vectors re-verified including the regenerated unwind plan.

pulls.title: Family critique edits: adoption ladder, maturity boundary, restatement removal
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Three improvements from the family critique. The architecture's deployment-bundle prose becomes the named, citable Adoption Ladder (Baseline Issuance, Enforced Agent, Governed Agent, Standalone Governance), each rung with its document set and the guarantee it buys, still explicitly guidance rather than a conformance class; the README's bundle table now points at it. The document map gains the maturity dependency rule: Standards-Track profiles never depend normatively on the six experimental ones, which extend only through the declared seams, and stabilization happens by reclassification. The AAuth binding's verbatim record-member enumeration, twice a rename casualty, becomes a pointer to the core's authoritative member list. Core-side critique items filed as issues #113 (rule dedup) and #114 (richer JCS vector). Both drafts rebuild clean.

pulls.title: Consent fatigue named as a residual with structural mitigations
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The family multiplies approval moments (approval event, expansion approvals, action-bound approvals, review queues) and never named the predictable human failure mode. The security model gains a fifth stated residual: a deployment that over-asks trains Approvers to rubber-stamp, a rubber-stamped approval carries the same commitments as a considered one, and the evidence layer proves what was shown and decided, not that attention was paid; the mitigations are structural (reserve action-bound approval, use progressive's ceiling), not evidentiary. Progressive's introduction now states this as its motivation: one considered consent to a ceiling replaces many hurried consents to increments. The runtime's action-bound approval gains the operative SHOULD reserving it for actions whose consequence genuinely warrants a human pause. Three cross-references added. All three drafts rebuild clean.

pulls.title: Solution-architecture resolutions: SoD exclusivity, aggregate bounds, scaling model, validity model
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Mechanism-level resolutions from the solution critique, all companion-homed per the maturity rule. Metering gains the exclusive controls member and its Exclusivity and Separation of Duty section: consented exclusivity groups over Authority Set selectors with a first-exercise-wins PDP latch, atomic with the permit, monotonic, denied as exclusivity_latched; this converts the quarantine pattern into consented enforceable structure and mechanically closes the authority-combination gap (read-sensitive-plus-egress) for deployments that consent to it. Metering also gains Aggregate Bounds: the same bound classes keyed by subject or client across Missions, closing the portfolio-risk gap, with the honest note that aggregate keying is its own consistency domain. Runtime gains Deployment Considerations stating the two scaling properties: token lifetime trades against the enforcement layer (extend lifetimes only where the PDP is the cutoff), and the consistency unit is the Mission (all strongly consistent state shards by Mission Identifier; only aggregate bounds cross the partition). Runtime custody notes the mediating PEP as the natural content-control (DLP) insertion point, and the containment claims gain the attestation upgrade path: scope statements MAY be bound to EAT evidence via the AI-agent-instance profile, distinguishing organizational from technical assertions. The architecture gains The Validity Model: the five horizons (token exp, Mission state, fresh_until, permit window, action-approval freshness), their setters, checkers, and compose-by-minimum rule. Core-side resolutions filed as issues #117 (promote exclusivity when stable), #118 (narrowing-mode RECOMMENDED), #119 (class-differentiated lifetimes), #120 (action-not-content applicability). All three drafts rebuild clean.

pulls.title: Assurance tiers: name the guarantee, force the proof obligation
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the solution critique's central recommendation. The architecture gains an Assurance Tiers section complementing the Adoption Ladder: the ladder says what to deploy, the tiers say what a deployment MAY claim and what it MUST show to claim it, because the family's strongest properties are deployment properties (PEP placement, freshness source, custody), not protocol properties. Five tiers, each resting on proof obligations defined normatively by the named profile: Mission-Bound Issuance (anchors, subset, state-gated issuance; task-bound authority, no per-action control), Mission Runtime-Enforced (per-action PEP/PDP, freshness source and published staleness bound, parameter binding; obligation is PEP-placement completeness and the declared bound), Agent-Compromise-Resistant (the runtime's four named conditions; a named high bar never implied by basic adoption), Trifecta-Contained (least exposure, taint rule as MUST, full egress mediation; composes with the prior tier), and MAS-Joined Governance (an explicitly weaker peer: no mission claim, no issuance gating, join proves subject/client not issuance-under-Mission, token-layer kill switch absent). Cross-cutting obligations (operation-profile normalization, evidence retention, audit registration) and the accountability-not-prevention nature of the evidence tiers are stated, and the attestation upgrade path lets a compromise-resistance or trifecta claim be technical rather than organizational. A tier is a verifiable claim stated in the enforcement-scope statement, not a label; a consumer treats an unproven tier as not claimed. This directly answers the critique's bottom line: keep forcing deployments to say exactly which guarantee they have earned. Architecture only, informative consolidation of existing normative obligations; rebuilds clean.

pulls.title: Solution-critique findings: static/dynamic tension, staleness floor guidance, honest anchor and narrowing limits
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the five findings from the mechanism critique. #1 (companion): the architecture's Mission Layers section now opens by naming the organizing tension, a Mission commits authority once but agent work is open-ended, so the Authority Set is a capability envelope not a task spec, and the six layers (constraint-bounding/subset, runtime enforcement, action-bound approval, progressive, metering, completion) are the levers that narrow the envelope-to-execution gap; this unifies the optional companions into one story. #4 (runtime): the staleness-bound rule now requires publishing the revocation-latency consequence (staleness bound + token lifetime) alongside the bound and gives non-normative per-class guidance (<=300 s for the high-consequence classes, larger values justified), anchoring the one number that determines the headline revocation property. #5 (runtime): agent-compromise-resistant enforcement now states the guarantee is the conjunction of custody, complete PEP placement, and correct classification, no stronger than the weakest, and that mediated custody alone prevents only off-path presentation, not in-scope misuse. #2 and #3 (security model, immediate): two named residuals added, anchor semantics (the two anchors commit objects not their relationship; authority_hash is an audit correlator not an enforcement input at a narrowed-token RS) and typed narrowing (the subset guarantee is defined only over mission_resource_access; opaque policy-language entries have no narrowing and are not delegated or projected). Core-side framing for #2 and #3 filed as issues #123 and #124. All three drafts rebuild clean.

pulls.title: Make guarantees mechanically claimable: scope statement, operation profile, tiers, tables
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the review's actionable deltas (much of it, the assurance tiers and per-class freshness numbers, shipped in PR #122/#125; the reviewer was on an older checkout). New this round:

Architecture: the assurance tiers gain the missing Mission-State-Aware Reliance tier (Status/introspection freshness with a published staleness bound, no per-action decision) between Issuance and Runtime-Enforced. A new Prevention, Detection, and Residue table states, per mechanism, what it prevents, detects, and does not solve, so no claim can be read as more than it is. A new Authority Derivation Boundary section states plainly that interoperability begins at the committed result, a Mission Intent has no portable semantics, audit detects what was derived not whether it was right, and high-risk deployments SHOULD publish a derivation policy identifier and test fixtures. The quarantine pattern is expanded into the named quarantined-ingestion / clean-output / bridge-evidence architecture, tied to metering's exclusivity control.

Runtime: the enforcement-scope declaration is now the named Enforcement Scope Statement (the artifact auditors, procurement, and interop key on), with credential custody mode and explicitly-excluded unmediated paths added as required fields. Parameter binding is anchored in a named Operation Profile shape consolidating the previously scattered per-operation requirements (action id, resource mapping, parameter schema, default insertion, canonicalization, parameter_digest fields, single-use/lease requirements, evidence fields) so implementers bind the same bytes. The staleness guidance becomes a per-class freshness-budget table.

MAS: the mode is labeled a partial-provision binding tying it to the substrate vocabulary and the MAS-Joined Governance tier, with a required deployment disclosure (what the join proves and does not, mapping granularity and instance identity, whether Join Assertions are required, which action paths runtime enforcement covers). Join-Assertion-SHOULD-for-high-consequence and the join-proves statement already existed.

Core framing for the derivation boundary filed as issue #126. All four drafts rebuild clean.

pulls.title: Fix CI build failures: dangling I-D references and invalid nested table
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The Update Editor's Copy workflow was failing (fresh .refcache, unlike local warm-cache builds). Two causes: (1) architecture cited I-D.draft-mcguinness-mission-metering and authority-server cited I-D.draft-mcguinness-mission-substrate, both added in recent edits, with no reference entry, so xml2rfc failed with IDREF-references-unknown-ID; local builds masked it because a warm .refcache auto-resolved the anchors. Added explicit informative entries in the family's hand-authored style. (2) the runtime freshness edit nested a markdown table plus a trailing paragraph inside a bulleted list item, producing an invalid v3 li (li has extra content t); the content duplicated an existing standalone freshness table, so the in-bullet table was removed and its distinct rows merged into the standalone one. Verified by a full clean-.refcache rebuild of all 26 drafts: zero errors, zero broken references, zero over-length lines.

pulls.title: Strengthen AAuth as a Mission substrate: resource-verifiable mode and runtime composition
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the AAuth-substrate review. The headline addition is a Mission Verification Modes section naming AAuth's resource-verifiability axis (which the OAuth binding lacks because its tokens always carry the authority payload): Reference-only (default; PS-gated, not independently verifiable), Credential-carried (token carries id/issuer/authority_hash), and Resource-verifiable (token carries authorization_details OR a signed Mandate, so a Resource or auditor can verify a token/request is within the approved Mission authority without trusting PS-private blob state); a deployment needing independent verification MUST serve the last. Federated mode is placed explicitly as at most Reference-only unless the AS carries the family members. Mission Status becomes a MUST (active freshness, published bound) for runtime-enforced high-consequence AAuth, deferring to the runtime profile's requirement. The PDP-shaped evaluation is tightened into runtime composition (PS as PDP implementing the runtime decision contract, not a duplicated profile) with the AAuth-specific request/reference/log mapping. The mission log gains required event types and commit ordering (proposal, clarification, approval render + authority_hash, token request, token issued, permission request + decision, lifecycle transition), grant-before-use, mappable to runtime decision/execution evidence. Directed per-audience Mission references are named as future work parallel to the issuance profile's audience-pairwise deferral. Two judgment calls against the literal proposal: composed with the runtime profile rather than duplicating its decision contract into AAuth, and mapped the runtime/high-consequence rungs onto the family assurance tiers rather than inventing parallel conformance names. Builds clean cold-cache. (Separately, the CI ghpages failure was a repo workflow-permission set to read, now write; the editor's-copy workflow is green again.)

pulls.title: Reposition MAS as the enterprise Mission control plane
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the MAS review. Reframes MAS from AS-optional workaround to the standalone Mission Issuer for an estate whose task governance spans many Authorization Servers, SaaS systems, APIs, and tool gateways, keeping the basic MAS conformance floor unchanged and deployable. Adds an Enterprise Mission Authority Profile above the floor (signed Status/Lifecycle; active freshness for high-consequence; Join Assertion required for high-consequence, mapping join as baseline and Join Assertion as enterprise mode; instance-bound (subject,client,instance) joins; runtime/AuthZEN enforcement with documented PEP coverage; audit evidence), mapped to the family's MAS-Joined Governance tier raised to Runtime-Enforced. Adds an Enterprise Mapping Contract (subject/client namespace mapping, delegate policy, instance support, mapping version, per-decision audit, fail-closed semantics) for the coarse/drifting-join risk, a Policy View Distribution role serving audience-scoped Authority Set / materialized policy views to PDPs (composing with runtime), and a non-normative Deployment section (Monday topology, six connector PEP patterns, five-phase progressive adoption). Composed with runtime/AuthZEN rather than duplicating; mapped to family tiers rather than a parallel taxonomy. Builds clean cold-cache.

pulls.title: System-guarantee conformance: deployment profile, token classes, derivation modes
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the architecture review's actionable P0/P1 items (MAS-as-bridge advice skipped per direction; much else already shipped in recent PRs). New: (P0) The Mission Deployment Profile, a system-level enforcement manifest composing the per-layer scope statements into one publishable object whose distinguishing field is residual_risks, so two deployments that both 'support Mission' expose their differing guarantees legibly; it ties to the assurance tiers. (P0) A derivation-mode portability table appended to the Authority Derivation Boundary (client-narrows = interoperable default; structured = profile-specific; free-text/LLM = local unless profiled). (P0) Commitment-anchor terminology in the anchor-envelope section: anchors prove what was committed, not derivation correctness, and a narrowed RS enforces what it receives. Token-class taxonomy (Mission-referenced / Mission-derived / Mission-bound), reserving 'Mission-bound' for the gated-subset-constrained-refresh-gated class. (P0) A per-assurance-tier revocation-latency view in the security model (issuance / cached-token / runtime-action / background-work columns). (P1) Runtime Negative Conformance Tests: the bypass scenarios (direct tool call, cached token post-revoke, stale resume, unauthorized sub-agent delegation, post-permit parameter change, secondary egress) with required outcomes. (P1) Evidence Minimization guidance in the audit privacy section (data minimization, log/audit separation, access control, retention, identifier strategy, export). Composed with existing profiles rather than duplicating; mapped to family tiers. Core-side items (reserve 'Mission-bound' terminology, expand the Common Constraints registry) filed as issues. All four drafts build clean cold-cache: zero broken references, zero long lines, no dangling references.

pulls.title: Unify the maturity ladder (D1) and split the Project verb (D2)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Reconciles the three inconsistent maturity namings (blog 4-rung ladder, draft Adoption Ladder, draft 6-tier Assurance Tiers) into one, and aligns the verb spine with the blog.

D1: The architecture's two overlapping sections (Adoption Ladder + Assurance Tiers) merge into one Maturity Ladder. Root fix: binding (OAuth AS / MAS / AAuth) is now an explicit orthogonal axis, so 'Standalone Governance' and 'MAS-Joined Governance' stop being rungs and become 'the Runtime-Enforced rung under the MAS binding.' Four canonical rungs, using the blog's public names: Baseline Issuance, Runtime-Enforced (labeled the Protocol MVP wedge), Governed Agent, High-Assurance Agent. Each rung carries both facets (document set + proof obligations). 'State-Aware Reliance' becomes a described half-step under Baseline rather than a rung; 'Agent-compromise-resistant enforcement' and 'trifecta containment' become the two named guarantees at the High-Assurance rung (claim names unchanged in the runtime profile). The 'Mission-Bound Issuance' alias is dropped in favor of 'Baseline Issuance,' resolving the internal inconsistency. The Deployment Profile's manifest field becomes maturity_rung; cross-references (MAS, AAuth, security model, README) updated to the single ladder and rung names.

D2: The verb spine gains Project as its own verb (Cross-Domain Projection) between Delegate and Prove, matching the blog's nine-verb spine; projection preserves authority across a trust boundary rather than narrowing it to a sub-actor, a distinct question from Delegate.

The blog is now the canonical naming source; no blog change needed for D1/D2. All four drafts build clean cold-cache: zero broken references, zero long lines, no dangling references, and no stale rung/anchor names remain anywhere in the suite.

pulls.title: Adopt Mission Assurance Levels vocabulary; delegated-authority-layer north star
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Replaces the Maturity Ladder / rungs metaphor with Mission Assurance Levels / levels, the security-standards-native register (NIST AAL/IAL/FAL, SLSA, Common Criteria all use named levels with proof obligations that a deployment claims). 'Levels' also fixes the semantic mismatch a ladder carried (no implication the top is mandatory) and recovers the family's own earlier 'assurance' word. The four descriptive names are unchanged (Baseline Issuance, Runtime-Enforced/Protocol MVP, Governed Agent, High-Assurance Agent); the section is now Mission Assurance Levels {#assurance-levels}, and MAS, AAuth, the security-model revocation table, and the README bundle table all speak 'levels.' The consent-evidence rendering-assurance rungs are a separate, intentional metaphor and are untouched.

Directional framing without business-speak: 'the levels build on one another; a deployment adopts the level its risk warrants and stops there,' expressing the crawl/walk/run goal in the family's own vocabulary rather than importing the phrase.

North star: a new paragraph in the architecture's Mission section states the family is the beginning of a delegated-authority layer, distinct from authentication and token issuance below it and from standing-entitlement governance, with the MAS as its binding-independent control plane and 'delegated authority management as a layer of its own' as the direction. MAS's progressive-adoption phases now frame the MAS as the enduring control plane of that layer, not a throwaway bridge. Product-category comparisons (IGA/PAM) deliberately stay blog-side, not in the draft. All four drafts build clean cold-cache.

pulls.title: Delegated-authority-layer thesis, Deployment Profile alignment, AAuth binding parity
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the alignment-review feedback (mechanisms were already in place from #130/#134/#135; this lands the framing and parity gaps) plus the directive that AAuth stand as an equal substrate across the suite.

**Layer thesis.** The architecture's Introduction now states the organizing thesis up front: the family defines a delegated-authority layer with OAuth 2.0, the standalone MAS, and AAuth as peer bindings into it, and the MAS as its estate control plane. The delegated-authority paragraph in The Mission gains the layer's operational surface (record and anchors, lifecycle and status, authority distribution, decision evidence, the Mandate as portable proof, the management plane), which also positions Management (operator surface of the layer) and Mandate inside the layer. README mirrors the thesis.

**Deployment Profile de-orphaned.** No draft outside the architecture referenced the Mission Deployment Profile. Runtime's Enforcement Scope Statement now states it earns the Runtime-Enforced level and feeds the Deployment Profile; the MAS Enterprise profile SHOULD publish one.

**MAS.** Dropped the 'flagship of the family' clause (binding parity), forward-positioned the mapping join as baseline vs Join Assertion as the enterprise mode at the join section lead, and tagged the Progressive Adoption phases with their assurance-level names (Baseline Issuance under the MAS binding, the state-aware half-step, Runtime-Enforced, Enterprise).

**AAuth parity sweep.** The AAuth Person Server now appears wherever the suite enumerates Mission Issuer bindings and had omitted it: security model (abstract, introduction, conventions, and a new AAuth Person Server trusted-base entry whose compromise is Mission Issuer plus token-issuer compromise), mandate (issuer terminology; substrate key discovery; 'any of the three bindings is verified identically'), audit (issuer key discovery via the PS jwks_uri; directed-reference future-work cite), runtime (Reference-only as the AAuth form of the externally established mode), shaping (PS accepts the same structured Mission Intent proposal), and README (three peer bindings on the first screen, 'flagship' wording retired, PS issuance gating noted).

**Declined by design** (conflicts with prior decisions): named Crawl/Walk/Run sections, re-splitting maturity from adoption (undoes D1), and four named MAS sub-profiles (recreates binding-specific levels D1 eliminated); the intent of each is carried in family vocabulary instead.

Also normalized seven hand-authored reference-entry titles to the actual draft titles ('An Architecture for Mission-Bound Authorization', 'Mission-Bound Authorization for AAuth'), including two pre-existing mismatches in metering and security-model. All nine touched drafts build clean cold-cache; suite-wide dangling-reference check passes.

pulls.title: Restructure the architecture document around its thesis
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Turns the architecture from an annotated index into an architecture, per the fresh review. No normative pointer was lost; every existing anchor is preserved.

**Promoted what was buried.** The capability-envelope tension, previously an unmarked lead-in at the two-thirds mark, is now an early top-level section (The Capability Envelope). Mission Assurance Levels is promoted from a subsection of Deployment Patterns to a top-level section. The abstract now leads with the delegated-authority-layer thesis and the envelope/gap tension instead of 'no single document shows how the pieces fit.' The duplicated layer thesis is deduplicated: the Introduction keeps a one-sentence pointer, The Mission holds the definitive statement.

**Added the missing spine.** New sections: Non-Goals (authority format, policy language, entitlement governance, agent framework, semantic derivation, agent trustworthiness); A Mission's Life, an end-to-end walkthrough of one Mission ('reconcile Q3 invoices') through propose, approve, issue, enforce, delegate, govern, stop, prove, with the family's first OAuth-binding sequence diagram; Mission Invariants, seven named laws (authority serves an approved task; only active permits; authority only narrows; revocation is possession-independent; attribution is carried, never inferred; enforcement fails closed / inert surfaces fail safe; anchors commit, not semantics), landing the pending C2 item in family vocabulary; and a planes view of the layer (control, enforcement, execution, evidence) beside the components diagram.

**Fixed the inversions and bugs.** Mission Layers is renamed The Mission Verbs (anchor unchanged), resolving the collision with the layer thesis. The Authority Derivation Boundary moves next to the substrate it qualifies. The quarantine pattern moves from Assurance Levels into Deployment Patterns where it belongs. Three inconsistencies repaired: the project verb added to the spine diagram (missed by D2), the AAuth Person Server added to the Mission Issuer diagram (missed by the parity sweep), and an AAuth chokepoint sentence added to Deployment Patterns. Requirements gains the six-criteria litmus lead (landing C4) and R18 for cross-domain projection, closing the gap where the Project verb had no requirement.

Cold-cache build clean; zero broken references; zero long lines; all diagrams verified in the rendered text.

pulls.title: Strengthen the README front door
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Revises the README per the fresh review.

**Correctness.** The adoption guidance misclassified three Standards-Track drafts (authority-server, aauth, substrate, all category: std) as 'Experimental, adopt for evaluation only,' contradicting both the document categories and the estate-control-plane framing. The ordering now has a dedicated 'By binding, where the estate calls for it' step; the experimental step is restricted to the six category: exp drafts (signals, approval-revision, progressive, metering, attenuation, orchestration), and deferred approval moves to 'advanced' (its unratified dependency stays flagged under Dependency stability).

**Vocabulary.** Retired 'adoption ladder,' 'tier,' 'Enforced bundle,' 'Governed bundle,' and 'Governed equivalents' for the Assurance Levels names, and fixed two pointers still describing the architecture's renamed 'layers' section (now the verbs).

**Front door.** The page now opens with the agent problem and the delegated-authority-layer thesis instead of repo boilerplate, followed by an at-a-glance block (26 drafts and their decomposition, the core on the datatracker, the four levels with the first three on ratified dependencies, the three peer bindings) and inline links to the Architecture and core. The standardization ask is promoted to its own section.

**Currency.** The Architecture catalog entry now describes the restructured document (capability envelope, a Mission's life, invariants, verbs, levels, Deployment Profile); the MAS entry gains the Enterprise Mission Authority Profile, policy-view distribution, topology, and connector patterns; the runtime entry names its two High-Assurance claims and the Mission Receipt; metering gains the exclusivity control. The Mission Deployment Profile is introduced in 'What to deploy,' an estate path joins the role-based reading guide, and the approve-and-record diagram gets the AAuth Person Server as a proper third box instead of a parenthetical.

**Scannability.** The 26 four-bullet link blocks are compressed to one line per draft, cutting about 80 lines with no information loss.

README-only change; the build workflow ignores it by paths-ignore.

pulls.title: Strengthen Mission Intent Shaping: re-shaping loop, child Intent seam, suite ties
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
## What

The shaping profile had drifted behind the suite's newer connective tissue: it missed the delegated-authority-layer thesis and Deployment Profile passes its siblings received, and two companions (deferred approval, approval revision) delegate a re-proposal loop to the shaper that the shaping draft never described. This pass closes those gaps without changing the shaper's role contract (proposes only; no wire protocol; no conformance class).

## Changes

- **Re-Shaping (new section).** Names the propose, learn what was refused, re-propose loop the approval and approval-revision profiles already assign to the shaper. Defines the three refusal surfaces (`invalid_authorization_details` derivation refusal, deferred `access_denied`, `rejected_scope`/`rejected_authorization_details` revision), requires fresh Shaping Evidence per re-proposal with the refusal input recorded, and adds the narrowing-only guard: no re-encoding refused authority in new vocabulary, no iterative resubmission to probe issuer policy.
- **Child Mission Intents.** The child-delegation profile defines a Child Mission Intent but no document assigned its construction. The Delegation subsection now claims it as a shaping act with the Parent Mission's Authority Set as the ceiling (matching the strict-subset rule).
- **What Shaping Adds and Does Not (new section).** The limits pattern the audit and security-model companions already use: four things a sound shaper contributes, four it cannot (authority, consent, enforcement, assurance), explicit positioning against the Mission Assurance Levels (on no rung, required by none, raises the input at every level), and a recommendation to document the shaping posture in the Mission Deployment Profile.
- **Evidence ties.** Shaping Evidence is now tied to the architecture's actor chain (the intent-generator role), and the consent-disclosure object's `shaping_evidence_hash` member is cited as the standard carrier for the evidence commitment.
- **Security.** Silent-broadening section gains the loop variant: widening by retry, with chained Shaping Evidence as the detection record.
- **Introduction/abstract.** Delegated-authority-layer thesis parity with the sibling companions; the loop is named up front.

Five informative references added (architecture, approval, approval-revision, child-delegation, consent-evidence). No normative changes to any other document.

## Verification

`make draft-mcguinness-mission-shaping.txt` builds clean (kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc OK); all cross-references resolve to section numbers in the rendered text.

pulls.title: Strengthen the Mission Security Model: full-suite coverage, named guarantees
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
## What this does

Revises `draft-mcguinness-mission-security-model` so the consolidated view actually covers the current suite and states its guarantees with the rigor the rest of the family now has. Builds clean (`kramdown-rfc` + `xml2rfc-txt`).

### Full-suite coverage
The model predated six profiles and never mentioned them:
- **Intent Shaping**: the shaper joins the agent on the untrusted side; a compromised shaper can propose a hostile Mission but cannot approve one.
- **Consumption Metering**: new trusted-base entry (compromise voids the volume bound, not the authority bound; false exhaustion is denial of service) plus the consent-integrity rule folded into the fail-closed assumption.
- **Mission Management**: new trusted-base entry; compromise cannot mint authority but reaches the whole fleet (bulk revoke/suspend is fleet-wide stoppage, bulk resume re-enables suspended authority, enumeration yields existence knowledge).
- **Child Delegation**: adversary row for observed fan-out, cascade note in the revocation section, and the offline-breadth residual now states the availability-vs-observation trade against offline attenuation.
- **Approval Revision**: covered in the escalation-path adversary row (revisions can only narrow).
- **Substrate Requirements**: cited from the Mission Substrate section for binding authors.

### Named guarantees with dependencies
The guarantees section is now a list of named claims (Bounded authority, Kill switch, Per-action enforcement, Bounded consumption, Agent-compromise-resistant enforcement, Trifecta containment, Attributable action), each tied to the Mission Assurance Level where it is earned and the components that must be intact for it to hold. The earned-not-implied caveat (SHOULD-vs-MUST split in the base profiles) is preserved verbatim.

### Adversary model
New rows: escalation through the request path (residual: rubber-stamping), in-scope volume abuse (residual without metering: bounded only by expiry), Child Mission fan-out, operator-surface abuse. The exfiltration residual now points at the quarantine pattern as the structural mitigation. New standalone residual: **in-scope volume** (eight total).

### Alignment
- Revocation table uses the canonical Mission Assurance Level names, splits Governed Agent from High-Assurance Agent, and names the freshness half-step per the architecture doc.
- The Documenting section now routes the trusted-base statement into the Mission Deployment Profile, with the named guarantees and residuals as the vocabulary for its claims and `residual_risks`.
- Retention adds Shaping Evidence and metering records; Privacy adds metering-as-activity-record and management-as-existence-oracle.

All existing threat notes and clauses are preserved; the passages other drafts cite (no-unmediated-path, availability trade, consent fatigue, anchor semantics, issuer-compromise semantics) are intact.

pulls.title: Strengthen the cross-domain projection profile
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Revisits draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-cross-domain to make it a stronger document: it now states its invariants normatively, gathers its implicit trust prerequisites, pins failure semantics, and honors every duty the base profile and companions delegate to it. Rigor, not length: net +167 lines including two consolidations.

## What changed

**Boundary invariants, stated instead of implied**
- New "What Crosses the Trust Boundary" section: three values cross unchanged (`mission.id`, `mission.issuer`, `authority_hash`), two things cross scoped (audience-scoped entries, Subject), everything else stays home. Absorbs Audience-Scoped Authority as a subsection.
- Introduction and abstract now carry the three design properties the architecture doc's R18 attributes to this profile: the Mission does not cross, authority only narrows, and honoring needs no session with the issuer (previously stated only in the non-normative appendix).

**Pre-Established Trust (new)**
- Gathers the bilateral configuration that was scattered as "local policy": issuer trust, resource-to-AS mapping, grant profile + PoP mechanism, client registration, subject resolution. Notes where a Mission Deployment Profile records the projection posture and residual revocation risk.

**Explicit typing (new MUSTs)**
- The grant MUST carry its profile's JWT `typ` (`oauth-id-jag+jwt` for ID-JAG, verified against draft -04) and the Resource AS MUST validate it. This completes the "mechanical separator" pairing the Mission Mandate draft already leans on.

**Error Responses (new)**
- Consolidated tables binding standard OAuth/RFC 7523/RFC 8693 codes to both surfaces (issuance and redemption), Status-draft style. No new codes.

**Duties the base delegates, now honored**
- Derivation accounting: cross-domain issuance counts once against the Mission's derivation budget; local tokens are uncounted and locally bounded.
- AS Metadata section: `grant_types_supported` signals per the base's discovery model; no new parameters.

**Fixes and tightening**
- Moved the derivation-record SHOULD out of the non-normative appendix into Validation at the Resource AS (it was a normative requirement stranded in an appendix that declares it adds none).
- Consolidated the two overlapping refresh-token-rationale paragraphs; all normative clauses preserved.
- Conformance recast as role-scoped MUST lists (attenuation style), referencing the new sections.
- Family vocabulary ties: assurance-level orthogonality (Status section), state-lease framing in revocation (runtime vocabulary), sharper Status/Signals split.
- Drive-by: AAuth draft cited this document with the wrong title ("Cross-Domain Authorization"); fixed to "Cross-Domain Projection".

Both touched drafts build clean (`make ...txt`, kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc OK); no unresolved references in the rendered text.

pulls.title: Strengthen the substrate requirements doc
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Strengthens draft-mcguinness-mission-substrate.md: rigor, not length (net +53 lines, all of it checkability).

## What changed

- **Abstract and introduction lead with the doc's job.** The abstract now opens with why substrate-neutral profiles port and what this document states, instead of an inventory of the existing bindings. The intro is three shorter paragraphs: the model and its bindings, the profile interface, and this document's role as the Binding Checklist's normative home.
- **The Mission record is now a stated requirement** (section retitled "Mission Record, Identifier, and Issuer"): durable, immutable after creation except state, retained per the audit horizon. The Mandate's committed members always assumed this; the doc never required it of a new binding.
- **Anchor computation is pinned for interop.** The envelope's three fields (`typ`, `iss`, `value`) are named, and the requirement states the computation is the core's byte for byte: the core's test vectors verify a binding's implementation, with only the envelope `iss` the binding's own.
- **The approval steps gain the core's re-consent rule**: if the derived Authority Set changes between rendering and consent, recompute the anchors and do not create the Mission without consent to the changed set.
- **Conformance is now checkable.** The trailing "SHOULD publish a Mission Substrate section" becomes a required **Mission Substrate Statement** with seven enumerated contents (provision level and join, identifier mapping, state sources and staleness bounds, envelope `iss` and native commitments, key discovery, audit horizon, approval surface), defined in terminology and anchored at `{#statement}`.
- **New security consideration: State Source Fidelity.** A staleness bound the source cannot meet turns the runtime's fail-closed freshness rules into fail-open in effect; the bound is a fidelity claim of the same kind as the join's assurance statement.
- **Alignment fix:** "post-terminal period" now matches the core's defining words, "post-expiry period" (the core governs where wording appears to differ).

## What did not change

The Status guarantee holds: nothing changes for the existing bindings and profiles, the composition table is untouched (validated against every profile's own Mission Substrate statement), and every requirement still points at the core section that owns its definition.

Builds clean: kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc-txt both pass.

pulls.title: Strengthen the runtime profile: contract first, enforcement invariants, named claims, heading register
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The runtime profile is the family's central enforcement document (the Runtime-Enforced level is the Protocol MVP, and its two named claims set the High-Assurance Agent bar), but it read as an appendix to the core: the abstract and introduction defined it by the issuance profile's out-of-scope list, the two named claims were buried in unrelated sections, and the heading register did not match the core or the architecture. This revision gives it the same treatment the architecture and README received: thesis first, a citable spine, no content changes.

## What changed

**Abstract.** Leads with what the document does (the point-of-use permit and what it evaluates) instead of the issuance profile's limitation, and now names both claims; trifecta containment was previously missing from the abstract.

**Introduction.** Thesis first (a Mission never consulted at the point of use is ambient authority), then three new subsections:

- **The Runtime Contract** (`{#runtime-contract}`): the one-sentence contract the rest of the document elaborates, formally stated in its home document for the first time (the term already circulates in the README diagram and the AuthZEN profile).
- **Enforcement Invariants** (`{#enforcement-invariants}`): seven named invariants (gated at the point of use, enforced at the last controllable boundary, bound to the bytes, fresh or refused, fail closed, evidenced, never widening), each pointing at its normative home section, mirroring the architecture's seven Mission invariants.
- **Invariants, Not a Wire Protocol**: the existing invariants-vs-binding boundary paragraphs, now under their own heading.

The four-item out-of-scope mapping moved into *Relationship to the Issuance Profile*, framed as the exact seam between the two documents.

**Named Enforcement Claims** (new Section 12, `{#named-claims}`): groups agent-compromise-resistant enforcement (previously under the runtime model) and trifecta containment (previously under the decision) as the profile's composite claims, with a lead paragraph tying them to the Enforcement Scope Statement, the negative tests, and the High-Assurance Agent level. Negative Conformance Tests now directly follows the claims it demonstrates, then Deployment Considerations. Both claim sections moved verbatim.

**Heading register.** All headings normalized to the core/architecture Title Case style; "The decision" is now "The Runtime Decision". Every anchor is unchanged, and no other draft references this document by section number.

**Terminology.** Two pointer entries added: *High-consequence classes* and *Established Mission* (both defined normatively in their existing sections; the entries only point).

## What did not change

No normative clause was added, dropped, or reworded outside the introduction/abstract framing; the moved sections are byte-identical. All section anchors are preserved. `make draft-mcguinness-mission-runtime.txt` builds clean (kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc both OK).

pulls.title: Strengthen the AuthZEN profile: response structure, Refusal Record, failure coverage, conformance rigor
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Revisits draft-mcguinness-mission-authzen with rigor-focused strengthening. All existing normative clauses are preserved (verified programmatically against the old section line by line); the additions pin interop the runtime profile implies but the binding never fixed on the wire.

## Structure

- **New `PDP Response` section**, paired with `PDP Request`. Permit response shape, split-topology permit binding, and decision-id propagation were buried under the "Runtime Denial Classification" H1; they now live in a proper response section (context members, permit shape, denial shape, denial classification, requestable denials, coverage table, permit binding, id propagation). Document order is now Request, Response, then the durable evidence objects. The `runtime-denial-classification` anchor stays on the enum, so every cross-reference keeps resolving.
- **Abstract rewritten** hook-first; the run-on delta list is now one compact sentence per delta.

## New rigor

- **Refusal Record is now a real object.** The pre-decision refusal record was prose only; it now has a full member list (`refusal_id`, verified-facts-only members, conditional `sequence` continuing the Mission's record stream), a REQUIRED `evidence_envelope` with its own registered media type (`application/mission-refusal-record+json`, closing the typ cross-use gap on exactly the fail-closed paths), and a worked PDP-outage example.
- **Failure-condition coverage table.** Every failure condition in the runtime profile's failure-modes table now maps to exactly one wire carrier: Refusal Record, PDP `denial_reason`, or Execution Evidence `error`. Implementations can check coverage instead of inferring it.
- **Anti-downgrade rule.** A PDP serving mixed traffic MUST refuse an in-scope request lacking `context.mission` rather than evaluate it as a plain AuthZEN request, plus a Security Considerations subsection on why (a generic allow rule silently standing in for Mission evaluation).
- **Two wire rules the runtime implies, now pinned:** `permit_expires_at` MUST NOT outlive the relied-on Mission state freshness (in particular a supplied `mission_status_expires_at`), and a PEP whose own classification is stricter than the returned `action_class` MUST enforce the stricter class's permit controls.
- **Conformance rebuilt by role** (PEP, PDP, executor, audit consumer), folding in already-normative obligations that were missing from the checklists (permit-lease enforcement, permit-cache keying, envelope verification). This also fixes a misassignment: the old PEP checklist required the PEP to emit Decision Evidence, which the PDP emits.

## Fixes

- The stale_state denial example reused the permit example's `decision_id`, contradicting the one-id-per-decision model; it now has its own.
- README catalog entry refreshed to match.

Build validated: `make draft-mcguinness-mission-authzen.txt` renders clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc OK); all internal anchors resolve.

pulls.title: Resolve the fresh full-family review (solution hardening + consistency sweep)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the five-reviewer fresh read of all 26 drafts: roughly 110 edits across 25 documents plus README. The frozen core is untouched; its items are filed as issues #145-#151. Every edit is capability-preserving (guidance, issuer-side shaping, or a one-rule addition using mechanisms the family already defines).

**Named-claim hardening (runtime, authzen, harness, metering).** Non-idempotent high-consequence operations now require an Operation Profile idempotency key with PDP refusal across re-permits, closing duplicate execution after a PEP crash. PDP-enforced taint fails closed (taint_context_missing) when the harness omits the taint member. Default-taint polarity in tainted sessions closes the paraphrase-sheds-taint hole and is a stated condition of trifecta containment. The agent-compromise-resistant claim gains a fifth condition (agent-isolated approval rendering) plus a digest-list batch-approval provision. High-consequence classes require an execution lease or published maximum duration, and the published revocation-latency formula is corrected in runtime and architecture. The exclusivity latch must live in one strongly consistent per-Mission domain, exempt from metering's multi-PDP relaxations, and unsettled reservations stay charged until reconciled.

**Delegation seams (MAS, substrate, child-delegation, attenuation).** The MAS join narrows delegates to the delegable subset, giving per-entry delegation policy an enforcement locus in the standalone binding; the substrate assigns that duty to whichever surface admits a delegate. Attenuation roots are shaped at issuance so non-delegable entries never ride re-delegable roots. The child derivation gate checks the full ancestor chain, closing the mid-cascade grandchild window; the issuer commits terminal cascade transitions regardless of cascade mode; cascade latency is stated; parent entry discharge propagates to child entries.

**Progressive ceiling.** The drawdown rate bound is scoped to the expansion chain (it was reset by the successor it was meant to bound) and made auditable via a cumulative chain count; successors must carry the ceiling and policy unchanged or narrowed, with anything else requiring fresh human approval; discharged entries cannot be resurrected by policy.

**Binding-abstraction gaps.** The substrate now requires an authenticated, possession-independent revocation capability (previously a conformant binding could omit the kill switch) and Intent-bounded derivation. Baseline Issuance is defined binding-neutrally, with the architecture, MAS, security model, and README all stating what it delivers under a partial-provision binding. max_derivations gets defined semantics at a MAS (refuse or mark non-binding) and at the AAuth PS (issuance + federation events count). AAuth's provision claim is scoped per mode and the PS delivery check covers exp.

**Approval-surface abuse.** Deferred approval gains anti-prompt-bombing bounds; expansion presentations count toward anomaly detection with a MUST rate limit for non-sender-constrained tokens, and expansion discloses live children before superseding them; management's bulk resume requires an elevated grant; the status lifecycle endpoint gets one unified idempotency rule (mirrored by management) and a caller-scoped dedup key.

**Evidence and audit.** The lifecycle-transition commitment is salted (vector recomputed; old digest independently reproduced first, new digest independently verified). Child Evidence gets canonical bytes and registers on the parent's feed, making the delegation tree enumerable from the log. Unwind plans are committed at dispatch. Denied high-consequence attempts retain parameters for forensics. Cross-domain coexists with plain ID-JAG, wires the Mandate in for partner-side subset verification, and states derivation-budget sizing. Mandate examples now match the recorded set their anchor commits (payload re-encoded); child-delegation and attenuation examples diverge their anchors with the cross-domain note pattern.

**Consistency sweep.** All retired vocabulary is gone (tier, rung, bundles); the AAuth PS appears in every binding enumeration (status, signals, approval, consent-evidence, README); the cross-domain intro matches the Project verb; shaping's proposed-authority seam has one home; 'shaping ceiling' is disambiguated from progressive's consented ceiling; reference titles corrected both directions between authzen and harness; the security model covers policy views, Join Assertions, the exclusivity latch, and the Mission Receipt. Two pre-existing nits fixed in passing: the security model's over-wide adversary table is now a definition list (109 over-length lines to zero) and attenuation's display-wrapped decoded token no longer claims to be validatable JSON.

Verification: frozen core has zero diff; no em-dashes added; suite-wide dangling-reference check passes; all 25 changed drafts build clean cold-cache with zero broken references and zero over-length lines; the audit vector and mandate JWS blocks were recomputed and independently re-verified.

pulls.title: Answer the latency-tax, semantic-gap, and double-ledger critiques
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Addresses three items of external feedback. All additive prose; no new mechanisms, no capability changes, core untouched.

**Machine-speed latency.** The machinery existed but was scattered across five places; runtime's Deployment Considerations now carries a third scaling property, 'Decision latency is budgeted by class,' consolidating it: classification confines the synchronous gate to the classes whose consequences warrant it; an embedded or colocated PDP over a materialized policy view makes the common-case decision local, its network cost paid per freshness window; permit windows, batch evaluation, and metering leases amortize; offline attenuation removes the issuer from fan-out entirely, and its Experimental status tracks its substrate's maturity, not the capability's importance (stated in attenuation's introduction too, with issuer-mediated child delegation named as the stable path). AuthZEN explicitly blesses the embedded-PDP topology; the architecture's Deployment Patterns echoes the budget in three sentences.

**Semantic vs. structural enforcement.** The schema-compliant-but-harmful scenario (approved send_email carrying injected content) was already the target of taint, trifecta containment, quarantine, exclusivity, and egress-volume bounds, but the strategy was never named. The architecture's Capability Envelope and runtime's What This Layer Adds now state it: the family converts semantic risk into structural signals (provenance, composition, enumeration and volume, separation of duty, re-consent) rather than asking the PDP to judge meaning; and content evaluators (DLP, LLM-based policy) get a defined seam, composing as Resource policy whose verdicts only narrow and whose latency belongs to the invoking action class.

**Double ledger.** The harness introduction now defends the separation by trust domain, a kill switch cannot live inside the thing it kills, and states that the two ledgers share exactly one key (the Mission Binding reference) with every harness obligation a consume-and-gate hook, never a copy. A new informative appendix, Mapping to Durable-Execution Engines, maps generic engine primitives (run handle, activity boundary, retry queue, resume, child-run spawn, human-in-the-loop wait, compensation, event history) onto the specific harness, runtime, and orchestration obligations each discharges.

All five drafts build clean cold-cache; zero broken references, zero over-length lines, no em-dashes.

pulls.title: Semantic Evaluators, Edge Validation, and the ledger ownership table
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Second round of the latency/semantics/ledger feedback; the increments beyond #153, all guidance on existing mechanisms, core untouched.

**Semantic Evaluators (runtime, new subsection under The Runtime Decision).** Makes the one-sentence seam from #153 a defined composition contract: the evaluator's rubric is the committed Mission Intent, tamper-evident via intent_hash (the reviewer's pairing idea, translated into family terms); the verdict enters as Resource policy in deployment-defined context and can deny or route to the existing step-up affordances (action-bound human approval, the reviewer's 'Step-Up Human Challenge', or step_up_required), never widen or substitute for a structural check; verdicts are recorded in decision evidence; latency belongs to the invoking class. Plus the honesty the ask was missing: an LLM evaluator reads the adversarial content it judges and is itself injectable, so it augments the structural signals and never replaces them, with reliance declared in the Enforcement Scope Statement. Standardizing the evaluator itself is declined, the derivation-boundary stance applied at runtime.

**Edge Validation (attenuation, new subsection).** The end-to-end deployment pattern the reviewer sketched, built from mechanisms the profile already defines: root minted at Mission start alongside issuance; children minted offline; the fronting PEP (harness, edge gateway, sidecar) validates the chain locally at memory speed (signature chain, monotonicity, aud/exp nesting, proof of possession); the kill switch is the one non-local fact, riding cached state within the published staleness bound for ordinary classes while the high-consequence classes and state transitions stay synchronous. Macaroon-style framing folded into prose, no citation, per house style.

**Ledger ownership table (harness appendix).** The engine-mapping appendix now opens with the who-owns-what split the reviewer asked for: execution ledger (step execution/retries, memory and context, spawning mechanics, tool caches, scheduling) versus authorization ledger (lifecycle and expiry, the committed record and anchors, child-Mission adjudication, credential custody and counters, permits and evidence). It also corrects the reviewer's own example: consumption counters live with the enforcement plane, not the Authorization Server proper.

All three drafts build clean cold-cache; zero broken references, zero over-length lines.

pulls.title: Mission Issuance Grant: the issuance join between MAS and the AS token plane
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds the 27th draft, draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-issuance-grant (Standards Track, oauth-prefixed per the naming boundary since it extends the AS token endpoint), filling the integration gap between the MAS's PDP-only runtime join and full native Mission-awareness.

**The mechanism.** A Mission Issuance Grant is a short-lived (300 s cap), one-time (jti), audience-bound, client-bound JWT with its own typ (mission-issuance-grant+jwt), minted by the standalone Mission Issuer only while the Mission is active, carrying the mission claim object, a mission_expiry cap, and the derived authorization_details subset scoped to the consuming AS. The AS redeems it as an RFC 7523 JWT authorization grant (optional PAR carriage for code-flow deployments, with a MUST NOT re-prompt rule) and mints Mission-bound tokens: claim carried verbatim, subset-bounded, capped at Mission expiry, refresh gated on Mission state within a published staleness bound or no refresh tokens at all. Every path to new authority re-enters a Mission-state gate, restoring the issuance-gate kill switch the MAS-only mode structurally lacks.

**Design points.** Each grant is a derivation event, giving controls.max_derivations a binding locus under the standalone binding for the first time. The Mandate stays evidence (typ discipline, MUST NOT redeem); the cross-domain grant remains the foreign-domain counterpart (relationship section distinguishes bilateral same-estate trust from federated projection). Tokens minted under the profile compose credential-carried at the PDP, ending the joins-prove-belonging-not-issuance caveat for those tokens; ordinary tokens still use the runtime join. Security considerations cover grant theft (defense in depth plus optional cnf), the MAS-compromise blast radius now reaching estate issuance (with minting-vs-redemption log reconciliation), trust inversion bounds, three-artifact type confusion, and the revocation-latency statement. IANA registers the media type and the MAS metadata endpoint parameter.

**Family integration.** MAS: abstract note, Limitations closure paragraph, the max_derivations locus, and a new adoption phase 5 (issuance join) before native Mission-awareness. Architecture: composite provision in the Mission-Bound Credential primitive, Baseline Issuance reachable under the standalone binding via the join, the middle path named in Deployment Patterns, document-map entry. Substrate: composite-provision sentence in the credential requirement. Security model: MAS-compromise extension and the further-issuance column returning at consuming ASs. README: 27 drafts, catalog entry, binding-paragraph and adoption-order mentions.

All five touched drafts plus the new one build clean cold-cache; suite dangling-reference check passes; zero broken references; zero over-length lines; frozen core untouched.

pulls.title: mission_expiry: RFC 3339 mirror of expires_at, per the Mandate precedent
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The issuance grant defined mission_expiry as NumericDate, deviating from the Mandate, which established the member as an RFC 3339 date-time mirroring the Mission record's expires_at, and from the core's rule that Mission-record facts are spelled identically on every surface while exp/iat describe a signed artifact's own envelope. Aligns the claim definition (with a Mandate cross-reference), fixes the example value, and adds the RFC 3339 normative reference. Builds clean.

pulls.title: Implement the mission claim's expires_at member ahead of core -01
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Implements issue #157's recommendation now, on the surfaces the companions own, leaving the frozen core untouched; -01 later canonicalizes the member into the core's claim definition.

**Defining home: the issuance grant.** New subsection 'The expires_at Claim Member' defines it once for the family: an RFC 3339 date-time mirroring the record's expires_at (spelled identically per the core's record-fact naming rule), a bounding and audit commitment with no liveness (expiry says nothing about revocation; only active permits reliance), giving consumers the Mission horizon for planning, a deterministic ceiling on offline paths (immutable, so a credential-carried copy is always safe), and a cap check. Any Mission Issuer under any binding MAY include it. Since the grant is unpublished, the member moved INSIDE the grant's mission object and the top-level mission_expiry claim is deleted outright, no deprecation debt: the grant's mission object rides verbatim onto issued tokens, so issuance-join tokens carry it automatically. Redemption, conformance, security-considerations, and example text updated accordingly.

**Minting**: the AAuth PS SHOULD carry the member on its auth tokens (normative reference added). **Consumption**: runtime's token-validation section lets the PEP/PDP refuse past-ceiling actions credential-side with the guardrail restated; attenuation's edge-validation pattern adds the Mission ceiling to the offline-checkable list.

All four drafts build clean cold-cache; zero broken refs; zero over-length lines; mission_expiry has zero remaining occurrences.

pulls.title: Open-world discovery: the encounter router, R3 composition, resource-family ceilings
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Addresses the ontology gap between the family's OAuth-inherited model (client-proposed authority enumerated at approval) and open-world agent operation, where resources are discovered at encounter time and, under AAuth's exploratory Rich Resource Requests companion (draft-hardt-aauth-r3), the ontology inverts outright: the resource declares its own operations, meaning, and consequences, content-addressed by r3_s256.

**Architecture (Capability Envelope).** New closing passage naming the gap and the encounter router: a resource within a pre-consented ceiling binds by progressive drawdown; a catalog tool binds through the capability-source binding; a partner-domain resource binds through projection; everything else requires fresh approval. Where the resource self-declares, its declaration digest becomes a third commitment beside intent_hash (what the client asked) and authority_hash (what was consented): what the resource claimed to be when authority bound to it.

**AAuth binding.** New informative Resource-Declared Semantics section composing R3 with the binding's governance: the PS gates every token, so encounter-time token issuance is the natural adjudication point (against the Authority Set or a progressive ceiling); the resource-authored R3 display composes into approval and expansion disclosures, which Consent Evidence then commits; r3_s256 rides into evidence. Explicit maturity boundary: R3 is exploratory, the section is composition, not a normative dependency, and conformance does not require it.

**Progressive.** Ceiling entries MAY name resource families under the subset rule's existing resource-narrowing semantics, so an Approver can consent once to a class of resources the agent will only meet during execution; and an encounter-triggered drawdown MUST evaluate the resource's self-declaration and record its digest as resource_declaration_digest in the audit record.

**AuthZEN.** The coordinated-extension example list names resource_declaration_digest so the evidence member has a sanctioned home.

Frozen core untouched; the R3 dependency is informative-only per the family's maturity rule; all four drafts build clean cold-cache.

pulls.title: Mission Open-World Discovery: the encounter as a governed operation
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds the 28th draft, draft-mcguinness-mission-discovery (Experimental, substrate-neutral naming), turning the open-world encounter from the router narrative of #159 into a defined contract with two hard floors.

**The contract.** An Encounter (resource, capability catalog, or foreign domain, the last routed to projection or fresh approval) is adjudicated only after identity pinning: TLS origin, the RFC 9728 protected-resource-metadata chain to the resource's AS (with domain-authorized-issuer as the informative legitimacy check, unverifiable associations route to a human), and the self-declaration digest where one exists. Discovery Adjudication takes the Mission reference, pinned identity, declaration digest, actor, and session taint, and returns bind (a progressive in-ceiling drawdown carrying encountered_resource and resource_declaration_digest on the expansion wire, or the AAuth PS token-gate decision), route-to-a-human (an expansion proposal carrying the same pinned facts), or refuse. Default-closed: no consented ceiling, no bind outcome, ever.

**The two floors, policy-independent.** (1) The lying resource: self-declarations are accountability material, never classification authority; consequence classes come from the deployment's own classification, declarations may only tighten, and irreversible/external-commitment/privileged-administration authority never binds on an encounter by policy alone. (2) Injection-driven discovery: in a tainted session, no external-communication- or commitment-capable binding by policy, a human decides with the taint disclosed; every discovered channel enters the harness egress enumeration at binding; optional exclusivity latching of discovered egress against sensitive reads.

**Discovery Evidence** (JCS canonical bytes, all three outcomes recorded): mission, outcome, pinned resource identity, declaration digest, ceiling-entry digest, actor, taint, adjudicator, time; registrable on the Mission's transparency feed. Security considerations cover the honest residuals (misdescription within a correct class is attributable, not impossible; an untainted session binding within an over-broad family is a consent-time breadth decision), declaration swap, ceiling probing (uniform refusals), and adjudicator availability (outage stops new bindings, never existing authority).

**Integration:** audit gains the Discovery Evidence table row; the harness egress enumeration explicitly admits discovered channels and brands any other new channel a mediated-environment violation; progressive, the AAuth R3 section, and the architecture's open-world passage point at the contract; the security model's adversary list gains the injection-steers-discovery entry with its residual; architecture document map and README (28 drafts, catalog entry, experimental list) updated.

RFC 9728 is ratified and normative; domain-authorized-issuer and R3 stay informative; the frozen core is untouched. All seven touched drafts plus the new one build clean cold-cache; suite dangling-reference check passes.

pulls.title: Strengthen Mission Discovery: fix the adjudicator model, specify the mechanisms
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Strengthens draft-mcguinness-mission-discovery per the fresh review.

**The design error, fixed.** The PDP can no longer be a Discovery Adjudicator: a bind creates authority, and the family's first invariant places authority creation at the Mission Issuer alone. The adjudicator is now always the Mission Issuer (the AAuth PS being that binding's issuer), catalog admission rides the drawdown like any resource (the binding names the catalog and its admitted tool_id set; the PDP enforces per-capability drift afterward), and the audit table's evidence producer corrects to issuer.

**Mechanisms specified.** The Encountered Resource object gets a defined shape (origin, resource_metadata_digest, issuer) shared by the wire parameter and the evidence member.
