Adjudication gains its missing input, the authority sought, whose deployment-assigned classes drive both floors. New Re-Encounter and Drift subsection: matching encounters are idempotent (no adjudication, no drawdown spent), a changed declaration digest is a new encounter for unbound authority and a drift signal for bound authority. Route-to-a-human is plumbed: the proposal travels the deferred-approval surface (its queue-pressure bounds applying to encounter floods), the disclosure renders identity, the declaration as claim not fact, and taint status, and encounter_id correlates the encounter with its human resolution.

**Evidence hardened.** Typed member definitions, new encounter_id and sought_classes members, adjudicator signing under the suite's evidence conventions (the signature identifies the adjudicator), foreign-domain encounters explicitly evidenced, and a worked bound-encounter JSON example.

**Honest oracle posture.** The bind/route/refuse trichotomy is acknowledged as a coarse ceiling leak; mitigations are the new adjudication-wide rate bound (all outcomes, since routed and refused encounters consume Approver attention and are the probing surface), uniform refusals, and an optional collapse of refused into routed_to_approval. The two floors' combined matrix is stated plainly. Both drafts build clean cold-cache.

pulls.title: Cross-domain: relationship to Transaction Token chaining
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds an informative Relationship to Transaction Token Chaining section to the cross-domain profile, addressing draft-fletcher-transaction-token-chaining-profile (Fletcher/Kasselman/O'Dell), a sibling profile of the same identity-chaining framework: same exchange-then-redeem shape, same short-grant discipline, distinct typ, different payload (in-flight transaction context versus the durable approved task).

Three composition rules: (1) the mission claim object may ride the sibling profile's minimized txn_claims as evidence only, exactly as it already rides an intra-domain Transaction Token in this document's end-to-end example, inert at the partner (no derivation, widening, or gating), with the Mandate as the verification path, so Mission-scoped authority reaches another domain only through this document's grant; (2) a Resource AS participating in a transaction chain may record the transaction identifier with its redemption and derivation evidence, correlating the chain and the projection in audit across both domains; (3) recursion does not extend projection: the sibling's recursive chaining carries the Mission reference onward as evidence only, and a third domain that must honor the Mission needs its own projection from the originating issuer.

Informative citation (individual draft), consistent with the family's dependency rule; the reference resolves via bibxml and the draft builds clean cold-cache.

pulls.title: README: accommodate Mission Discovery fully
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: PR #160 gave discovery the draft count, the experimental adoption-order entry, and a catalog entry, but four README surfaces still omitted it: the naming-boundary enumeration of non-oauth-named profiles (which also omitted consumption metering, a pre-existing gap, both added); the architecture diagram's govern block (Discovery line added beside Expansion and Completion); the Governed Agent row's add-on list (discovery added, noted experimental and paired with progressive); and the role-based reading paths (added to the issuer path, since the encounter is adjudicated at the issuer, and to the harness-builder path, since the harness reports taint and admits discovered channels into its egress enumeration). README-only change; the build workflow ignores it by paths-ignore.

pulls.title: Architecture: Discovery joins the Govern verb's owners
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The Govern verb's owner list (Status, Signals, Expansion, Completion, Management) omitted the discovery companion even though the architecture's document map and open-world passage both name it. Adds Discovery as an owner, marked experimental, with its one-line role: binding encountered resources within a pre-consented ceiling. Builds clean cold-cache.

pulls.title: Strengthen the Mission Mandate
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the fresh review of the Mandate.

**Two operational holes closed.** (1) Key rotation was silently fatal to old Mandates: evidence lives for the audit horizon but kid resolved against live keys only. The issuer now MUST keep each minted Mandate's verification key resolvable for that Mandate's evidence lifetime (mandate_exp, else the audit horizon); rotation retires a key from signing, never from resolvability within the bound. Stated in Minting and Conformance, with the Unverifiable class noting that an unresolvable kid inside the bound is itself worth recording. (2) Minting was an unstated oracle: the minting surface now MUST authenticate and authorize requesters under the deployment's visibility policy, with unauthorized requesters indistinguishable from a nonexistent Mission, per the family's anti-oracle discipline.

**Verification and taxonomy.** The dangling structure and mandate_exp checks are now numbered steps (Structure at 2, Evidence lifetime at 5; eight steps total), and the taxonomy gains its missing fourth class, Expired: verified but past its evidence lifetime, not cured by a state check, and not evidence against the facts. Conformance now cites the steps generically.

**Family-consistency migration.** The mission object gains REQUIRED expires_at as its fifth member (the claim member the issuance-grant profile defines, headed to core -01 per #157) and top-level mission_expiry is deleted, one spelling for one fact family-wide. The example payload and JWS signing-input encodings were recomputed, with the old payload reproduced byte-exactly first and the new blocks verified round-trip.

**Hardenings.** state_at_issuance now reads under the fail-safe rule (anything but exact active means not active at minting). New OPTIONAL minted_for member: attribution, never audience or access control, so a leaked Mandate names its leak path; Non-Goals states no-audience as deliberate. SD-JWT issuers MAY add decoy digests so partial presentations do not reveal the authority-entry count. Vertical derivation gains audit symmetry: rails SHOULD register their derivation events on the Mission's feed, so the Mission's evidence shows what external artifacts it spawned, as it shows what children it delegated.

Builds clean cold-cache; zero broken references; zero over-length lines; no other draft referenced the removed member.

pulls.title: Propagate the fifth compromise-resistant condition
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The family-review wave added a fifth condition to the runtime profile's agent-compromise-resistant enforcement claim (approval disclosures rendered by a component isolated from the agent), but the summaries in the README (note and level table), the architecture's High-Assurance Agent definition, and the security model's rests-on line still enumerated four. Found by the blog-series review sweep, whose corrected posts would otherwise have contradicted the repo's own summaries. All three now enumerate five, matching the runtime profile's authoritative statement. Both drafts build clean cold-cache.

pulls.title: Situate discovery infrastructure in the open-world discovery profile
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Registries and federated catalogs (the class of systems behind the MCP Registry and Google's Agentic Resource Discovery spec) solve the supply side of discovery: findability and publisher authenticity. This change situates that layer against the profile's authority side, in four short additions to draft-mcguinness-mission-discovery:

- **The Encounter**: discovery infrastructure determines what an agent meets and what is known about it at encounter; it changes the encounter's quality, never its governance. One MAY: a deployment may scope a ceiling family to a curated registry or catalog, with every floor applying within it unchanged.
- **Resource Identity**: publisher verification (namespace-verified registry entry, domain-anchored catalog) is an additional pinned fact, recorded and rendered in disclosures.
- **The Lying Resource**: a registry listing or publisher-verified catalog entry is a self-declaration; verification proves authorship, never safety.
- **Injection-Driven Discovery**: a searchable capability index is the steering surface; the attacker's entry is legitimately verified, so every publisher check passes, which is why the taint floor is policy-independent.

The systems are described generically per the suite's convention; no external citations added. `make draft-mcguinness-mission-discovery.txt` builds clean.

pulls.title: Fix six defects from the family-wide Chief Architect review
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Six concrete defects from a family-wide review (all 28 drafts), companion drafts only; core-spec findings are filed separately as #168, #169, #170 since the core is published.

## Changes

- **aauth**: split the merged normative-reference YAML entry (the core's title/target/author had collided into the issuance-grant entry, so the issuance-grant reference rendered with the core's metadata). Verified fixed in the built references section.
- **security-model**: the recommendation-vs-requirement caveat under the agent-compromise-resistant guarantee classified only four of the claim's five conditions; the fifth (agent-isolated rendering of the approval disclosure) is now classified, completing the propagation started in 044bff8. Also fixes the `pEP-placement` typo.
- **consent-evidence**: the Conformance item requiring Consent Evidence for `narrowed` decisions is now scoped to deployments supporting approval revision (the only defined trigger, and an Experimental profile; unscoped it was unsatisfiable for std-track deployments without it). The media-type registration's mention of the audit profile now cites it, and the audit draft is added to the informative references.
- **discovery**: the evidence `typ` carried an `application/` prefix; dropped per the family's JWS `typ` convention (the Mandate states the RFC 7515 Section 4.1.9 rule).
- **child-delegation**: `parent_token` presentation now carries the same replay handling as expansion's `predecessor_token` (no rotation, no replay-detection registration, anomaly recording, rate limit when not sender-constrained); previously an equivalent surface had none. Adds a fail-safe rule for consumers encountering an unrecognized `cascade_mode` (a REQUIRED member whose value space includes appendix-experimental values).
- **approval-revision**: removed `audience` from the per-parameter subset list in Security Considerations (not a dimension the client authors; the doc's own dimensions are scope, resource, `authorization_details`).

## Verification

All six edited drafts build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc (`make draft-<name>.txt`).

pulls.title: Harmonize the denial carrier; apply the reference-classification convention
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Applies the two recommended design changes from the family-wide Chief Architect review, in the recommended order (carrier first, so the reference sweep classifies the post-harmonization graph).

## 1. Error-carrier harmonization (expansion / child-delegation)

The two related-Mission profiles used different members in the same wire position, with uneven IANA treatment, and expansion's single member dual-carried two semantically different code sets.

- **expansion** now splits along the real seam: `mission_expansion_status` carries reconciliation statuses only, and a new **shared `mission_denial_reason` member** carries adjudication denial reasons. The member is defined and IANA-registered in expansion, with the child delegation profile named as a co-user, and the IANA text now anticipates one shared "Mission Denial Reason" registry instead of two per-profile ones.
- **child-delegation** carries its closed set in the shared member and no longer needs its own registration.
- **progressive**'s `out_of_ceiling` rides the shared member.
- **authority-server**'s native carriage and conformance text reflect the reconciliation-vs-denial split.

Wire impact: denial reasons move member name (`mission_expansion_status` to `mission_denial_reason`) in expansion error responses; reconciliation statuses are unchanged. All value strings are unchanged.

## 2. Reference-classification convention, applied family-wide

The convention is written down in CONTRIBUTING (normative when any BCP 14 requirement consults it, even conditionally; maturity is a dependency boundary; named claims bind to properties, not documents). Applied:

- **status**: expansion and child-delegation elevated to normative (their states sit in the authoritative state machine; prose already scopes them to adopting deployments).
- **management**: authority-server (its IANA registry) and child-delegation (the `parent` filter) elevated.
- **audit**: authzen, consent-evidence, and child-delegation elevated for the evidence-type table; a new paragraph scopes companion rows to producing deployments and keeps the Experimental rows (Signals SET form, Discovery Evidence) informative per the maturity boundary.
- **progressive**: runtime elevated (the prohibited-class rule consults its taxonomy).
- **metering**: authzen and consent-evidence elevated (their sections carry conditional MUSTs).
- **orchestration**: authzen elevated; orchestration now owns the joint-deployment duty (harness visibility into active unwind decisions, stricter-outcome rule).
- **harness**: the orchestration-interaction MUST becomes a pointer to the duty orchestration owns, removing the family's one std-doc MUST conditioned on an Experimental profile.
- **runtime**: the agent-compromise-resistant claim's second condition binds to the property (mediated environment, no unmediated path, published scope statement) with the harness profile cited as the defined way to establish it, avoiding a runtime/harness normative cycle; the trifecta untrusted-content bullet gets the same treatment.

## Verification

All eleven touched drafts build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc. Post-change greps confirm `mission_expansion_status` appears only in reconciliation contexts and every moved reference sits in its normative block exactly once.

pulls.title: Resolve the harness-developer review: seven companion fixes
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolutions from a harness-developer review of the full family. Core findings are filed as issues (core is published as -00); these are the companion fixes, each a tight capability-preserving addition.

- **audit**: Harness Evidence joins the Evidence Types table (JCS canonical bytes, `application/mission-harness-evidence+json`, harness key as authoritative producer). Previously the harness profile required the evidence but no producer row existed, so it could not verifiably register on the Mission's feed.
- **harness**: taint follows derivation across session boundaries (sub-agent spawns from tainted sessions inherit; compaction-summary-seeded sessions are not fresh); rendering surfaces that dereference remote references join the minimum secondary-egress enumeration; a single-process/local-first applicability paragraph names what such a harness can and cannot claim; Harness Evidence registrability and key publication; the taint section names the practical effect (model-composed egress in open-world sessions is human-gated, the trust list is the lever).
- **runtime**: argument-dependent operations (shell, generic HTTP client, code interpreter) classify per invocation via a declared classifier, defaulting to the widest reachable class; Operation Profiles MAY derive mechanically for schema-bearing discovered tools, recorded with the capability source digest.
- **child-delegation**: the child-Mission-vs-delegated-token choice gets a testable criterion: survivability beyond the delegating flow, applied at spawn.
- **orchestration**: staleness stops dispatch and holds in-flight work but MUST NOT run post-completion behavior (compensation only on established non-active state, so an issuer outage no longer looks like revocation); model-proposed unwind plans are admissible through the deployment's review surface, keeping the model-output floor intact.
- **signals + status**: the Status response carries the lifecycle `version` counter where Signals is deployed, so a consumer that recovered via Status can re-seat gap detection (previously guaranteed divergence); the executing harness is named an intended receiver class for its own Missions' events.

Deferred deliberately: a lease/heartbeat mechanism for abandoned Missions (new mechanism, needs design), and discharge visibility beyond `authorization_details` diffing (omission is intentional in the completion profile).

All seven drafts build clean (`kramdown-rfc` + `xml2rfc`).

pulls.title: Graduated hardening of five companions from the CISO review
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Implements the quick hardening pass from the Fortune 500 CISO review of the family, reworked after feedback so that no change raises the base adoption floor. The pattern throughout is graduated normativity: behavior stays SHOULD at base, the deployment's published statement discloses its posture, and MUST binds only the named stricter operating mode or falls out of fail-closed absence semantics.

## Changes

**issuance-grant** (MAS compromise reaches estate-wide issuance; reconciliation is the only check)
- Reconciliation of MAS minting evidence against consuming-AS redemption logs stays SHOULD at base, with unmatched redemptions treated as security events.
- The conformance statement now declares the reconciliation posture: the window, or that logs are not reconciled.
- Under the Enterprise Mission Authority Profile, reconciliation is MUST.

**cross-domain** (the derivation record is the only evidence a partner's minting stayed within the grant)
- The Resource AS derivation record stays SHOULD, with the rationale strengthened.
- Whether the record is kept becomes a pre-established trust input; an originating AS MAY condition projection on it, so the decision is per partner rather than family-wide.

**status** (an undeclared propagation bound was silently ambiguous)
- `mission_max_stale_seconds` stays OPTIONAL. Absence now has defined semantics: no bound is declared, and a consumer MUST size reliance to token lifetime alone. Nothing new to publish; silence simply means worst case.

**orchestration** (experimental; compensation is the orchestrator attesting to its own conduct)
- `evidence_envelope` is REQUIRED only on compensation records in the irreversible, external-commitment, and privileged-administration classes. All other records are unchanged.

**discovery** (experimental; the injection floor keys on the harness's taint report)
- A missing, stale, or unverifiable taint state is treated as tainted at adjudication. Zero infrastructure cost; it only routes egress-capable bindings to a human when the report is absent.

## Notes
- The aauth normative-reference defect flagged in the review was already fixed on main by "Fix six defects from the family-wide Chief Architect review" (9eb54b3); no change needed.
- All five drafts build clean with kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc.

pulls.title: Resolve OAuth-expert review findings across the companion drafts
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
## What this is

Resolves the findings from a family-wide, OAuth directorate-style review of the Mission-Bound Authorization suite (all 28 drafts read at review depth, plus a cross-family consistency audit). This PR fixes every Critical and Major finding, and the Minor findings, in the **editable** documents.

The published **core** (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`) is deliberately untouched. Its findings are filed as issues instead:

- #179 - Token Exchange delegation surface (three hardening gaps)
- #180 - extensibility seam for Deferred Approval + stale forward-references
- #181 - minor interoperability, IANA, and editorial fixes

## Scope

28 files changed (27 companion `.md` drafts + `README.md`), +2236 / -669 lines. `.txt` / `.html` are gitignored build artifacts and are regenerated by CI, so only `.md` sources are committed.

## The three Criticals

- **Progressive** - a single policy-adjudicated in-ceiling drawdown could activate the entire pre-consented ceiling unattended. Now a per-drawdown bound is required and must be published in the Deployment Profile.
- **Discovery** - the lying-resource and taint floors trusted encounter data the agent authored. The Mission Issuer must now establish resource identity itself (server-side RFC 9728 re-fetch) and obtain taint from a non-agent channel, routing to a human where it cannot.
- **Mandate** - the key-retention rule foreclosed compromise recovery, so a leaked key could mint a self-consistent Mandate over a real active Mission. Routine rotation is now split from compromise-revocation (with a published revocation signal), and audit registration is mandatory for high-consequence reliance.

## Cross-cutting fixes

- **Freshness is now honest** - Status `fresh_until` is bounded by the advertised `mission_max_stale_seconds`, so the Runtime-Enforced level's revocation claim holds; the four worked-example timestamps were corrected.
- **Compromise-resistance covers acquisition** - the five-condition claim (count preserved) now closes the gap where a compromised agent mints a fresh credential bound to its own key; propagated consistently to the runtime, architecture, and security-model docs.
- **Issuer anchoring** - cross-domain redemption rejects any grant whose `iss` != `mission.issuer` (restoring one-hop); mirrored in attenuation.
- **Wire completeness** - the issuance-grant minting endpoint, the AuthZEN action-approval / idempotency-key / emitter-identity carriage, and the child-delegation credential-acquisition grant are now specified.
- **Consolidation drift** - substrate now requires an authenticated state source and lists Discovery; the security model adds Approver-compromise, the bearer-token case, PEP key custody, and the full revocation-latency formula; the architecture stops leaking normative language.

## Verification

- Core (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`) not modified.
- No em-dashes or ` -- ` dash-asides introduced (house rule).
- Added kramdown `{{refs}}` balance per file; code fences balanced.
- Recomputed test vectors verified: consent-evidence `consent_rendering_hash` (cascaded to the approval-revision example) and the audit `token_digest` with the new `token_digest_kind` member.
- `.txt`/`.html` not built locally (toolchain absent in the worktree); CI regenerates them.

Draft PR: each document's own Security Considerations remain authoritative; reviewers should sanity-check the normative wording added to their area.

pulls.title: Reconcile retired-key retention and harden the Enterprise MAS profile
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the highest-leverage companion findings from an enterprise deployability review (identity-vendor / IAM-operator seat). Core-draft findings from the same review were filed as #183-#188 rather than edited, per the published-core convention.

## Retired-key retention (status, mandate)

The Mandate required retired verification keys to stay resolvable in published key material for each Mandate's evidence lifetime, while the status profile forbade serving retired keys at `jwks_uri`. One JWKS could not satisfy both, so a years-old Mandate verified against a compliant issuer would land in the Unverifiable class at every audit.

- **Status**: retired keys now stay resolvable in `jwks_uri` for the record retention period, the pattern the AuthZEN profile already uses for its evidence key sets; the deployment-defined audit interface and the MUST NOT-serve rule are gone. No new metadata member or endpoint.
- **Mandate**: the resolvability clause points at the status profile's matching rule. Issuer Key Compromise is reworked: the old text claimed rotation bounds the exposure and verifiers resolve against live key material, both of which the draft's own retention rule forbids. Now: rotation does not bound the exposure, registration does, and an issuer that learns of a compromise publishes the compromise time; unregistered Mandates under that `kid` whose `iat` cannot be anchored earlier classify as Unverifiable (the class definition gains that arm).

## Enterprise MAS profile hardening (authority-server)

- **Estate Prerequisites** (new subsection): the profile's mandatory Join Assertion path assumes the unchanged AS provides RFC 7662 introspection, sender-constrained issuance for high-consequence agent clients, and `cnf` in introspection responses; an estate that cannot provide these stays at the mapping-join conformance floor and does not claim the profile. Also states plainly that digest-based introspection is not something mainstream ASes provide.
- **`mapping_version`** claim on the Join Assertion, recorded in join evidence, so a drifted mapping is attributable in evidence rather than only detectable by comparing documents. The mapping contract already required the version identifier; nothing consumed it on the wire.
- **Staleness clarification**: the runtime profile requires per-class staleness bounds while discovery carries one scalar; the Active freshness bullet now defines `mission_max_stale_seconds` as the tightest bound in force, with per-class bounds in the Enforcement Scope Statement.

All three drafts build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc.

pulls.title: Specify the PDP operational envelope: outage posture, retries, cost model
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Second PR from the enterprise deployability review (follows #189): resolves the PDP round-trip cost cluster, the review's highest-severity findings. Guidance and reconciliation only; no new mechanism, no wire changes beyond two OPTIONAL Refusal Record members.

**Runtime**
- The Enforcement Scope Statement now declares a PDP-unavailability posture per mediated class (max ride-through on unexpired unconsumed permits, queue-vs-drop for suppressed work). Fail-closed was uniform but the outage blast radius was undeclared.
- The per-class freshness table is promoted from illustrative to RECOMMENDED defaults, adopted absent a documented analysis.
- The idempotency-match rule gains a retransmission fast path: a matching request whose prior permit is unexpired and unconsumed returns the prior decision instead of refusing, so a lost permit response no longer locks the action out for the reconciliation window.
- Deployment Considerations states where the embedded-PDP locality claim ends: the single-use store, idempotency match, metering counters, exclusivity latch, and per-Mission evidence sequence are strongly consistent per-Mission state, so for high-consequence classes the Mission's consistency domain, not PDP placement, sets the latency floor.

**AuthZEN**
- New "Timeouts, Retries, and Overload" subsection: timeout inside the class's staleness budget failing to `pdp_unreachable`, safe retry composing with the runtime retransmission rule and no double metering, 429/Retry-After overload shedding, batch size bounds.
- `permit_expires_at` derivation defined for `fresh` mode with no supplied lease; no mode leaves the permit window unbounded on the wire.
- Permit-cache expectations stated plainly: parameter-bound single-use classes never amortize; the steady state for writes is one evaluation per action.
- Refusal Records MAY aggregate under sustained PEP-side failure (`first_evaluated_at`, `attempt_count`), so a retrying agent during an outage doesn't mint one signed record per attempt.

**Metering**: lossy settlement named as a self-inflicted DoS (reservations accumulate until `quota_exceeded`), with a reconciliation-cadence SHOULD; lease intervals sized to the class staleness bound.

**Status**: `fresh_until` is capped by the advertised `mission_max_stale_seconds` and named the transitive ceiling on permit windows; the 60s worked value is reconciled with the runtime's sub-300s defaults.

**Harness**: resume checks stated as freshness-bound checks satisfiable from cached state, once per window, not per continuation.

All five drafts build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc.

pulls.title: Make the transparency log operable: independence, monitoring, read path, erasure
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Third PR from the enterprise deployability review (follows #189, #190): resolves the transparency-log operations cluster. The write path was already right (hash-only, non-blocking, trivial volume); these changes close the gap between "a log you can write to" and "an audit capability you can operate and rely on."

**Audit**
- **Operator independence.** Nothing prevented the Mission Issuer from operating the only Transparency Service, which put the auditor back to trusting the issuer's infrastructure with Merkle decoration. Now: the service SHOULD be operator-independent of its feeds' producers; where producer and service share an operator, a second independent service is the compensating control, and without one the deployment MUST NOT claim cross-domain verifiability. Topology and operator relationships go in the Mission Deployment Profile.
- **Monitoring is a duty.** A deployment MUST designate the party that periodically re-verifies feed consistency against each service, with an alerting obligation; a single unmonitored service MUST NOT claim non-equivocation (it has tamper evidence only against post-registration alteration).
- **The read path must be documented.** The by-`sub` feed enumeration the abstract promises was explicitly out of scope with no obligations at all; a deployment now MUST document its enumeration mechanism (order, completeness relative to What to Register, auditor authentication). Deployment-chosen mechanism, no new API surface.
- **Erasure Record** (new evidence type, `application/mission-erasure-record+json`): registered when committed evidence is destroyed, carrying the erased commitment, basis, time, and salt. Lawful GDPR erasure and convenient loss were normatively indistinguishable (relying parties are forbidden from reading a gap as tampering); the tombstone makes a gap explained-on-feed or conspicuous by absence. Wired into the verification-failure taxonomy and the privacy section (erasure now requires the record).
- **Receipt durability**: a producer claiming offline verifiability MUST retain the Receipt or a resolvable entry identifier for the audit horizon.
- **Registration bound with teeth**: the documented time bound, backlog metric, and alerting component are published in the Mission Deployment Profile, mirroring the runtime profile's orphaned-evidence pattern.
- **Interim TS key rule**: a service key that signed any retained Receipt stays resolvable for the audit horizon (the family's retired-key posture, per #189), pending the deferred log-lifecycle work.

**Security model**: the Transparency Service trusted-base entry now names the structural case that needs no compromise: producer-operated logs degrade the cross-domain claim.

**Discovery**: corrects the privacy claim that the transparency log "learns every resource an agent met"; under the audit profile it receives only hash commitments, so it learns the feed, cadence, and that encounters occurred.

All three drafts build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc.

pulls.title: Add the brownfield entry-ramp table and declare Resource Server coverage
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Fourth PR from the enterprise deployability review (follows #189, #190, #191): the migration-artifacts cluster. The review found the suite's brownfield story derivable but scattered: the core's day-one AS prerequisite stack (PAR, RAR, RFC 9068) is never consolidated, the README's adoption order assumes the AS-change path first, and nothing declares which Resource Servers actually enforce per-entry constraints.

**Architecture**
- New **Entry Ramps by Estate** table in Mission Deployment Patterns: five estate starting conditions (changeable AS with the token plane in place, changeable AS lacking RAR or on opaque tokens, unchangeable AS, many-AS estates wanting one governance point, no PEP coverage) mapped to the recommended ramp and its day-one delta per component, with the shared-substrate note that a ramp is an entry point, not a fork.
- The **Mission Deployment Profile** now composes two more per-layer statements: the Resource Server coverage split (which resources enforce `authorization_details` and which see only the scope projection, so the silently-unenforced-constraints gap is declared in the same manifest as `residual_risks`) and the transparency-service topology and registration schedule (the fields #191 requires deployments to publish). Example JSON and `residual_risks` updated to match.

**README**
- The Baseline Issuance row states its day-one AS prerequisites inline, and that the standalone MAS needs none of them.
- Adoption-order step 1 forks on whether the AS can change, pointing the majority estate at MAS phase 1 instead of misdirecting it into the core.

Architecture builds clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc; the README is not a build target.

pulls.title: Key-custody hardening: minting logs, attenuation roots, MAS custody, migration pattern
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Fifth and final PR from the enterprise deployability review (follows #189-#192): the scattered key-custody and MAS-operations findings. Core-draft custody findings remain issues #183-#185.

**Mandate**: the minting surface already authenticated and authorized requesters but never had to remember anything; minting is now a recorded evidence event (authenticated requester, `jti`, `iat`, `minted_for`) retained for the evidence lifetime. Without an issuer-side record, `minted_for` leak attribution cannot stand up against a repudiating recipient; the Join Assertion's minting-evidence sentence was already the family pattern.

**Attenuation**: the parent token's `cnf` key doubles as an offline minting key for its whole subtree, which the draft never said. Named plainly, with SHOULD-level mediated custody (hardware-backed where available) for attenuation roots specifically: a key whose theft mints credentials warrants a higher custody class than one whose theft replays one.

**Security model**: Documenting the Trusted Base now covers key custody per key class (issuer signing, evidence signing, agent sender-constraint, mediating-PEP custody, attenuation roots): location, custody grade, and compromise-recovery procedure. The model's strongest assumption reduces to these keys and the checklist never asked about them.

**Authority Server**
- New Signing-Key Custody security consideration: SHOULD-level non-exportable keystore with dual-controlled generation, distinct `kid`s for high-volume vs long-lived artifact classes so custody can differ by blast radius, and the AS introspection credential tiered as the same class of secret (its compromise forges joins).
- Join Assertion cost guidance: assertion lifetime tracks the token, so short token lifetimes put minting plus introspection on every rotation; deployments size token lifetimes to the runtime cutoff, and the MAS MAY reuse an introspection result within the staleness bound.
- Multi-issuer estates document the resource-to-Mission-Issuer mapping; an unmapped resource is outside the profile's governance, not guessed.
- Progressive Adoption gains the missing starting point: migrating standing service accounts per task, with the entitlement catalog as derivation-policy input and the account's ungoverned residue as the adoption metric.

All four drafts build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc.

pulls.title: Handbook alignment: laws, litmus, levels, current practice, and OWASP coverage
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Applies Proposals 1-5 of the handbook alignment review; Proposal 6 (Mission Receipt portable schema) is tracked as #194.

**Architecture:**
- **P4 (Introduction):** one paragraph naming the three compensating controls in current practice (read-only scoping, human-executed writes, fenced pilots) and their costs.
- **P1 (Mission Invariants):** the five laws of delegated authority stated inline with an informative many-to-many table mapping each law to the invariants that carry it. The last two invariants are presented as the wire-level mechanics that carry Containment and Attribution onto a substrate, not laws of their own.
- **P2 (Mission Requirements):** the litmus converged to the handbook's six-property slotting as a numbered list. Integrity commitment folds into property 1; state-gating of issuance and reliance is preserved in property 5. A family-wide sweep found no other draft quoting the old wording.
- **P3 (Assurance Levels):** informative table reading the levels as an adoption ladder of defensibly grantable action classes, using the runtime profile's classification, with the non-normative caveats (resource policy authoritative, varies by binding, Deployment Profile carries the claim).

**Security Model:**
- **P5 (new Appendix A):** OWASP Agentic Threats T1-T15 mapped with three verdicts (contained/bounded/delegated); tally is six contained, nine bounded, none waved away. The LLM Top 10 is included so the delegated verdict is exercised rather than defined-and-unused. Taxonomy versions pinned in the citations with a drift caveat.

Both drafts build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc.

pulls.title: First-class standing agents: the pattern, the Ceiling Review, and the charter posture
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Closes the standing-agent gap at spec level, matching the handbook's new "standing agent at scale" treatment (the charter/unit split, renewal-with-evidence, and decay tells now live in the lifecycle chapter but had no wire counterpart).

**Architecture:**
- New **standing-agent pattern** in Mission Deployment Patterns, alongside quarantine: the agent stands, the authority cycles. Charter = Mission with consented ceiling + drawdown policy; units of work draw bounded Missions as in-ceiling successors (single agent) or policy-approved Child Missions (fan-out); Completion discharges per unit; the guard classes never ride the policy path. States honestly that without the experimental progressive profile the same pattern runs as ordinary freshly-approved unit Missions with Deferred Approval: the experimental profile changes the unit economics, not the governance shape.
- Deployment Profile composes the **standing-charter posture** (per-drawdown and rate bounds, action-class mapping, ceiling review cadence and evidence) and the illustrative JSON gains a `standing_charters` block.

**Progressive Authorization:**
- New normative **Ceiling Review** section: a published review cadence bounds each chain in time; past it, policy adjudication is refused and the chain falls back to a fresh human approval that re-consents (or narrows) the ceiling, whose disclosure MUST render the chain's record since the prior review (drawdowns, guard exceptions, out_of_ceiling refusals, discharged entries, metering consumption where run), committed via Consent Evidence where claimed. The decay tells are stated as checkable from the chain's own records.
- Conformance gains the chain-bounding duty; Security Considerations gains the temporal-decay entry (the per-drawdown and rate bounds cap what a chain draws per unit time, not for how long).

Deliberately not done here: no maturity changes (Progressive stays experimental; graduating its ceiling core is a separate decision), no README changes.

pulls.title: Approval-authority composition: a spectrum, not a species
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
States for the approval event what the runtime profile states for enforcement, in the Architecture's Approve and Record verb. The handbook carries this doctrine in Who May Approve (the approval chapter) and the admission-time bet; the family had no spec-level statement.

The paragraph: the approval event requires an accountable `{iss, sub}` principal deciding against committed inputs before any authority exists, and the proposer is never the approver. A human approves directly with Consent Evidence; a deterministic, versioned policy approves at machine speed within a human-consented ceiling, with `policy_version` keeping the chain re-checkable (policy approves the instance because a human approved the policy). Risk scores, fraud signals, and model judgments compose as decision inputs that inform or narrow either approver, exactly as content-aware controls compose at the runtime layer: signals, never the approving authority. Generated judgment is never the sole authority for granting or widening (an LLM approver reading attacker-influenced proposals is itself an injection surface), and the class guard keeps a fresh human decision on the irreversible, external-commitment, and privileged-administration classes.

Companion finding on the published core filed as an issue per the published-core convention: the core's definition says "human-approved" twice, which is the strongest species compression in the family.

pulls.title: Survivable incorrectness and least exposure become first-class in the Architecture
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Names at spec level the two organizing principles the handbook carries (the architecture chapter's design stance and Appendix C) that the family's mechanisms implement but never state.

**Capability Envelope:**
- **Survivable incorrectness** named as the stance beneath the levers: the agent is probabilistic, the family never bets on the model being right, and wrongness is made survivable on two arms. The action arm is the envelope and its levers, wire-backed (per-action checks, the class guard, metering, unwinding through recorded reversibility classes), so execution fails in bounded, detectable, recoverable ways.
- **Least exposure** named as the input arm: a Mission is a budget for authority and for disclosure at once, and mediated custody generalizes from credentials to context (the agent gets the working set the current step justifies, not its principal's reach).
- The maturity split stated rather than blurred: the exposure arm's enforceable edges are the harness taint rule, egress mediation, and catalog filtering; Mission-scoping retrieval, memory, and context assembly has no interoperable form anywhere yet, so the interior is deployment discipline, declared in the Deployment Profile rather than claimed.

**Deployment Profile:** the exposure posture joins the composed statements, the illustrative JSON gains an `exposure` block (taint rule, egress enumeration, Mission-scoped vs unscoped exposure points), and `residual_risks` gains the matching line, so the least-exposure claim is checkable and its gap declarable in the same manifest as everything else.

**Prevention, Detection, and Residue:** one closing sentence extends the residue pattern to the exposure arm.

Deliberately not done: no new requirement row (the exposure interior has no wire surface to answer it), no new draft or endpoints for retrieval/memory scoping (the named community gap), no Security Model changes.

pulls.title: Lifetime-bounded reliance: name the TTL posture, make runtime an overlay
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Meets estates where they are: in the short-to-medium term deployments will run short-lived ephemeral tokens that expire by TTL rather than evaluating Mission status at every resource, and many legacy systems can only ever validate `exp`. The family already supports this normatively (issuance/refresh gating plus sized TTLs is the core's own baseline; the runtime profile requires an active source only for the three high-consequence classes), but the handbook rhetoric framed it as a consolation prize. This PR names the posture and flips the framing, with no capability changes and the high-consequence active-freshness floor untouched.

The three-sentence reframe:
1. **Expiry is a state check performed by the clock.** Gated issuance/refresh plus TTLs sized to the staleness bound gives a quantified revocation cutoff with zero Resource Server changes and zero status traffic.
2. **Revocation latency is a number, not a level.** Baseline with 300s tokens and Runtime-Enforced with a 300s staleness bound have the same worst-case cutoff; what the higher levels add is per-action enforcement, parameter binding, and evidence.
3. **Runtime enforcement is a targeted overlay, not a substrate.** Mediate the paths where the high-consequence classes live; everything else rides the token lifetime.

Edits:
- **Runtime**: "token-lifetime freshness" becomes a named, conforming state source below the high-consequence floor, declared per class in the Enforcement Scope Statement like any other source; the scope rules now state that resources outside the declared scope rely on issuance gating plus lifetime as a stated posture ("this profile does not require every resource to evaluate Mission state; it requires the deployment to say which do").
- **Architecture**: the Baseline assurance level names **lifetime-bounded reliance** with its revocation math; the Runtime-Enforced blurb no longer dismisses Baseline tokens as "governance metadata"; Deployment Patterns states the overlay principle; the entry-ramp table's no-PEP row uses the posture name.
- **README**: the Baseline row's "audit, not action-time defense" becomes the quantified cutoff statement; a note under the levels table says most estates start (and legacy resources stay) on lifetime-bounded reliance, with runtime as the overlay for consequence-warranted paths.
- **Status**: Recommended Access-Token TTL notes that sizing to the bound is itself a propagation mechanism; introspection and status checks are upgrades, not prerequisites.

Runtime, architecture, and status build clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc; the README is not a build target.

pulls.title: Core: explicitly approved, the Approver principal sentence, and the retired-key rule's single home
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The core slice of the consolidation review, kept deliberately small since the core is published. Closes #198.

- **"human-approved" becomes "explicitly approved"** in both definitional sentences (introduction and terminology). The species compression invited the consent-screen critique against a design that already answers it; everything else in the family is species-neutral.
- **One terminology sentence on the Approver**: an accountable principal, human or authorized policy authority, with provenance deferred exactly as the Single Accountable Approver section already defers it. No new mechanism.
- **The retired-key rule gets its single normative home** in Signing and Key Rotation: rotation retires a key from signing, never from resolvability within its retention bound; the core's bound is token lifetime; companions (status, Mandate, audit) state their longer bounds as extensions of this rule rather than new ones; compromise revocation is named as the exception. This resolves the SHOULD-vs-MUST divergence the review found across five statements of the same rule, and the companion pointer-ization lands in the follow-up consolidation PR.

Builds clean through kramdown-rfc and xml2rfc.

pulls.title: Consolidation pass: one home per doctrine, the Architecture as a map again
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Executes Tiers 1-2 of the simplification review (four parallel reviewers over the family; verdict: the concepts are sound, the accretion is textual). Net -124 lines with zero normative loss; everything cut exists in an owning profile or in the companion handbook. Pairs with #201 (the core slice).

**Architecture** (the bulk): enforces its own "a map, not the territory" yardstick.
- Survivable incorrectness / least exposure compress to the two-arm statement with pointers; the vivid examples return to the handbook; the residue echo becomes one clause.
- The standing-agent pattern, approve-spectrum, quarantine, and open-world paragraphs each compress to their load-bearing sentences plus pointers (the third-commitment insight, the injection-surface sentence, and the no-untrusted-input-plus-egress rule all survive verbatim).
- The five-laws crosswalk table returns to the handbook (one sentence remains); the layer manifesto is stated once; the current-practice paragraph compresses to one sentence; the Binding Checklist defers to the substrate draft that normatively owns it.
- The Deployment Profile is declared to be one artifact, the published serialization of the statements the profiles themselves demand, with owning profiles governing meaning; the exposure block loses its invented field names and standing_charters its redundant evidence array.
- The high-consequence class list is no longer enumerated in prose (the runtime profile owns definitions, the progressive profile owns the prohibited set), which also removes a live three-versus-five-class divergence between sections.

**Companions** (one home per rule):
- The retired-key rule now points at the core's single statement (#201) from mandate, status, and audit, each keeping only its own retention bound; this removes the three-way mutual cross-reference.
- Harness defers the stop-precedence rule to orchestration instead of duplicating it verbatim.
- Progressive's decay bullet compresses to the ceiling-review pointer.
- The Security Model's OWASP appendix declares the adversary model as the owner of coverage and residuals, so the two can't silently diverge.

**Not in this PR (next slice, per the review's Tier 3):** the runtime/AuthZEN/MAS burden reduction: ESS-internal duplicate publish-duties, the RS profile as a delta, the MAS mapping-duty collapse into its Mapping Contract, conformance sections frozen as pointer checklists, and homes for the two floating duties. Tier 4 (document merges, 28 to 25) stays parked pending your call.

pulls.title: Burden reduction: one home per publish-duty, conformance as pointers
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Tier 3 of the simplification review: the deployer-burden slice. The review found 31 publication duties of which 11 were the same ~6 facts demanded two to four times; this PR gives each fact one home. Pairs with #201 and #202.

**Runtime:**
- The Enforcement Scope Statement is now the single home for the five facts the body re-demanded (Mission-establishment mode joins the first bullet; staleness bounds, reconciliation window, retention window, and mediated-paths naming become references to the ESS instead of second MUSTs). The retention constraint (no shorter than the audit horizon) survives as a constraint on the declared value.
- The Resource Server runtime profile becomes a delta over the ESS: it inherits the enforcement-scope items and records only per-operation specifics, with the separable-annex option kept for independently operated Resource Servers.

**Mission Authority Server:** the four scattered mapping duties (client mapping documentation, the join statement, the shared-client_id residual, mapping granularity) collapse into the Enterprise Mapping Contract as their one home, with the residual routed to the Deployment Profile's residual_risks.

**AuthZEN:** the conformance section (which had grown 0 to 15 bullets across review waves, each wave appending re-normative summaries) is frozen as a pointer checklist: each role's obligations cite their owning sections without restating mechanics. Net effect per the review: the protocol-MVP deployment's published surface drops from 7 artifacts (~30 facts, a third duplicated) toward 4 artifacts (~21 facts, none duplicated).

**Filed, not edited:** the two homeless/theater duties that live in the published core (the approval-authentication-strength declaration with no home artifact, and the correlation-property SHOULD-document that belongs in Privacy Considerations prose) follow the published-core convention as an issue.

pulls.title: Harness: deviations-only stop matrix, staleness bound pointed at its home
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves the last actionable leftover from the simplification review. The stop-behavior matrix the spec prints is now a deployment's matrix as written, with documentation owed only for cells that deviate (and only toward stricter behavior), and the conformance list's staleness bullet becomes a pointer to the Enforcement Scope Statement that #203 made the bound's single home. The other leftover (only-active/subset restatements in the Architecture) was verified already resolved by #202: the remaining occurrences are the structurally required artifacts and references-by-name.

pulls.title: Substrate: the binding contract, conforming instantiations, move-on-touch
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the enterprise review's P0 finding 1 (the substrate abstraction is inverted) on the conservative path chosen over the big-bang Mission Model split.

- The Status section is re-scoped from "a profile for new bindings that existing bindings claim no conformance to" to **the family's binding-neutral contract**, with the three existing bindings recorded as its conforming instantiations. The falsifiable flaw (neutrality as description rather than protocol boundary) is closed: the contract binds new bindings normatively and names the existing three as its source instantiations.
- A new informative **Binding Crosswalk** appendix records where each binding satisfies the eight-part conformance role: the OAuth core (full provision, the contract's source text), the MAS (partial provision by design, join in place of the credential), and AAuth (full provision in PS-asserted mode, per its own point-by-point substrate section).
- The **move-on-touch migration policy** is stated: when a binding-neutral definition next changes substantively, the change lands here and the owning binding section becomes a reference, so the boundary exists now and text crosses it opportunistically at zero dedicated churn. No change is ever made solely to move words.

Deliberately not done: no text relocation, no new normative requirements anywhere, the OAuth core untouched (its discovery and metadata sections in particular). The full relocation map for the eventual Mission Model split is preserved on the roadmap issue with its triggers.

pulls.title: Deferred Approval: the Approval Decision Set
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the enterprise review's P0 finding 2 (approval authority is not governed) in the home the verdict chose: the IGA-shaped Deferred Approval profile, not the core.

New OPTIONAL, experimental section **The Approval Decision Set**: a Mission Issuer MAY record, alongside the approval event and joined by `approval_event_id`, the `approval_policy` (id + version of the delegation-of-authority matrix), the `threshold` the assertions satisfied, and one or more decision assertions (principal, kind human/service/policy, decision, time, optional provenance and reason). An approval whose assertions do not satisfy the declared threshold MUST NOT commit; a committed set is immutable and retained for the audit horizon.

The design honors every family constraint: the set is governance evidence, never a wire artifact (no tokens, claims, parameters, or endpoints); the Mission record still carries exactly one accountable Approver as the projection, so the core and every downstream consumer are untouched; the one-person approval is the degenerate case and need not be recorded; human assertions compose with Consent Evidence and policy assertions carry policy id + version, which is the who-may-approve provenance chain (policy approves the instance because a human approved the policy). Conformance gains one paragraph and Security Considerations one (the set threatens auditability, not authority). Worked example included.

This answers "under which role, policy, delegation, and organizational authority was this approval valid" without modeling an IAM governance layer into the family: the complete governance chain stays in the control plane, exactly as the reviewer conceded it could.

pulls.title: Runtime: the RS profile named and versioned as the enforcement adapter contract
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the enterprise review's P0 finding 3 (authorization semantics are not interoperable) per its verdict: the proposed Enforcement Adapter Profile already exists as the Resource Server runtime profile (action identifiers, operation profiles and normalization, constraint support, class floors, permit windows, policy composition, evidence fields); what was missing was its identity.

- The RS runtime profile is **named the family's enforcement adapter contract**: the artifact two independent implementations name and version to agree on a resource's action identifiers, parameter semantics, and enforcement composition, standardizing the adapter contract rather than every business action.
- The MUST-define list gains **a profile identifier and version**, so contract changes are detectable and cross-vendor implementations can bind to the same contract.

Derivation locality is deliberately untouched: the core's position (different ASes derive differently, exactly as with RAR or scopes) stands; portability applies to enforcement semantics, which this contract carries. The reference adapter that proves the contract end to end (ERP or messaging) is real validation work, filed as the companion roadmap issue.

pulls.title: MAS: mode-bounded claims and join hardening
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the enterprise review's P0 findings 4 and 5 in one PR since both land in the MAS document.

**Finding 4 (MAS modes):** the six Progressive Adoption phases group into three named modes with claim boundaries: **records mode** (inventory, approval, lifecycle, audit; no prevention claim of any kind), **enforced-paths mode** (prevents on exactly the ESS-enumerated paths, records mode everywhere else), and **issuance mode** (the token-layer gate returns via the issuance join or native issuance). "No AS code change" holds in every mode; what changes is the claim, and a high-consequence enforcement claim requires issuance mode's machinery or the Estate Prerequisites' AS features, never records alone. The existing phases and text are unchanged; this is labeling plus the claim rule.

**Finding 5 (join ceiling):**
- New join rule 8: **the permit intersects three bounds**, the acting credential's own authority, the Mission's approved authority, and current resource policy, each independently evaluated; the join adds the Mission bound and MUST NOT widen the other two, and a Mission permit never overrides what the credential or the resource would refuse.
- The **ceiling paragraph**: the join proves the credential belongs to the Mission's parties, never that it was issued for the Mission, and no assertion raises that ceiling. For the high-consequence classes association is not the terminal architecture: the issuance join or native Mission-bound issuance restores cryptographic derivation, deployments SHOULD adopt one for those classes, and running high-consequence work on an asserted join alone MUST be stated in `residual_risks`.
- **Join Assertion `aud`** upgraded OPTIONAL to RECOMMENDED (replay scoping); the assertion was already token-digest-bound, `jkt`-bound, lifetime-capped by the token, and instance-bound where the substrate exists, which the review asked for and the text already had.

This completes the five-finding enterprise review batch: findings 1 (#206), 2 (#208), 3 (#209 + issue #210), and 4-5 here, with the Mission Model plan of record preserved as issue #207.

pulls.title: Runtime: named conformance tiers and the exactly-once bound
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves P1 finding 6 per its verdict. The runtime's duties already tier by action class; this names the tiers so the MVP floor is legible: the **core enforcement tier** (Mission establishment, per-action evaluation, freshness, permit/deny with decision identifier and Decision Evidence, parameter binding for parameter-bound classes) and the **transaction-assurance tier** (single-use permits, execution leases, Execution Evidence, outcome reconciliation), claimed per mediated class and required for the high-consequence classes, with the ESS naming which tier covers which class. Adds the honesty bound the reviewer is owed: reconciliation detects divergence, never manufactures exactly-once execution, which exists only where the resource supports idempotency per its operation profile. No duty added or removed; the floor was never the full stack, and now the text says so.

pulls.title: Architecture: adoption ladder vs assurance claims, and the delegation chooser
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves P1 finding 7 and the accepted half of finding 11. New Assurance Claims subsection: the levels stay the adoption ladder (what a deployment has built), and what it can prove becomes named claims listed in the Deployment Profile: approved-record integrity, bounded revocation latency, action-time enforcement, parameter-bound enforcement, and the two existing High-Assurance claims, each pointing at the proof obligation its profile already fixes. Two deployments at one level under different bindings can hold different claims (the MAS modes are the worked case), and the claims are what a relying party compares. This fixes the conflation the reviewer named without renaming levels the handbook depends on. Finding 11's chooser lands in the Delegate verb (token exchange for same-lifecycle execution hops, Child Mission for independent lifecycle/approval/audit, attenuation only where offline minting is the constraint); the client_id rebinding proposal is declined on the core's existing Design Alternative analysis, which already answers it.

pulls.title: Management: administrative metadata for the enterprise estate
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Resolves P1 finding 13 in the Management profile, no core change. A Mission Issuer MAY maintain a per-Mission administrative metadata object (owner distinct from subject and approver, admin_domain, tenant distinct from any Authority Set tenant constraint, risk_class, labels, retention reference): control-plane state that MUST NOT appear on tokens or audience-scoped projections and grants no authority, driving approval routing, filter scope, enumeration, retention, and incident response. The Mission Filter gains owner, admin_domain, and label members. Population is deployment-defined at this revision; a dedicated update operation is deferred until deployment experience shows the shape.

pulls.title: Lifecycle concurrency: the state version, first-class
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves P1 finding 9. The reviewer's race is real: nonce deduplication guards a retransmitted request but not a valid request decided over stale state (a fresh suspend from a console that has not seen a newer resume is legal from active, and commits). Four changes across the lifecycle surfaces, none touching the core:

- **Status** makes `version` REQUIRED and its home: the Mission's state version, a strictly monotonic per-Mission counter incremented on every committed transition and metadata change, ordering observations across every surface. This knowingly reverses the earlier only-with-Signals conditionality; concurrency correctness beats minimalism here.
- **Status lifecycle mutations** gain the OPTIONAL `expected_version` compare-and-set: on mismatch the AS refuses with 409 `stale_version`, and deployments SHOULD require it for high-risk operations. The nonce remains the replay key; the guards are independent (duplicate delivery vs stale decisions).
- **Signals** defers its counter definition to the status profile's state version and carries its value at emission (the one-home flip).
- **Runtime** policy views commit the state version current at materialization where served, so a PDP can tell a fresh-but-obsolete view from current state; the consistency equality test is unchanged.

Bulk management operations already handle their races via membership pinning and per-member outcomes, so they are untouched. All three drafts build clean.

pulls.title: Architecture: the control-plane/data-plane split, stated exactly
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Companion to the handbook's control-plane positioning, in protocol vocabulary. Extends the existing layer paragraph by three sentences: the issuer side is the control plane (desired state: the record, authority, lifecycle, state version; reconciliation: gating, ceiling review, evidence reconciliation; distribution: policy views, status, management), tokens and the PEP/PDP boundary are the data plane, Status and Signals are the channel, and the governed plane is authority, never operations (the harness and orchestrator keep how an agent runs). No new section, no table; the one existing home gains the split, and the MAS profile's control-plane sentences stand unchanged.

pulls.title: README: control-plane sentence and the levels/claims split
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: The two README edits approved by name: (A) one sentence appended to the layer paragraph stating the operational split (the Mission Issuer as control plane, tokens and the PEP/PDP boundary as data plane), mirroring the Architecture's exact statement from #218; (B) the assurance-levels bullet updated to match #215's axis split: the levels are the adoption ladder, and what may be claimed is the orthogonal set of named assurance claims listed in the Deployment Profile. Nothing else touched.

pulls.title: Correctness pass: wire-layer integration debt from the review cycle
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Executes the full-review synthesis' correctness wave (S2 + S11-S14): 25 fixes across 13 drafts, all sentence-and-member scale, no design changes.

**State-version threading:** the status profile's three worked examples now carry the REQUIRED `version` (and the error table gains `conflict`/`stale_version`); `context.mission` gains an OPTIONAL `version` with the PDP staleness comparison against the loaded view (using the existing `stale_state`/`view_inconsistent` vocabulary); parent-suspension projections and lift are committed metadata-only changes (version moves, Signals fire); a committed discharge is version-visible and obsoletes materialized views; management summaries may carry `version` and the dry-run/bulk-token exchange is named the bulk analog of `expected_version`.

**Consistency:** Execution Evidence emission is scoped to the runtime's class rule in the AuthZEN object and checklist; the runtime's conformance tiers join the ESS bullet list, the AuthZEN checklist, and the architecture's assurance claims (new transaction-grade execution claim); the Approval Decision Set and consent evidence's co_approvals cross-reference with a precedence rule, and the core's Single Accountable Approver deferral now points at both hooks.

**Gaps:** the architecture gains the Error Surfaces map (three envelopes, owners, the invalid_request disambiguation) and the IANA-posture sentence; PDP/PEP and harness evidence keys join the core's retired-key rule with the compromise import; erasure is threaded from the PII sinks and the core's privacy section notes the unsalted-anchor disclosure channel; Signals delivery and Join Assertion minting get DoS paragraphs; consent's locale cites BCP 47 with the one-locale-one-hash model; the management filter gains tenant, audited metadata changes, and the MAS mapping-contract subjection.

**Verified:** the policy-view worked example's digest was recomputed and confirmed correct as published (the reviewer's placeholder concern was unfounded); its preamble now acknowledges the state-version member. Zero em-dashes introduced; all 13 drafts build clean.
